In a malignant effusion, a pericardial drain or pericardial window may be needed for ongoing or recurrent drainage.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Immediately after successful drainage of a large effusion, the patient develops pulmonary edema and a rising RV pressure. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Pericardial decompression syndrome
Acute myocardial infarction from a dislodged plaque
Tension pneumothorax from the procedure
Anaphylaxis to the local anesthetic
stages.explanation: Pericardial decompression syndrome is a rare but recognized complication after rapid drainage of a large effusion: sudden unloading can precipitate acute ventricular dysfunction and pulmonary edema, thought to relate to abrupt changes in ventricular geometry and preload/afterload. Acute MI (option B) is possible but not the expected immediate post-drainage picture. Tension pneumothorax (option C) would cause unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, and hyperresonance rather than diffuse pulmonary edema, and should be excluded but is less consistent. Anaphylaxis (option D) presents with urticaria, bronchospasm, and distributive shock, not isolated pulmonary edema with rising RV pressures. Drainage of large effusions in controlled, staged fashion reduces this risk.

cases.diagnosis: Cardiac tamponade from a malignant pericardial effusion
pearls: Beck triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) plus pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg and electrical alternans should trigger immediate evaluation for tamponade.
Bedside echo confirms tamponade: diastolic RV collapse, RA collapse, IVC plethora, and respirophasic inflow variation.
Temporize with cautious IV fluids and avoid positive-pressure ventilation and preload-reducing agents; definitive therapy is pericardiocentesis.
Tamponade is a clinical diagnosis—do not wait for hypotension; early/low-pressure tamponade can occur, especially with malignancy.

cases.title: Racing, Irregular Heartbeat in a Young Man With Palpitations
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of a rapid, irregular pounding heartbeat with lightheadedness.
cases.history: A 26-year-old man presents with 90 minutes of sudden-onset palpitations described as rapid and irregular, accompanied by lightheadedness, mild dyspnea, and chest fluttering. He has had brief, self-terminating palpitations in the past that he never reported. There was no clear trigger today, though he had two energy drinks earlier. He denies syncope, but felt he might pass out. He has no known cardiac disease and takes no medications. He has no allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and denies illicit or stimulant drug use beyond caffeine. Family history is notable for a cousin who died suddenly in his thirties. He has never been told he has an abnormal ECG. He has no fever, cough, or recent illness. He is otherwise healthy and active, running several times per week without exertional symptoms until today. He has had no recent surgery, immobilization, or leg swelling.
cases.exam: Anxious but alert and conversant. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular, very rapid rhythm with variable intensity of S1; no murmurs clearly heard at this rate. Peripheral pulses are rapid, irregular, and of variable volume. Lungs are clear without crackles. No jugular venous distension. Extremities are warm with intact distal perfusion and brisk capillary refill. No focal neurologic deficits. No peripheral edema or calf tenderness. He is mentating normally despite the rapid rate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The monitor shows an irregularly irregular WIDE-complex tachycardia with rates exceeding 250 in places and varying QRS morphology. What is the most likely rhythm?
options: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia
Atrial fibrillation conducting down an accessory pathway (pre-excited AF)
Sinus tachycardia with a bundle branch block
AV nodal reentrant tachycardia
stages.explanation: An irregularly irregular, wide-complex tachycardia with extremely fast rates and beat-to-beat changes in QRS width/morphology is the hallmark of atrial fibrillation conducting antegrade over an accessory pathway (WPW with pre-excited AF). Monomorphic VT (option A) is regular with a uniform QRS. Sinus tachycardia with bundle branch block (option C) is regular and rarely exceeds ~150–160 in an adult at rest, and would not be irregularly irregular. AVNRT (option D) is a regular, typically narrow-complex tachycardia. The irregularity plus wide, varying complexes and a family history of sudden death strongly suggest pre-excited AF.

stages.label: ECG
stages.content:
ECG: irregularly irregular wide-complex tachycardia
Ventricular rate ranging 180–280 bpm
QRS complexes vary in width and morphology beat to beat
No discrete P waves; fibrillatory baseline
Shortest R-R intervals <250 ms
No prior ECG available for comparison

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient remains alert with a systolic BP of 104. Which medication is the MOST appropriate pharmacologic agent?
options: Intravenous diltiazem
Intravenous metoprolol
Intravenous adenosine
Intravenous procainamide
stages.explanation: In pre-excited atrial fibrillation, procainamide is the preferred agent: it slows conduction through the accessory pathway and can chemically cardiovert, without selectively blocking the AV node. AV nodal blockers—diltiazem (option A), metoprolol (option B), and adenosine (option C)—are contraindicated because blocking the AV node preferentially channels atrial impulses down the accessory pathway, accelerating the ventricular rate and risking degeneration into ventricular fibrillation. This is the classic 'avoid the ABCD' teaching (Adenosine, Beta-blockers, Calcium-channel blockers, Digoxin) in WPW with AF. If the patient becomes unstable, synchronized cardioversion is indicated instead.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: During treatment, the patient becomes diaphoretic, hypotensive to 78/50, and confused. What is the immediate next step?
options: Switch to IV amiodarone
Synchronized direct-current cardioversion
Administer IV adenosine to break the rhythm
Start a norepinephrine infusion and continue observation
stages.explanation: Hemodynamic instability (hypotension, altered mentation, signs of shock) in a tachyarrhythmia mandates immediate synchronized DC cardioversion regardless of the underlying rhythm. Switching to amiodarone (option A) is not advised acutely in pre-excited AF, as it can enhance accessory-pathway conduction and is too slow for an unstable patient. Adenosine (option C) is contraindicated in pre-excited AF and will not address instability. Pressors with continued observation (option D) fail to correct the arrhythmia driving the shock and delay definitive therapy. Electricity is the universal answer for the unstable tachycardic patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful cardioversion, the resting ECG shows a short PR interval and a delta wave. What is the recommended definitive management?
options: Lifelong digoxin therapy
Reassurance and discharge with no follow-up
Referral for electrophysiology study and catheter ablation of the accessory pathway
Permanent pacemaker placement
stages.explanation: A short PR interval with a delta wave confirms Wolff-Parkinson-White. After an episode of pre-excited AF—a marker of higher risk for sudden death—the patient should be referred for electrophysiology study and catheter ablation, which is curative by eliminating the accessory pathway. Lifelong digoxin (option A) is contraindicated, as it can shorten the accessory pathway refractory period. Discharge without follow-up (option B) is unsafe given the documented life-threatening arrhythmia and family history. A permanent pacemaker (option D) does not treat an accessory pathway and is unnecessary. Ablation has high success and low complication rates for symptomatic WPW.

cases.diagnosis: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with pre-excited atrial fibrillation
pearls: An irregularly irregular, very rapid wide-complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology is pre-excited atrial fibrillation until proven otherwise.
Avoid AV nodal blockers (Adenosine, Beta-blockers, Calcium-channel blockers, Digoxin) in pre-excited AF—they can accelerate the rate and precipitate VF.
Procainamide is the preferred pharmacologic agent in a stable patient; any instability mandates synchronized cardioversion.
After pre-excited AF, refer for EP study and catheter ablation, which is curative for symptomatic WPW.

cases.title: Recurrent Fainting Spells With a Dangerously Irregular Rhythm
cases.chiefComplaint: Two episodes of sudden collapse with palpitations earlier today.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman is brought in after two witnessed syncopal episodes today, each preceded by a few seconds of palpitations and lightheadedness, with rapid spontaneous recovery. She also reports nausea and being treated for a recent gastrointestinal illness. She was started on ondansetron and a course of azithromycin for a suspected respiratory infection by her primary doctor, and she takes citalopram 40 mg daily for depression and furosemide 40 mg daily for hypertension. She uses an over-the-counter potassium-wasting laxative for chronic constipation. She has no allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks rarely, and lives alone. Family history is unremarkable, with no known sudden cardiac death. She denies chest pain, fever today, focal weakness, or seizure activity; witnesses report no tonic-clonic movements and no prolonged postictal confusion. She has had poor oral intake and several days of vomiting and diarrhea. She has no history of structural heart disease or prior arrhythmia, and her last ECG years ago was reportedly normal.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented, currently asymptomatic between episodes. Cardiac rhythm is regular and bradycardic at rest with normal S1 and S2; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs clear. No jugular venous distension. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal strength, sensation, gait, and no tongue laceration. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced, consistent with volume depletion. Extremities warm without edema. No carotid bruits. Abdomen soft and nontender with normoactive bowel sounds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given syncope with palpitations and her medication list, what is the most important initial test and intervention?
options: Head CT and neurology consultation for seizure
Continuous cardiac monitoring with a 12-lead ECG and electrolytes
Orthostatic vital signs alone and discharge if normal
Carotid duplex ultrasound for embolic source
stages.explanation: Syncope preceded by palpitations in a patient on multiple QT-prolonging drugs demands continuous cardiac monitoring, a 12-lead ECG, and electrolyte measurement to capture a dysrhythmia and identify reversible causes. Head CT for seizure (option A) is low-yield given no tonic-clonic activity, no postictal state, and a cardiac-sounding prodrome. Orthostatics and discharge (option C) would dangerously miss a malignant arrhythmia. Carotid duplex (option D) evaluates focal ischemic stroke, which does not cause transient global syncope and is not suggested here. The clinical pattern points to a primary arrhythmic cause requiring telemetry.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
ECG (between episodes): sinus bradycardia at 56 bpm, QTc 580 ms
Telemetry capture during an episode: polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with a twisting, sinusoidal QRS axis
K+: 2.9 mEq/L
Mg2+: 1.2 mg/dL
Ca2+: 8.4 mg/dL
Troponin I: negative
TSH: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The telemetry strip and prolonged QTc are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia
Torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with QT prolongation)
Atrial flutter with variable block
Ventricular fibrillation
stages.explanation: Polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with a characteristic twisting of the QRS around the baseline, occurring in the setting of a prolonged QTc, defines torsades de pointes. Monomorphic VT (option A) has a uniform QRS morphology and is usually scar-related, not QT-related. Atrial flutter (option C) is a regular, narrow-complex atrial tachyarrhythmia with sawtooth waves, not a wide twisting rhythm. Ventricular fibrillation (option D) is a chaotic, disorganized rhythm without organized QRS complexes and causes immediate pulselessness, whereas torsades is self-terminating in this case and tied to QT prolongation. Her low potassium and magnesium plus QT-prolonging drugs are the perfect substrate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has another run of the arrhythmia but maintains a pulse. What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment?
options: Intravenous magnesium sulfate
Intravenous amiodarone
Intravenous adenosine
Oral metoprolol
stages.explanation: Intravenous magnesium sulfate is first-line for torsades de pointes—even when serum magnesium is normal—because it stabilizes the myocardium and suppresses the early afterdepolarizations driving the arrhythmia. Amiodarone (option B) prolongs the QT interval and can worsen torsades, so it is avoided. Adenosine (option C) treats AV-nodal-dependent supraventricular tachycardias and has no role in torsades. Oral metoprolol (option D) is too slow and is not the acute treatment; while beta-blockade is used in congenital long QT, the acute ED priority is magnesium, correction of electrolytes, and removal of offending drugs. Overdrive pacing or isoproterenol can be used for refractory, bradycardia-dependent torsades.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: Beyond magnesium, which combination of measures best addresses the underlying cause and prevents recurrence?
options: Continue azithromycin and citalopram; add an antiemetic
Replete potassium and magnesium, stop all QT-prolonging drugs, and increase heart rate if bradycardia-dependent
Start digoxin and discharge home
Administer flecainide to suppress ventricular ectopy
stages.explanation: Definitive management targets the substrate: aggressively replete potassium and magnesium to the high-normal range, discontinue all QT-prolonging agents (azithromycin, ondansetron, citalopram), and, if torsades is pause/bradycardia-dependent, increase the heart rate with overdrive pacing or isoproterenol to shorten the QT. Continuing the offending drugs (option A) perpetuates the arrhythmia. Digoxin with discharge (option C) is inappropriate and unsafe. Flecainide (option D) is a sodium-channel blocker that can be proarrhythmic and is not indicated for acquired long-QT torsades. Correcting electrolytes and removing culprit medications is the cornerstone of prevention.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Before adding any new medication for this patient in the future, which step is most important to prevent recurrence?
options: Obtain a baseline ECG to review the QTc and screen for QT-prolonging drug interactions
Order genetic testing on every patient
Restrict all dietary potassium
Avoid all beta-blockers permanently
stages.explanation: Reviewing a baseline ECG for the QTc and screening any new prescription for QT-prolonging effects and drug-drug interactions is the most practical, high-yield prevention step in acquired long-QT torsades. Routine genetic testing on every patient (option B) is reserved for suspected congenital long-QT, not all patients. Restricting dietary potassium (option C) is exactly wrong—hypokalemia prolongs the QT and provoked her event. Permanently avoiding all beta-blockers (option D) is incorrect; beta-blockers are actually protective in congenital long-QT syndromes. Medication reconciliation against the QTc is the key safeguard.

cases.diagnosis: Torsades de pointes from drug-induced and electrolyte-related QT prolongation
pearls: Torsades is polymorphic VT with a twisting QRS axis in the setting of a prolonged QT; look for culprit drugs and low K+/Mg2+.
IV magnesium is first-line even when the serum magnesium level is normal.
Avoid QT-prolonging antiarrhythmics like amiodarone; for pause-dependent torsades use overdrive pacing or isoproterenol.
Replete K+ and Mg2+ to high-normal, stop all QT-prolonging drugs, and reconcile future medications against the QTc.

cases.title: Elderly Man Who Keeps Passing Out With a Slow Pulse
cases.chiefComplaint: Several fainting episodes over two days with persistent fatigue and a slow heartbeat.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man presents after three syncopal episodes over the past 2 days, each occurring without warning and followed by quick recovery. Between events he feels fatigued, dizzy, and short of breath with minimal exertion. He denies chest pain today but reports it has been hard to keep up with daily activities. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a prior anterior myocardial infarction 6 years ago. Medications are metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no known allergies. He is a retired carpenter, former smoker (30 pack-years), and drinks one beer occasionally. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. He has had no fever, recent illness, or medication changes. He has not fallen with head trauma during the episodes. He has noticed his pulse feels 'very slow' when he checks it at home over the past day.
cases.exam: Alert but appears fatigued and pale. Cardiac exam reveals a slow, regular rhythm with intermittent variation in the intensity of S1 and occasional prominent 'cannon' A waves visible in the neck veins. No murmurs or gallops. Lungs clear bilaterally. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam nonfocal; no tongue laceration or post-ictal confusion. Extremities warm with intact distal pulses but mildly delayed capillary refill. Abdomen soft. He is mildly hypotensive and bradycardic but mentating appropriately at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step given his bradycardia and syncope?
options: 12-lead ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring
CT head without contrast
Tilt-table testing
Echocardiogram before any other testing
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG with continuous monitoring is the immediate priority to characterize the bradyarrhythmia, identify AV block, and guide urgent management. CT head (option B) is low-yield without head trauma or focal deficits and does not explain bradycardic syncope. Tilt-table testing (option C) evaluates suspected vasovagal/neurocardiogenic syncope and is inappropriate and unsafe in the acute setting with profound bradycardia. An echocardiogram (option D) is useful later to assess structural disease but does not establish the rhythm diagnosis or address acute risk. The cannon A waves and variable S1 already hint at AV dissociation, which the ECG will confirm.

stages.label: ECG
stages.content:
ECG: atrial rate ~88 bpm, ventricular rate ~38 bpm
P waves and QRS complexes are completely dissociated (no fixed relationship)
Regular P-P and regular R-R intervals, but independent of each other
Wide QRS escape rhythm
Old anterior Q waves consistent with prior infarct
Troponin pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ECG shows atrial and ventricular activity that are completely independent. What is the diagnosis?
options: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block
First-degree AV block
Third-degree (complete) AV block
Sinus bradycardia
stages.explanation: Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS complexes, each marching out at its own regular rate with no relationship, defines third-degree (complete) heart block, with a slow ventricular escape rhythm maintaining output. Mobitz I (option A) shows progressive PR prolongation until a dropped beat, with conducted beats preserved. First-degree block (option B) is simply a prolonged but constant PR interval with 1:1 conduction. Sinus bradycardia (option D) maintains normal P-QRS association with a constant PR interval. The cannon A waves on exam result from atrial contraction against a closed tricuspid valve, a classic sign of AV dissociation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He becomes increasingly lightheaded with a BP of 84/50 and remains profoundly bradycardic. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Hold all home medications and observe on telemetry
Transcutaneous pacing as a bridge, with preparation for transvenous pacing
Administer a fluid bolus and recheck in one hour
Give IV metoprolol to stabilize the rhythm
stages.explanation: Symptomatic, hemodynamically significant complete heart block requires immediate transcutaneous pacing as a bridge while preparing for transvenous pacing and ultimately a permanent pacemaker. Simply observing on telemetry (option A) is unsafe given symptomatic hypotension and high risk of asystole. A fluid bolus and delayed recheck (option C) does not address the electrical conduction failure causing the shock. IV metoprolol (option D) is a beta-blocker that would further suppress the escape rhythm and is dangerous. Atropine is often ineffective in infranodal/wide-complex complete block, so pacing is the definitive temporizing measure here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization with pacing, what is the definitive long-term treatment for his complete heart block?
options: Permanent pacemaker implantation
Lifelong atropine therapy
Coronary artery bypass grafting
Initiation of an antiarrhythmic such as flecainide
stages.explanation: Permanent pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment for symptomatic acquired complete heart block, restoring reliable AV synchrony and preventing recurrent syncope and sudden death. Lifelong atropine (option B) is not a viable chronic therapy and is often ineffective in infranodal block. CABG (option C) addresses coronary ischemia but does not reliably correct established conduction-system disease, which here is from chronic degeneration and prior infarction. Flecainide (option D) is a sodium-channel blocker that slows conduction and would worsen the block. Reversible contributors (ischemia, drugs, electrolytes) should be excluded, but degenerative complete block in an elderly patient typically requires permanent pacing.

cases.diagnosis: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block
pearls: Complete heart block shows AV dissociation: regular P waves and regular QRS complexes marching independently, with a slow escape rhythm.
Cannon A waves and variable S1 intensity are bedside clues to AV dissociation.
Symptomatic/unstable complete block needs transcutaneous pacing as a bridge; atropine is often ineffective in wide-complex infranodal block.
Definitive treatment is a permanent pacemaker after reversible causes (ischemia, AV-nodal-blocking drugs, electrolytes) are excluded.

cases.title: Sudden Pounding Heartbeat in an Otherwise Healthy Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt onset of a fast, regular pounding heartbeat with mild chest fluttering.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset palpitations that began abruptly 40 minutes ago while she was sitting at her desk. She describes a fast, regular pounding in her chest with mild lightheadedness and a fluttering sensation in her throat. She has had a few similar self-limited episodes over the past year that resolved spontaneously within minutes. She denies chest pain, syncope, or significant dyspnea. She drinks two to three coffees daily and had an extra cup this morning. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only a combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no allergies. She is a nonsmoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and exercises regularly. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death or known arrhythmias. She has no fever, recent illness, or weight change suggestive of hyperthyroidism. She reports no leg swelling, recent travel, or immobilization. She has never been hospitalized and has no prior cardiac workup.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly anxious, in no respiratory distress. Cardiac exam reveals a rapid, regular tachycardia with normal S1 and S2 and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses are rapid and regular with good volume. Lungs are clear bilaterally. There is no jugular venous distension. Neck is supple without thyromegaly. Extremities are warm and well perfused with brisk capillary refill and no edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. She is mentating normally and remains hemodynamically stable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The rhythm strip shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 188 bpm without visible P waves. In this stable patient, what should be tried first?
options: Immediate synchronized cardioversion
Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva or carotid sinus massage)
Intravenous amiodarone bolus
Intravenous procainamide
stages.explanation: In a stable patient with regular narrow-complex SVT, vagal maneuvers—particularly the modified Valsalva or carotid sinus massage (after auscultating for bruits)—are the appropriate first step, increasing vagal tone to slow AV-nodal conduction and often terminating the arrhythmia. Synchronized cardioversion (option A) is reserved for unstable patients, not a stable, comfortable one. Amiodarone (option C) is not first-line for typical stable SVT and carries more adverse effects. Procainamide (option D) is used for pre-excited or wide-complex tachyarrhythmias, not routine narrow-complex SVT. Starting with the least invasive, safest maneuver is the standard approach.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Vagal maneuvers fail to convert the rhythm and she remains stable. What is the next pharmacologic step?
options: Intravenous adenosine (6 mg rapid push, then 12 mg if needed)
Intravenous digoxin loading
Oral flecainide
Intravenous magnesium sulfate
stages.explanation: Adenosine, given as a rapid IV push (6 mg, then 12 mg if needed) followed by a saline flush, is the next step for stable SVT refractory to vagal maneuvers; it transiently blocks the AV node and terminates AV-nodal-dependent reentrant tachycardias. Digoxin (option B) has a slow onset and is not the acute drug of choice. Flecainide (option C) is used for chronic rhythm control in select patients, not acute termination in the ED. Magnesium (option D) is the treatment for torsades, not typical SVT. Warn the patient that adenosine causes a brief, uncomfortable sensation of chest pressure and impending doom that resolves within seconds.

stages.label: POST-CONVERSION ECG
stages.content:
After 12 mg adenosine: abrupt conversion to sinus rhythm
Sinus rhythm at 78 bpm
Normal PR interval, no delta wave
No ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities
QTc normal
Electrolytes within normal limits; TSH normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the abrupt onset/offset, regular narrow-complex tachycardia, and termination with AV-nodal blockade, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial fibrillation
Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (e.g., AVNRT)
Multifocal atrial tachycardia
Ventricular tachycardia
stages.explanation: A regular, narrow-complex tachycardia with abrupt onset and offset that terminates with vagal maneuvers or adenosine is classic for paroxysmal SVT, most commonly AV-nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT). Atrial fibrillation (option A) is irregularly irregular and would not terminate so cleanly with AV-nodal blockade. Multifocal atrial tachycardia (option C) is an irregular rhythm with multiple distinct P-wave morphologies, typically in patients with severe pulmonary disease. Ventricular tachycardia (option D) is a wide-complex rhythm. The clean conversion to sinus rhythm with adenosine strongly supports an AV-node-dependent reentrant SVT.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She is now asymptomatic in sinus rhythm. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling?
options: Admit for urgent catheter ablation tonight
Discharge with reassurance, advice to reduce caffeine, instruction in vagal maneuvers, and outpatient cardiology follow-up
Start lifelong amiodarone before discharge
Implant a permanent pacemaker
stages.explanation: After successful conversion of a first or infrequent symptomatic SVT in a stable patient with a normal post-conversion ECG, discharge with reassurance, trigger avoidance (reducing caffeine), instruction in self-administered vagal maneuvers, and outpatient cardiology follow-up is appropriate. Urgent same-night ablation (option A) is unnecessary; ablation is elective and considered for recurrent or poorly tolerated episodes. Lifelong amiodarone (option C) is excessive and toxic for a young patient with occasional SVT. A pacemaker (option D) treats bradyarrhythmias, not SVT. Catheter ablation can be offered electively if episodes become frequent or bothersome.

cases.diagnosis: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (AVNRT)
pearls: Stable regular narrow-complex SVT: start with vagal maneuvers (modified Valsalva, carotid massage), then adenosine.
Give adenosine as a rapid push with immediate saline flush; warn the patient about transient flushing, chest pressure, and a sense of doom.
Any hemodynamic instability changes the plan to immediate synchronized cardioversion.
Recurrent or poorly tolerated SVT can be cured with elective catheter ablation; counsel on caffeine and other triggers.

cases.title: Fevers, Chills, and a New Heart Sound in a Man Who Injects Drugs
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of fevers, chills, night sweats, and worsening fatigue.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with 1 week of intermittent fevers to 39 °C, drenching night sweats, rigors, malaise, and progressive fatigue. He also reports mild dyspnea on exertion and a few painful spots on his fingers. He has a long history of intravenous heroin use and admits to recent injections with shared equipment. He denies cough, dysuria, or focal pain but notes pleuritic chest discomfort. Past medical history includes hepatitis C and a prior skin abscess. He takes no regular medications and has no known allergies. He is unhoused, smokes one pack of cigarettes daily, and drinks alcohol heavily. Family history is noncontributory. He has not traveled recently and has had no dental procedures. He has had subjective weight loss over the past two weeks. He has no prior known cardiac murmur or valve disease. He reports the finger lesions are tender and appeared over the last few days, and he has noticed some dark streaks under his nails.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing and diaphoretic. Cardiac exam reveals a new holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration. Lungs have scattered crackles bilaterally. Skin shows several tender, raised violaceous nodules on the finger pads, small nontender erythematous macules on the palms, and splinter hemorrhages under several nails. There are track marks over both antecubital fossae. No conjunctival lesions noted. Mild splenomegaly on abdominal exam. No focal neurologic deficits. Extremities warm with intact pulses; no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the single most important initial diagnostic step before starting antibiotics in this patient?
options: Obtain at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites
Order a single set of blood cultures and start antibiotics immediately
Obtain a transesophageal echocardiogram first
Send a urinalysis and discharge with oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: In suspected infective endocarditis, drawing at least three sets of blood cultures from separate venipuncture sites before antibiotics maximizes the yield for the causative organism—a major Duke criterion—and guides targeted therapy. A single culture before antibiotics (option B) reduces sensitivity and the ability to demonstrate persistent bacteremia. Starting with TEE (option C) is valuable for vegetations but should not delay the culture draw, and TTE is typically obtained first; cultures take priority before antibiotics. Discharging on oral antibiotics with only a urinalysis (option D) is dangerous and inappropriate for a febrile IV drug user with a new murmur and embolic stigmata. Cultures first, then prompt empiric therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC: 17.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL
ESR: 78 mm/hr; CRP: 142 mg/L
Blood cultures (3/3 sets): Staphylococcus aureus (methicillin-resistant)
Urinalysis: microscopic hematuria
Chest X-ray: multiple bilateral peripheral nodular opacities, some cavitating (septic emboli)
Transthoracic echo: 1.2 cm vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate regurgitation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the modified Duke criteria, which combination establishes a diagnosis of definite infective endocarditis here?
options: Fever alone plus a single positive blood culture
Two major criteria: persistently positive blood cultures with a typical organism AND echocardiographic vegetation
Two minor criteria only: fever and IV drug use
One major plus one minor: a vegetation plus splinter hemorrhages
stages.explanation: Definite IE by the modified Duke criteria can be met with two major criteria. Here, persistently positive cultures growing a typical organism (S. aureus) across multiple sets constitute one major criterion, and the echocardiographic vegetation with new regurgitation constitutes the second—satisfying definite IE. Fever plus one positive culture (option A) is insufficient. Two minor criteria alone (option C: fever and a predisposing condition) meet at most 'possible' IE, not definite. One major plus one minor (option D) requires additional minor criteria to reach definite by that pathway. This patient also has multiple supporting minor criteria (fever, predisposition/IVDU, vascular and immunologic phenomena).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Pending sensitivities, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy for this IV drug user with suspected staphylococcal endocarditis?
options: Oral amoxicillin
Vancomycin (empiric MRSA coverage)
Ceftriaxone monotherapy
Azithromycin
stages.explanation: In an IV drug user with suspected endocarditis, empiric coverage must include MRSA, so vancomycin is the appropriate empiric choice while awaiting susceptibilities; therapy is later tailored (e.g., to nafcillin/cefazolin if methicillin-susceptible). Oral amoxicillin (option A) is inadequate for serious staphylococcal bacteremia and the wrong route for endocarditis. Ceftriaxone monotherapy (option C) does not reliably cover MRSA and is insufficient as sole empiric therapy here. Azithromycin (option D) is a macrolide with no role in treating staphylococcal endocarditis. Endocarditis requires prolonged IV bactericidal therapy (typically several weeks) guided by organism and valve involvement.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On day 3, he develops sudden right-sided weakness and aphasia. What complication is most likely, and what is the best next step?
options: Septic embolic stroke; obtain urgent neuroimaging and involve the stroke/endocarditis team
Simple migraine; give an analgesic and reassure
Hypoglycemia; give oral glucose and recheck later
Medication side effect; stop all antibiotics immediately
stages.explanation: New focal neurologic deficits in endocarditis most often reflect a septic embolus from a vegetation causing ischemic stroke (or a mycotic aneurysm with hemorrhage); urgent neuroimaging and multidisciplinary involvement (neurology, infectious disease, cardiac surgery) are essential, and thrombolysis is generally avoided due to hemorrhagic risk from mycotic aneurysms. A migraine (option B) does not explain acute aphasia with weakness in a septic patient. Hypoglycemia (option C) causes deficits but is quickly excluded with a glucose check and is unlikely the primary cause here. Stopping all antibiotics (option D) is dangerous and would worsen the underlying infection. Embolic complications can be an indication for early valve surgery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly support a recommendation for early surgical valve intervention?
options: Isolated splinter hemorrhages
Heart failure from severe valvular regurgitation, persistent bacteremia despite appropriate antibiotics, or recurrent emboli with a large mobile vegetation
A single positive blood culture
Fever resolving within 48 hours of antibiotics
stages.explanation: Indications for early surgery in IE include heart failure from severe valvular dysfunction, uncontrolled infection (persistent bacteremia or abscess despite appropriate antibiotics), and prevention of embolism with large (>10 mm) mobile vegetations or recurrent emboli—exactly the high-risk features in option B. Isolated splinter hemorrhages (option A) are a peripheral stigma but not a surgical indication. A single positive culture (option C) is a diagnostic data point, not an operative trigger. Defervescence within 48 hours (option D) actually indicates a favorable response to medical therapy, arguing against urgent surgery. Surgical timing is individualized with a multidisciplinary endocarditis team.

cases.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis (tricuspid valve, MRSA) in an IV drug user
pearls: Draw at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics in suspected endocarditis—it is a major Duke criterion.
Right-sided (tricuspid) endocarditis in IV drug users classically causes septic pulmonary emboli with cavitary lung nodules.
Empirically cover MRSA with vancomycin in IV drug users, then narrow based on susceptibilities; treatment is prolonged IV therapy.
Surgical indications include heart failure from valve destruction, uncontrolled infection, and recurrent emboli with large mobile vegetations.

cases.title: Headache and Confusion Days After a Fall in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening headache, drowsiness, and confusion several days after a fall.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man is brought in by his wife for 3 days of progressively worsening headache, drowsiness, and intermittent confusion. He fell and struck his head on a tiled bathroom floor about 10 days ago while on a mountain hiking trip; at the time he had only a brief headache and no loss of consciousness, which he attributed to altitude. Over the past several days he has become more lethargic, slow to answer questions, and unsteady on his feet, with one episode of vomiting. He has atrial fibrillation and is on warfarin, with a recent INR he believes was 'a little high.' Other history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily. He has no allergies. He drinks alcohol regularly and does not use illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory. His wife notes mild word-finding difficulty and new clumsiness of the right hand today. He has had no fever, neck stiffness, or seizures. He recently returned from high altitude and initially blamed his symptoms on the trip.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable, oriented to person and place but not date. Pupils equal and reactive. There is mild right pronator drift and subtle right-hand clumsiness; reflexes slightly brisker on the right. Speech shows mild word-finding hesitancy. Gait is unsteady and wide-based. No neck rigidity. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs. Lungs clear. No external scalp laceration but mild tenderness over the left parietal area. No papilledema appreciated on fundoscopy. Extremities without edema; pulses intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the head injury, anticoagulation, and evolving neurologic signs, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Reassurance that symptoms are from recent high-altitude exposure
Non-contrast CT of the head
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis
Outpatient MRI in one week
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the immediate test of choice for an anticoagulated patient with head trauma and new neurologic deficits, rapidly identifying intracranial hemorrhage including subdural hematoma. Attributing symptoms to altitude (option A) is dangerous in an anticoagulated patient with focal findings and a clear head-injury history. Lumbar puncture (option C) is contraindicated before imaging when a space-occupying lesion or raised ICP is suspected and would not be the initial step here. Outpatient MRI in a week (option D) unacceptably delays diagnosis of a potentially life-threatening bleed. Anticoagulation plus head trauma plus new focal deficits is an emergency until proven otherwise.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: crescent-shaped hyperdense/mixed-density extra-axial collection over the left cerebral convexity
Collection crosses suture lines; ~12 mm thick with 6 mm midline shift
Effacement of the left lateral ventricle; no intraventricular blood
INR: 3.8
Platelets: 210 ×10³/µL
Glucose: 104 mg/dL
Electrolytes within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The crescent-shaped collection crossing suture lines with mixed density is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Epidural hematoma
Subdural hematoma
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: A crescent (concave) shaped extra-axial collection that crosses suture lines is the classic appearance of a subdural hematoma, arising from torn bridging veins; the mixed density indicates a subacute-to-chronic bleed with possible acute extension—fitting the delayed presentation in an anticoagulated patient. An epidural hematoma (option A) is biconvex (lens-shaped) and typically does NOT cross suture lines, usually from arterial (middle meningeal) injury. Subarachnoid hemorrhage (option C) shows blood in the sulci, cisterns, and fissures, not a crescentic convexity collection. Ischemic stroke (option D) is hypodense and intra-axial, not a hyperdense extra-axial collection. The shape and suture-crossing distinguish subdural from epidural.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging neurosurgical consultation, what is the most important immediate medical intervention given his INR of 3.8?
options: Continue warfarin and observe
Reverse anticoagulation with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate and IV vitamin K
Administer aspirin to prevent further clot
Start a heparin infusion to treat his atrial fibrillation
stages.explanation: In warfarin-associated intracranial hemorrhage, urgent reversal with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate plus IV vitamin K rapidly corrects the coagulopathy and limits hematoma expansion—the single most important medical step alongside neurosurgical involvement. Continuing warfarin and observing (option A) allows ongoing bleeding and deterioration. Aspirin (option C) is an antiplatelet agent that would worsen, not prevent, intracranial bleeding. A heparin infusion (option D) for AF would be catastrophic in the setting of active intracranial hemorrhage. PCC is preferred over fresh frozen plasma for speed and volume; vitamin K sustains the reversal.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After reversal, the patient becomes more somnolent with worsening right-sided weakness and his midline shift increases. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Continued observation with serial neuro checks only
Surgical evacuation of the hematoma (e.g., craniotomy or burr-hole drainage)
Mannitol infusion as the sole definitive therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation once stable
stages.explanation: A symptomatic subdural hematoma with significant midline shift and clinical deterioration requires surgical evacuation—craniotomy for acute clot or burr-hole drainage for more liquefied subacute/chronic components—to relieve mass effect and prevent herniation. Observation alone (option A) is inappropriate once there is neurologic decline and progressive shift. Mannitol (option C) is a useful temporizing measure to reduce ICP but is not definitive and does not remove the clot. Resuming therapeutic anticoagulation (option D) is contraindicated in the acute phase of an expanding intracranial bleed. Surgical decompression is the definitive treatment for a deteriorating patient with significant mass effect.

cases.diagnosis: Delayed subdural hematoma in an anticoagulated patient after head trauma
pearls: Anticoagulation plus head trauma plus new neurologic signs is an emergency—get an immediate non-contrast head CT.
Subdural hematoma is crescent-shaped and crosses suture lines (torn bridging veins); epidural is lens-shaped and does not cross sutures.
Reverse warfarin-associated intracranial hemorrhage urgently with 4-factor PCC plus IV vitamin K to limit hematoma expansion.
Don't anchor on benign explanations (e.g., altitude illness) when red-flag features and a trauma history are present; image first.

cases.title: Lower Abdominal Pain and Inability to Pass Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe lower abdominal pain and inability to urinate for the past 10 hours.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with 10 hours of progressively severe lower abdominal pain and a complete inability to pass urine despite a strong, constant urge. For several months he has had worsening urinary hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia, and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. Two days ago he started an over-the-counter cold medication containing a decongestant and an antihistamine for nasal congestion. Past medical history includes benign prostatic hyperplasia, hypertension, and osteoarthritis. Medications include amlodipine 5 mg daily and acetaminophen as needed; he is not on any alpha-blocker. He has no allergies. He is a retired accountant, former light smoker, and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is notable for prostate issues in his brother. He denies fever, chills, gross hematuria, or flank pain. He has had no recent instrumentation or catheterization. He describes the suprapubic pain as constant and worsening, with relief impossible despite repeated attempts to void. He has not had similar complete retention before, only the chronic obstructive symptoms.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, restless, frequently shifting position. Abdomen reveals a tender, distended, palpable suprapubic mass that is dull to percussion, consistent with a full bladder; the rest of the abdomen is soft and nontender with no rebound or guarding. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged, non-tender prostate without nodules; normal sphincter tone. External genitalia are normal with no phimosis or meatal stenosis. Lower extremity neurologic exam is normal with intact perineal sensation. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention to relieve this patient's symptoms?
options: Immediate urethral (Foley) catheterization to decompress the bladder
Renal ultrasound before any intervention
Intravenous furosemide to increase urine output
Urgent prostate biopsy
stages.explanation: Acute urinary retention is relieved by immediate bladder decompression with urethral catheterization, which provides rapid symptom relief and prevents ongoing bladder and upper-tract injury. Renal ultrasound first (option B) delays needed relief; imaging can follow if there is concern for hydronephrosis or if catheterization is difficult. IV furosemide (option C) would worsen the situation by producing more urine into an already obstructed, distended bladder. Prostate biopsy (option D) is never an emergency intervention and has no role in acute relief. Drain the bladder first; the clinical diagnosis of obstructive retention is already evident from the distended, percussion-dull suprapubic bladder.

stages.label: POST-CATHETERIZATION DATA
stages.content:
Immediate catheter drainage: 1100 mL of clear yellow urine
Urinalysis: no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, no significant blood
Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN: 26 mg/dL
PSA: deferred (acute retention falsely elevates PSA)
Bladder scan post-drainage: minimal residual volume

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What most likely precipitated this episode of acute urinary retention in a man with known BPH?
options: A urinary tract infection
The over-the-counter decongestant/antihistamine cold medication
His amlodipine
Dehydration from reduced fluid intake
stages.explanation: Sympathomimetic decongestants increase prostatic and bladder-neck smooth-muscle tone (alpha-adrenergic effect), and antihistamines have anticholinergic effects that impair detrusor contraction—both can precipitate acute retention in a man with underlying BPH, exactly the recent exposure here. A UTI (option A) is unlikely given a bland urinalysis without pyuria, nitrites, or symptoms of infection. Amlodipine (option C), a calcium-channel blocker, is not a typical precipitant of retention. Dehydration (option D) reduces urine production but does not by itself cause obstructive retention. Recognizing culprit medications is key, and the patient should avoid these agents in the future.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: To improve the likelihood of a successful voiding trial after catheter removal, which medication should be started?
options: An anticholinergic such as oxybutynin
An alpha-1 blocker such as tamsulosin
A loop diuretic
An additional decongestant
stages.explanation: Starting an alpha-1 adrenergic blocker such as tamsulosin relaxes prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle and significantly improves the success of a subsequent trial of void after acute retention from BPH. An anticholinergic like oxybutynin (option A) would worsen retention by further impairing detrusor contraction. A loop diuretic (option C) increases urine volume without relieving outlet obstruction and is not indicated. Adding a decongestant (option D) would aggravate the alpha-mediated obstruction that helped cause the retention. Alpha-blockers are the standard adjunct before a voiding trial, with a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor considered for longer-term prostate volume reduction.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next several hours after decompression, his urine output rises to 250 mL/hr. What complication should you monitor for and manage?
options: Post-obstructive diuresis with potential volume depletion and electrolyte derangement
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
Acute glomerulonephritis
Diabetes insipidus from pituitary injury
stages.explanation: After relief of prolonged obstruction, post-obstructive diuresis can occur—a brisk diuresis from accumulated solute, retained urea, and transient tubular dysfunction—risking volume depletion and electrolyte abnormalities (sodium, potassium, magnesium). Management includes monitoring urine output, vital signs, and electrolytes, and replacing fluids judiciously (often replacing a portion of urine output) when diuresis is pathologic. SIADH (option B) causes water retention and hyponatremia, not a high-output diuresis after obstruction. Acute glomerulonephritis (option C) presents with hematuria, hypertension, and active urinary sediment, not this scenario. Central diabetes insipidus (option D) involves pituitary/hypothalamic pathology and is unrelated to bladder decompression. Most post-obstructive diuresis is self-limited but requires vigilance.

cases.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention from benign prostatic hyperplasia (medication-precipitated)
pearls: Acute urinary retention is a clinical diagnosis—relieve it immediately with bladder catheterization; a palpable, percussion-dull suprapubic bladder confirms it.
Decongestants (alpha-agonists) and antihistamines/anticholinergics commonly precipitate retention in men with BPH—review the medication list.
Start an alpha-1 blocker (e.g., tamsulosin) to improve the success of a subsequent trial of void.
Watch for post-obstructive diuresis after decompressing a chronically obstructed bladder; monitor urine output and electrolytes and replace fluids judiciously.

cases.title: Rapidly Worsening Perineal Pain and Swelling in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe perineal and scrotal pain with swelling and skin discoloration worsening over a day.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man presents with 24 hours of rapidly worsening perineal and scrotal pain, swelling, and skin discoloration, with pain that he describes as far out of proportion to the appearance of the area earlier. He reports fevers, chills, and progressive malaise. He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus with a recent hemoglobin A1c of 11%, hypertension, and obesity. He had a perianal abscess drained two weeks ago. Medications include metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glipizide, and lisinopril. He has no allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. He reports the pain initially seemed minor but escalated dramatically overnight, and he now feels systemically unwell with nausea. He denies trauma but notes a foul-smelling discharge and that the skin 'feels like it is crackling.' He has not been able to eat today and feels lightheaded when standing. He has had no prior episodes like this.
cases.exam: Toxic-appearing, diaphoretic, and in significant distress. The perineum and scrotum show dusky, erythematous-to-violaceous skin with areas of skin necrosis, tense edema extending toward the lower abdominal wall, and palpable subcutaneous crepitus. There is foul-smelling, serosanguinous discharge and skin tenderness that extends beyond the visible margins of erythema, with patchy anesthesia over the affected skin. Pain is markedly out of proportion to exam findings. Abdomen soft but with lower-wall tenderness. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. Distal pulses intact; no focal neurologic deficits. Capillary refill is delayed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most concerning for a necrotizing soft-tissue infection rather than simple cellulitis?
options: Mild erythema with well-defined borders and no systemic symptoms
Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, skin necrosis, anesthesia, and systemic toxicity
Localized warmth that resolves with elevation
An itchy, raised, sharply demarcated plaque
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion to physical findings, subcutaneous crepitus (gas), skin necrosis, anesthesia of the overlying skin (from destruction of cutaneous nerves), and systemic toxicity are the hallmark red flags of necrotizing soft-tissue infection. Mild erythema with well-defined borders and no systemic symptoms (option A) describes uncomplicated cellulitis. Localized warmth that resolves with elevation (option C) suggests benign or dependent edema, not necrotizing infection. An itchy, sharply demarcated plaque (option D) describes erysipelas or a dermatitis, not a deep necrotizing process. The combination here, in a poorly controlled diabetic with rapid progression, is alarming for Fournier gangrene.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC: 22.4 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Na: 128 mEq/L
Glucose: 412 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.1)
CRP: 320 mg/L
Lactate: 4.2 mmol/L
Plain film/CT of pelvis: subcutaneous gas tracking through the perineal soft tissues and scrotum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These findings—gas in the perineal soft tissues, profound systemic toxicity, and rapidly spreading necrosis in a diabetic—are diagnostic of which condition?
options: Epididymo-orchitis
Fournier gangrene (necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum/genitalia)
Inguinal hernia incarceration
Simple scrotal abscess
stages.explanation: Fournier gangrene is a necrotizing fasciitis of the perineal, genital, and perianal regions—typically polymicrobial—characterized by rapidly progressive necrosis, subcutaneous gas, and sepsis, most common in diabetics and immunocompromised hosts, exactly as seen here. Epididymo-orchitis (option A) causes scrotal pain and swelling but not gas-forming necrosis with systemic toxicity and skin necrosis. Incarcerated inguinal hernia (option C) presents with a tender groin bulge and bowel obstruction signs, not gas in the soft tissues with fasciitis. A simple scrotal abscess (option D) is localized and fluctuant without the diffuse necrosis, crepitus, and septic shock seen here. The CT gas plus clinical picture clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important definitive intervention for this patient?
options: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics alone
Emergent surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy first, then reassess
Bedside incision and drainage in the ED
stages.explanation: Emergent, aggressive surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue is the single most important, life-saving intervention in Fournier gangrene/necrotizing fasciitis; delay markedly increases mortality. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (option A) are essential adjuncts but are insufficient alone because the infection spreads along poorly perfused fascial planes that antibiotics cannot penetrate adequately. Hyperbaric oxygen (option C) may be an adjunct at some centers but must never delay surgery and is not the primary treatment. Bedside incision and drainage (option D) is inadequate for the extensive fascial debridement required in the operating room. Source control in the OR, plus antibiotics and resuscitation, is the cornerstone of management.

stages.label: RESUSCITATION
stages.question: Alongside arranging surgery, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic strategy?
options: A single narrow-spectrum agent such as oral cephalexin
Broad-spectrum coverage including gram-positives, gram-negatives, anaerobes, plus an agent for toxin suppression (e.g., clindamycin or linezolid)
Antifungal therapy alone
Withhold antibiotics until intraoperative cultures return
stages.explanation: Fournier gangrene is typically polymicrobial, so empiric therapy must broadly cover gram-positive organisms (including MRSA), gram-negative rods, and anaerobes—commonly a regimen such as vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam—with the addition of clindamycin (or linezolid) for its antitoxin/anti-exotoxin effect against streptococcal and staphylococcal toxins. A single narrow oral agent (option A) is grossly inadequate for a necrotizing, septic infection. Antifungal monotherapy (option C) does not address the predominant bacterial pathogens. Withholding antibiotics until cultures return (option D) is dangerous and inappropriate in septic shock. Antibiotics, aggressive fluid resuscitation, and emergent surgical source control proceed in parallel.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial debridement, what does ongoing management of this patient typically require?
options: Single washout and immediate discharge once afebrile
ICU-level care with serial operative re-debridements, ongoing resuscitation, glycemic control, and later reconstruction
Outpatient wound care with oral antibiotics only
Permanent urinary diversion as the first step before any debridement
stages.explanation: Fournier gangrene requires multidisciplinary, ICU-level critical care with serial operative re-explorations and re-debridements (often every 24–48 hours until the infection is controlled), continued broad-spectrum antibiotics, aggressive resuscitation, tight glycemic control in this diabetic, and eventual reconstructive/wound coverage—reflecting its high morbidity and mortality. A single washout with immediate discharge (option A) is unsafe and ignores the need for repeated debridement. Outpatient oral antibiotics alone (option C) cannot treat this life-threatening infection. Permanent urinary diversion (option D) is not the first step; while diverting colostomy or suprapubic catheter may be used for source control, definitive debridement is the immediate priority. Survival depends on early, repeated surgical control.

cases.diagnosis: Fournier gangrene (necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum)
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, skin necrosis, anesthesia, and systemic toxicity signal necrotizing soft-tissue infection—do not wait for imaging if the diagnosis is clinically clear.
Fournier gangrene is a polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum, most common in diabetics; subcutaneous gas on CT/plain film supports it.
Emergent surgical debridement is the definitive, life-saving treatment; antibiotics and resuscitation are adjuncts, never substitutes.
Cover broadly (gram-positives including MRSA, gram-negatives, anaerobes) and add clindamycin/linezolid for toxin suppression; expect serial re-debridements in the ICU.

cases.title: Sudden racing heartbeat and breathlessness
cases.chiefComplaint: My heart has been racing and pounding for the last few hours and I feel short of breath.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to the ED with about 5 hours of palpitations that began abruptly while watching television. He describes an irregular, pounding sensation in his chest accompanied by mild dyspnea and lightheadedness, but denies chest pain, syncope, fever, or recent illness. He has had two prior brief episodes of 'fluttering' over the past year that resolved on their own without evaluation. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and metformin 1000 mg twice daily; he takes no anticoagulants or antiarrhythmics. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks three to four beers on weekends, drank heavily the night before at a wedding, and smoked a half-pack of cigarettes daily for 30 years before quitting at age 60. He works as a retired postal worker and lives with his wife. Family history is notable for a father with a stroke at age 72 and a brother with coronary artery disease. He denies prior heart attack, heart failure, or thyroid disease.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly anxious man in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. He is warm and well-perfused with brisk capillary refill. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm that is tachycardic, with variable intensity of S1 and no audible murmur, rub, or gallop. JVP is not elevated. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles or wheezes. Abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no peripheral edema, and pedal pulses are intact though irregular. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no facial droop, dysarthria, or limb weakness. There is no thyromegaly or thyroid nodule on neck palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test?
options: 12-lead electrocardiogram
Transthoracic echocardiogram
CT angiography of the chest
Exercise stress test
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is the immediate, essential test in any patient with palpitations and an irregularly irregular pulse; it confirms the rhythm, defines the ventricular rate, and screens for ischemia or pre-excitation that would change management. A transthoracic echocardiogram is important to assess LV function, valvular disease, and atrial size, but it is not the first-line test in the acute setting and does not establish the rhythm diagnosis. CT angiography evaluates pulmonary embolism or aortic pathology and is not indicated when the presentation points to an arrhythmia without pleuritic pain or hypoxia. An exercise stress test is contraindicated in an actively tachycardic, symptomatic patient and addresses ischemia rather than the acute rhythm.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
ECG: irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, ventricular rate 146 bpm, narrow QRS, no acute ST-T changes
Troponin T: <0.01 ng/mL (normal)
TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
BMP: Na 139, K 4.1, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, glucose 162 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 7.4, Hgb 14.8 g/dL, platelets 230
Chest X-ray: no infiltrate, normal cardiac silhouette, no pulmonary edema

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Multifocal atrial tachycardia
Atrial flutter with variable block
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Ventricular tachycardia
stages.explanation: The ECG shows an irregularly irregular rhythm with absent discrete P waves, a fibrillatory baseline, and a narrow QRS at a rapid rate, which is diagnostic of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. Multifocal atrial tachycardia is irregular but shows at least three distinct P-wave morphologies with discernible P waves, typically in patients with severe COPD. Atrial flutter classically produces a regular or regularly-irregular ventricular response with sawtooth flutter waves, not a chaotic fibrillatory baseline. Ventricular tachycardia produces a wide-complex, usually regular rhythm; this patient has a narrow QRS, excluding it. The acute alcohol binge ('holiday heart') is a recognized precipitant in this patient.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He remains hemodynamically stable with a BP of 118/76. What is the most appropriate initial management of his ventricular rate?
options: Immediate synchronized cardioversion at 120 J
Intravenous metoprolol or diltiazem for rate control
Intravenous amiodarone bolus for rhythm conversion
Intravenous adenosine 6 mg rapid push
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable patient with AF and RVR, rate control with an IV beta-blocker (metoprolol) or non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (diltiazem) is the appropriate first step; both slow AV nodal conduction and relieve symptoms. Immediate synchronized cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamic instability (hypotension, ischemia, decompensated heart failure), which he does not have, and carries thromboembolic risk if AF duration is uncertain and the patient is not anticoagulated. Amiodarone is a reasonable rhythm-control or rate-control agent in selected patients but is not the routine first choice for stable RVR and can chemically cardiovert, raising embolic risk. Adenosine only transiently blocks the AV node and is used to terminate AV-nodal-dependent SVTs, not to control AF.

stages.label: ANTICOAGULATION
stages.question: After rate control, you calculate his CHA2DS2-VASc score. Which statement best guides his long-term anticoagulation?
options: His score is 0, so no anticoagulation is needed
His score is 3, so a direct oral anticoagulant is recommended
Aspirin 81 mg alone provides adequate stroke prophylaxis
Anticoagulation should be withheld until AF recurs
stages.explanation: His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 3: hypertension (1), diabetes (1), and age 65-74 (1). A score of 2 or more in men warrants long-term oral anticoagulation, and a DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, or edoxaban) is preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF for comparable efficacy with lower intracranial bleeding risk. A score of 0 is incorrect given his comorbidities. Aspirin monotherapy is inferior and no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF with elevated CHA2DS2-VASc. Withholding anticoagulation until recurrence is unsafe, because paroxysmal AF carries the same thromboembolic risk as persistent AF and stroke may be the first manifestation.

cases.diagnosis: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
pearls: An irregularly irregular narrow-complex tachycardia with absent P waves and a fibrillatory baseline is atrial fibrillation until proven otherwise.
Rate control with IV beta-blocker or diltiazem is first-line for stable AF with RVR; reserve electrical cardioversion for hemodynamic instability.
Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc in every AF patient; a score of 2 or more in men (3 or more in women) warrants a DOAC for stroke prevention.
An acute alcohol binge ('holiday heart') is a classic reversible precipitant of new-onset atrial fibrillation.

cases.title: Stabbing chest pain and gasping after a fall
cases.chiefComplaint: I can't catch my breath and my chest hurts after I fell off my bike.
cases.history: A 27-year-old tall, thin man is brought in by EMS after a high-speed bicycle crash 30 minutes ago in which he struck his left chest on the handlebars. He reports sudden, severe, sharp left-sided chest pain and progressively worsening shortness of breath that has become much worse over the past 10 minutes. He denies loss of consciousness, neck pain, or abdominal pain. He has no significant past medical history but recalls a 'collapsed lung' on the right side three years ago that was managed with a chest tube. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He smokes about half a pack of cigarettes daily and occasionally vapes, drinks socially, and denies illicit drug use. He works as a barista and lives alone. Family history is notable for a father who is tall and had a spontaneous pneumothorax in his twenties. He is increasingly agitated, restless, and difficult to keep still on the gurney as his breathing worsens, and he keeps trying to sit upright to breathe.
cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic young man in severe respiratory distress, sitting forward and using accessory muscles. The trachea is deviated to the right. The left hemithorax is hyperexpanded with markedly decreased chest wall movement, absent breath sounds, and hyperresonance to percussion on the left; the right lung has breath sounds present but diminished. JVP is distended. Heart sounds are tachycardic and faint, with the point of maximal impulse difficult to localize. There is no subcutaneous emphysema palpable initially. Radial pulses are thready and rapid. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill. He is alert but agitated, oriented to person and place.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his presentation, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a portable upright chest X-ray before any intervention
Perform immediate needle decompression of the left chest
Send him for CT chest to confirm the diagnosis
Intubate and place him on positive-pressure ventilation
stages.explanation: This is a clinical diagnosis of tension pneumothorax: hypotension, severe respiratory distress, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, absent breath sounds with hyperresonance, and distended neck veins. Tension pneumothorax is treated immediately with needle decompression (or finger thoracostomy) without waiting for imaging, because delay can be fatal. Obtaining a chest X-ray first dangerously delays life-saving treatment in an unstable patient. CT chest is even slower and inappropriate while the patient is in extremis. Intubation with positive-pressure ventilation before decompression can rapidly worsen the tension physiology by forcing more air into the pleural space, precipitating cardiovascular collapse.

stages.label: PROCEDURE
stages.question: You proceed with needle decompression. Which landmark is most appropriate for immediate needle thoracostomy in an adult?
options: Second intercostal space at the midclavicular line, just above the third rib
Second intercostal space at the midclavicular line, just below the second rib
Fifth intercostal space at the posterior axillary line
Eighth intercostal space at the midscapular line
stages.explanation: Needle decompression is performed at the second intercostal space in the midclavicular line, inserting the needle just above the upper border of the third rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle that runs along the inferior margin of each rib; the alternative recommended site is the fourth/fifth intercostal space at the anterior axillary line. Inserting just below the second rib risks laceration of the intercostal vessels and nerve, which course along the rib's lower edge. The fifth intercostal space at the posterior axillary line is the site for chest-tube (tube thoracostomy) insertion, not the standard needle decompression landmark. The eighth intercostal space at the midscapular line is too low and risks intra-abdominal injury (diaphragm, liver, or spleen).

stages.label: POST-DECOMPRESSION
stages.content:
Immediately after needle insertion: audible rush of air
SpO2 improves to 94% on non-rebreather over 2 minutes
BP rises to 108/70 mmHg, HR decreases to 98 bpm
Repeat exam: trachea now midline, faint breath sounds returning on the left
Portable chest X-ray (now obtained): residual left pneumothorax with re-expanding lung, no widened mediastinum, no rib fractures, catheter in place

stages.label: DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After successful needle decompression and clinical improvement, what is the next definitive step?
options: Remove the catheter and observe with serial chest X-rays
Discharge home with outpatient pulmonology follow-up
Apply a three-sided occlusive dressing over the catheter
Place a chest tube (tube thoracostomy)
stages.explanation: Needle decompression is a temporizing measure that converts a tension pneumothorax into a simple (open) pneumothorax; definitive treatment requires tube thoracostomy (chest tube) to fully evacuate the pleural space and allow lung re-expansion. Removing the catheter and merely observing risks recurrence of tension physiology and is unsafe after a large traumatic pneumothorax. Discharge home is inappropriate after a tension pneumothorax requiring decompression. A three-sided occlusive dressing is the field treatment for an open ('sucking') chest wound, not the management following needle decompression of a tension pneumothorax with an intact chest wall.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Shortly after the chest tube is placed and the lung rapidly re-expands, he develops worsening hypoxemia with new unilateral pulmonary infiltrates on the side that was decompressed. What is the most likely complication?
options: Recurrent tension pneumothorax
Re-expansion pulmonary edema
Aspiration pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Rapid re-expansion of a chronically or significantly collapsed lung can cause re-expansion pulmonary edema, which presents with hypoxemia and ipsilateral infiltrates within hours of drainage; the risk is reduced by avoiding rapid, large-volume evacuation. Recurrent tension pneumothorax would present with the original tension picture (hypotension, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, distended neck veins), not new infiltrates in a re-expanded lung. Aspiration pneumonia typically develops over a longer time course with risk factors such as depressed consciousness and would not appear immediately after re-expansion. Pulmonary embolism can cause acute hypoxemia but does not characteristically produce unilateral pulmonary infiltrates limited to the freshly re-expanded lung in this immediate temporal pattern.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Considering his body habitus, prior contralateral pneumothorax, smoking, and family history, which underlying condition is most likely predisposing him to recurrent pneumothoraces?
options: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
Cystic fibrosis
Catamenial pneumothorax
Marfan syndrome with apical subpleural blebs
stages.explanation: A tall, thin young man with recurrent spontaneous pneumothoraces and a family history of pneumothorax in a similarly tall relative suggests a connective tissue disorder such as Marfan syndrome, which is associated with apical subpleural blebs that rupture; smoking further increases bleb-related pneumothorax risk. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes panacinar basilar emphysema and typically presents with COPD-like disease, not isolated recurrent pneumothorax in youth. Cystic fibrosis can cause pneumothorax but presents with chronic productive cough, recurrent infections, and pancreatic insufficiency, none described here. Catamenial pneumothorax is recurrent pneumothorax linked to menstruation due to thoracic endometriosis and occurs only in women, so it is impossible in this male patient.

cases.diagnosis: Tension pneumothorax
pearls: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis (hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance) treated with immediate needle decompression before imaging.
Decompress at the 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line or 5th ICS anterior axillary line, passing the needle just above the rib to spare the neurovascular bundle.
Avoid positive-pressure ventilation before decompression, as it can rapidly worsen tension physiology.
Needle decompression is temporizing; every tension pneumothorax requires definitive tube thoracostomy.

cases.title: Severe waves of flank pain and pink urine
cases.chiefComplaint: I have the worst pain of my life in my side and my urine looks pink.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents with 4 hours of severe, intermittent right flank pain that began abruptly while at work and radiates to his right groin and testicle. The pain comes in waves, and during paroxysms he is unable to sit still and paces the room, which he reports is different from his usual back pain. He has associated nausea and vomited twice. He noticed his urine looked pink on his last void. He denies fever, chills, dysuria initially, penile discharge, or trauma. He has had one similar but milder episode two years ago that 'passed on its own.' Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He works in construction, drinks little water during long shifts, eats a high-sodium and high-protein diet, and consumes several energy drinks daily. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not smoke or use drugs. Family history is significant for kidney stones in his father and a brother. He has no personal history of gout or hyperparathyroidism.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man writhing on the stretcher, unable to find a comfortable position, but non-toxic appearing. He is afebrile and not diaphoretic at rest. Abdomen is soft with mild right-sided tenderness but no guarding, rebound, or palpable masses; bowel sounds are present. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness; the left flank is nontender. There is no abdominal bruit. Genital exam reveals a normal, nontender testicle with intact cremasteric reflex and no scrotal swelling or erythema. No inguinal hernia is palpable. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. There is no skin rash or peripheral edema, and pulses are intact in all extremities.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of his presentation most strongly distinguishes renal colic from an acute surgical abdomen?
options: The presence of nausea and vomiting
Restlessness and inability to lie still during pain
Mild abdominal tenderness on palpation
Elevated blood pressure and tachycardia
stages.explanation: Patients with renal colic characteristically writhe and cannot find a comfortable position because the pain is visceral and colicky, whereas patients with peritonitis from a surgical abdomen lie very still because movement worsens pain. This behavioral distinction is a classic and high-yield bedside clue. Nausea and vomiting are nonspecific and occur in both renal colic and surgical conditions like appendicitis or bowel obstruction. Mild diffuse abdominal tenderness is also nonspecific and does not differentiate the two. Tachycardia and hypertension reflect pain and sympathetic response and occur in many acute pain states, offering no discriminating value.

stages.label: LABS & URINALYSIS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: large blood, 30 RBC/hpf, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, pH 5.8, no casts
BMP: Na 140, K 4.2, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 16
CBC: WBC 8.9, Hgb 15.1 g/dL, platelets 245
Urine pregnancy test: not applicable
Urine culture: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis (CT stone protocol)
Abdominal radiograph (KUB) alone
CT abdomen with IV contrast
MRI of the abdomen
