stages.explanation: Non-contrast helical CT (CT stone/KUB protocol) is the gold-standard imaging study for ureterolithiasis: it detects stones of nearly all compositions, localizes the stone, measures its size, and identifies hydronephrosis, all without contrast. A plain KUB radiograph misses radiolucent (uric acid) stones and stones obscured by bowel gas, and cannot assess obstruction. CT with IV contrast can obscure small calcifications against contrast-filled collecting systems and exposes the patient to unnecessary contrast; non-contrast is preferred for stone detection. MRI poorly visualizes calcified stones and is reserved for pregnant patients or when avoiding radiation, after ultrasound. In young or pregnant patients, ultrasound is a reasonable radiation-sparing first study.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms a 4 mm stone at the right ureterovesical junction with mild hydronephrosis and no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate urologic intervention with ureteroscopy
NSAID analgesia, hydration, and medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin
Empiric IV antibiotics and admission for sepsis
Opioids alone with discharge and no follow-up
stages.explanation: A 4 mm distal ureteral stone has a high likelihood of spontaneous passage, so management is conservative: NSAIDs (e.g., ketorolac) are first-line analgesics because they reduce ureteral smooth-muscle spasm and inflammation more effectively than opioids, supplemented by hydration and antiemetics, with an alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin offered as medical expulsive therapy to facilitate passage of distal stones. Immediate urologic intervention is reserved for stones unlikely to pass, refractory pain, obstruction with infection, or solitary kidney. Empiric antibiotics and sepsis admission are unwarranted without evidence of infection (no fever, pyuria, or leukocytosis). Opioids alone without analgesic optimization or follow-up is inadequate, as patients need a strainer, return precautions, and urology follow-up.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His pain is controlled and he is ready for discharge with a urine strainer and urology follow-up. Which finding would most strongly mandate emergent urologic consultation instead of outpatient management?
options: Microscopic hematuria on urinalysis
Pain that responded to a single dose of ketorolac
A 4 mm stone located at the ureterovesical junction
Fever with pyuria indicating an obstructing infected stone
stages.explanation: An obstructing stone in the setting of infection (fever, pyuria, leukocytosis, or bacteriuria) is a urologic emergency: the obstructed, infected collecting system can progress to urosepsis and requires urgent decompression with a ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy plus antibiotics, not outpatient management. Microscopic hematuria is expected in ureterolithiasis and does not itself warrant emergent consultation. Pain that responds to a single NSAID dose indicates the patient is suitable for discharge, the opposite of an admission criterion. A 4 mm stone at the ureterovesical junction has a high spontaneous passage rate and can be managed as an outpatient with medical expulsive therapy and follow-up, provided there is no infection, intractable pain, acute kidney injury, or solitary kidney.

cases.diagnosis: Renal colic (ureterolithiasis)
pearls: Patients with renal colic cannot lie still and writhe in pain, unlike peritonitis patients who lie motionless.
Non-contrast helical CT (stone protocol) is the gold standard for detecting and sizing ureteral stones and assessing hydronephrosis.
NSAIDs are first-line analgesia for renal colic, often superior to opioids by reducing ureteral spasm and inflammation.
Stones under 5 mm usually pass spontaneously; offer tamsulosin as medical expulsive therapy for distal ureteral stones.

cases.title: A man who won't stop convulsing
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by EMS actively convulsing for over 10 minutes.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is brought to the ED by EMS after his wife found him convulsing on the kitchen floor. According to her, the generalized shaking began at least 10 minutes ago and has not stopped; EMS administered intramuscular midazolam 10 mg en route 5 minutes ago without termination of seizure activity. He has a history of alcohol use disorder and a prior withdrawal seizure two years ago, plus hypertension. He was recently trying to quit drinking and his wife believes he has not had alcohol in about 48 hours. His medications include amlodipine and a thiamine supplement he takes inconsistently; he is not on any antiepileptic drug. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes one pack of cigarettes daily and has a 30-year history of heavy alcohol use. There is no known history of epilepsy, head trauma, diabetes, or recent fever. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he continues to have rhythmic generalized tonic-clonic movements with eyes deviated and is unresponsive to voice.
cases.exam: Unresponsive man with ongoing generalized tonic-clonic convulsions, frothy secretions at the mouth, and clenched jaw. There is rightward conjugate gaze deviation with nystagmoid jerks. Pupils are 4 mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally. He is diaphoretic and warm. There is urinary incontinence and a bitten lateral tongue. Cardiopulmonary auscultation is limited by movement but reveals tachycardia and clear lungs without focal crackles. The abdomen is soft. There is no obvious external head trauma, no neck rigidity that can be assessed during convulsions, and no rash. Capillary refill is brisk. He does not localize or follow commands and has not regained consciousness between the convulsive activity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: EMS already gave IM midazolam without effect and he is still seizing. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Load IV phenytoin before any further benzodiazepine
Administer a second adequate-dose benzodiazepine (e.g., IV lorazepam) while securing the airway and checking glucose
Proceed directly to rapid-sequence intubation with propofol
Obtain an emergent CT head before further medication
stages.explanation: This is convulsive status epilepticus (continuous seizure 5 minutes or more, or recurrent seizures without recovery). Benzodiazepines are first-line, and adequate dosing is critical; a single often under-dosed prehospital dose frequently fails, so a second appropriately dosed benzodiazepine (IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg, typically 4 mg, repeated once) should be given while simultaneously managing the ABCs, applying oxygen, obtaining IV access, and checking a point-of-care glucose to catch hypoglycemia. Jumping to phenytoin skips completing first-line therapy. Immediate intubation is not the first priority unless the airway cannot be maintained; benzodiazepines may terminate the seizure first. CT head is important later but must not delay seizure termination in an actively convulsing, hypoxic patient.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best confirms that this is true generalized convulsive status epilepticus rather than a psychogenic nonepileptic event?
options: Forced eye closure with resistance to opening and side-to-side head shaking
Sustained rhythmic tonic-clonic activity with lateral tongue biting, incontinence, and postictal unresponsiveness
Asynchronous, fluctuating limb movements that stop when the patient is distracted
Pelvic thrusting with preserved responsiveness throughout the event
stages.explanation: Sustained rhythmic tonic-clonic activity with lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, and a depressed level of consciousness that does not recover between events strongly supports true generalized convulsive status epilepticus, as seen in this patient. Forced eye closure with active resistance and side-to-side head movements are characteristic of psychogenic nonepileptic events, not epileptic seizures, where the eyes are typically open. Asynchronous, fluctuating, distractible movements that cease with attention diversion suggest a nonepileptic etiology. Pelvic thrusting with preserved awareness throughout also points toward a psychogenic event; genuine convulsive status produces impaired consciousness, a metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate, and stereotyped, synchronized motor activity.

stages.label: POINT-OF-CARE DATA
stages.content:
Point-of-care glucose: 104 mg/dL
After second dose IV lorazepam 4 mg: seizure continues at 25 minutes total
VBG: pH 7.18, pCO2 52, lactate 7.8 mmol/L
BMP: Na 131, K 3.4, Mg 1.4 mg/dL, Cr 1.2
Ethanol level: <10 mg/dL
Acetaminophen/salicylate: negative

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: He continues to seize despite two adequate doses of benzodiazepines. What is the most appropriate second-line (urgent control) therapy?
options: Repeat a third dose of IV lorazepam
Start a continuous propofol infusion without intubation
IV levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate at a loading dose
Administer IV magnesium sulfate as the definitive agent
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus requires prompt escalation to a second-line IV antiepileptic: levetiracetam, fosphenytoin (or phenytoin), or valproate, given as a loading dose; the ESETT trial showed these three are roughly equivalent in efficacy. Repeating a third benzodiazepine dose adds sedation and respiratory depression without added benefit once two adequate doses have failed and only delays definitive therapy. Continuous propofol infusion is a third-line (refractory status) agent that mandates intubation and continuous EEG, and is premature before a second-line drug is tried. IV magnesium is the treatment for eclamptic seizures, not generalized convulsive status epilepticus, though correcting his low magnesium is appropriate adjunctively.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Given his history of recent alcohol cessation and inconsistent supplementation, which intervention is critical to administer to prevent a serious complication?
options: IV dextrose bolus before any vitamins
High-dose corticosteroids
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics and acyclovir
IV thiamine, ideally before or with glucose
stages.explanation: In a malnourished patient with alcohol use disorder, thiamine deficiency predisposes to Wernicke encephalopathy, and administering glucose before thiamine can precipitate or worsen it; therefore IV thiamine should be given before or together with dextrose. A dextrose bolus alone is not indicated because his glucose is normal (104), and giving glucose without thiamine is the very error to avoid. Corticosteroids have no role in this acute seizure etiology. Empiric antibiotics and acyclovir would be appropriate if CNS infection were suspected (fever with meningismus or focal findings on imaging) and may be added during workup, but the immediate critical and uniquely indicated step here, given his nutritional and alcohol history, is thiamine.

stages.label: REFRACTORY MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Despite a full loading dose of a second-line agent, he is still seizing at 45 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Rapid-sequence intubation and a continuous anesthetic infusion (e.g., propofol or midazolam) with continuous EEG monitoring
Discharge planning once the seizure stops on its own
A fourth benzodiazepine dose intramuscularly
Oral loading of an additional antiepileptic via nasogastric tube
stages.explanation: Refractory status epilepticus (continued seizures despite a benzodiazepine plus an adequate second-line agent) requires escalation to continuous-infusion anesthetic therapy, most commonly propofol or midazolam (or pentobarbital), which mandates endotracheal intubation for airway protection and continuous EEG monitoring to titrate to seizure suppression. Waiting for spontaneous resolution risks permanent neuronal injury, rhabdomyolysis, and death as status becomes increasingly refractory. Another benzodiazepine dose has no incremental benefit and worsens respiratory depression. Oral or NG antiepileptic loading is too slow and unreliable in an actively seizing, unprotected airway and is inappropriate in this emergency.

cases.diagnosis: Status epilepticus
pearls: Status epilepticus is a seizure lasting 5 minutes or more, or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness; treat immediately.
Adequately dosed benzodiazepines are first-line; under-dosing is the most common reason for apparent benzodiazepine failure.
Benzodiazepine-refractory status requires a second-line IV agent (levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate), which are roughly equivalent per ESETT.
In alcohol-use or malnourished patients, give thiamine before glucose to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy.

cases.title: Ripping chest pain radiating to the back
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a tearing pain in my chest that's going through to my back.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe, sharp, 'tearing' chest pain that began abruptly 45 minutes ago while he was mowing the lawn. The pain was maximal at onset, is located in the anterior chest, and radiates between his shoulder blades into his back. He describes it as the worst pain he has ever felt and reports it migrated downward toward his abdomen. He had a brief episode of feeling faint. He denies fever, cough, or recent illness. Past medical history includes long-standing poorly controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 40 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily, though he admits he frequently forgets his blood pressure pill. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes one pack of cigarettes daily for 35 years and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as an accountant. Family history is notable for a father who died suddenly of a 'burst blood vessel' in his sixties. He denies prior cardiac catheterization, known aneurysm, cocaine use, or connective tissue disease, though he is unusually tall with long arms.

vitals.BP: right arm 198/108 mmHg, left arm 148/82 mmHg

cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic man in obvious distress clutching his chest. There is a measurable blood pressure differential of 50 mmHg systolic between the right and left arms. The right radial pulse is strong while the left radial pulse is diminished. Cardiac auscultation reveals a soft early diastolic decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border consistent with aortic regurgitation; S1 and S2 are present without rub. JVP is normal. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The abdomen is soft and nontender without a pulsatile mass or bruit. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with symmetric facial movements and equal grip strength. Lower extremity pulses are intact and symmetric. There is no peripheral edema. Skin is cool and clammy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests acute aortic dissection over acute coronary syndrome?
options: Substernal pressure with diaphoresis and nausea
Tearing pain maximal at onset, interarm BP differential, and a new diastolic murmur
Pain reproducible with chest wall palpation
ST-segment elevations in contiguous leads
stages.explanation: The triad of abrupt 'tearing' pain that is maximal at onset and radiates to the back, an interarm systolic blood pressure differential, and a new diastolic (aortic regurgitation) murmur is classic for acute aortic dissection, particularly Stanford type A involving the ascending aorta. Substernal pressure with diaphoresis and nausea is the typical pattern of acute coronary syndrome, where pain usually crescendos rather than being maximal instantly. Chest wall reproducibility suggests a musculoskeletal cause and argues against both dissection and ACS. ST-elevations point toward STEMI; while a type A dissection can occlude a coronary (usually the RCA) and cause inferior ST changes, the constellation here is most specific for dissection.

stages.label: INITIAL WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: sinus tachycardia, left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage, no acute ST-segment elevation
Troponin T: 0.02 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Chest X-ray: widened mediastinum (>8 cm), abnormal aortic contour
D-dimer: markedly elevated
BMP: Cr 1.3 mg/dL; CBC: Hgb 13.9 g/dL
Bedside echo: dilated aortic root with an intimal flap visualized in the ascending aorta and moderate aortic regurgitation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive imaging study to confirm and classify the dissection in this hemodynamically stable patient?
options: CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Coronary angiography
Ventilation-perfusion scan
Non-contrast CT head
stages.explanation: CT angiography of the chest/abdomen/pelvis is the imaging study of choice for a hemodynamically stable patient with suspected aortic dissection; it rapidly confirms the diagnosis, delineates the intimal flap, defines proximal versus distal extent (Stanford A vs B), and assesses branch-vessel involvement to guide surgery. Coronary angiography is for ACS and is generally avoided when dissection is suspected, since it delays definitive imaging and catheter manipulation can be hazardous near a dissection. A V/Q scan evaluates pulmonary embolism, not the aorta. A non-contrast CT head evaluates intracranial pathology and would not address the aortic process; transesophageal echocardiography is the alternative when the patient is too unstable for CT.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms a Stanford type A dissection involving the ascending aorta. While awaiting the operating room, what is the most appropriate initial medical management?
options: Start IV nitroprusside first to lower blood pressure rapidly
Administer IV beta-blockade (e.g., esmolol) to lower heart rate and shear stress, then add a vasodilator as needed
Give IV fluids and thrombolytics for presumed coronary occlusion
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of acute dissection medical therapy is reducing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt), achieved first with IV beta-blockade such as esmolol to target a heart rate around 60 bpm, followed by a vasodilator like nitroprusside to bring systolic BP to roughly 100-120 mmHg. Beta-blockade must precede pure vasodilators, because giving nitroprusside first causes reflex tachycardia that increases shear stress and can extend the dissection. Thrombolytics are contraindicated and catastrophic in dissection, which is not a thrombotic coronary occlusion. Anticoagulation with heparin is likewise contraindicated because it promotes hemorrhage into the false lumen and pericardium. Definitive treatment of type A dissection is emergent surgical repair.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before reaching the operating room he becomes acutely hypotensive with muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, and pulsus paradoxus. Which complication of his proximal dissection is most likely?
options: Acute pericardial tamponade from proximal dissection into the pericardium
Tension pneumothorax
Massive pulmonary embolism
Septic shock
stages.explanation: Proximal extension of a Stanford type A dissection can rupture into the pericardial sac, producing hemopericardium and acute cardiac tamponade, classically presenting with Beck's triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) plus pulsus paradoxus; it is a leading cause of death in type A dissection and demands emergent surgical repair, with pericardiocentesis generally avoided or done cautiously as it can accelerate bleeding. Tension pneumothorax causes tracheal deviation, absent unilateral breath sounds, and hyperresonance, none described here. Massive pulmonary embolism causes acute right heart strain and hypoxemia but is not a direct consequence of an ascending aortic dissection. Septic shock develops with infection and warm, vasodilated physiology over a longer course, inconsistent with this acute mechanical presentation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for this Stanford type A aortic dissection?
options: Medical management alone with strict blood pressure control
Percutaneous coronary intervention
Outpatient follow-up with serial CT surveillance
Emergent open surgical repair (graft replacement of the ascending aorta)
stages.explanation: A Stanford type A dissection (involving the ascending aorta) is a surgical emergency requiring emergent open repair with graft replacement of the ascending aorta, because of the high risk of lethal complications such as aortic rupture, cardiac tamponade, coronary occlusion, and acute aortic regurgitation. Medical management alone is the primary strategy for uncomplicated Stanford type B (descending) dissections, not type A. Percutaneous coronary intervention treats coronary thrombosis and has no role in repairing the dissected ascending aorta. Outpatient surveillance is entirely inappropriate for an acute type A dissection, which carries an hourly mortality of roughly 1-2% in the first 48 hours if untreated.

cases.diagnosis: Aortic dissection (Stanford type A)
pearls: Abrupt tearing chest pain maximal at onset, an interarm BP differential, and a new aortic regurgitation murmur strongly suggest aortic dissection.
CT angiography is the definitive test in stable patients; use TEE if too unstable for CT.
Control shear stress with beta-blockade FIRST (target HR ~60), then add a vasodilator; never give vasodilators alone or anticoagulate.
Stanford type A (ascending) dissection is a surgical emergency; type B (descending) is usually managed medically.

cases.title: A spreading red leg in a diabetic man
cases.chiefComplaint: My lower leg is red, hot, and getting more painful and swollen.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with 3 days of progressive redness, warmth, swelling, and pain of his right lower leg. He recalls a small scrape on his shin from gardening about a week ago. The redness has been spreading upward, and he now has a low-grade fever and feels generally unwell. He denies severe pain out of proportion to exam, skin blistering, crepitus, or dark discoloration. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and empagliflozin 10 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He works as a landscaper, frequently sustains minor leg abrasions, smokes occasionally, and drinks alcohol socially. He has no history of intravenous drug use, recent hospitalization, or known MRSA. Family history is unremarkable. He reports his last hemoglobin A1c was about 8.5%. He has no prior episodes of leg infection or deep vein thrombosis, and he denies recent prolonged immobility or long travel.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, overweight man in no distress. The right lower leg shows a confluent area of erythema with warmth, mild swelling, and tenderness extending from the mid-shin to just below the knee, with indistinct borders and a small healing abrasion centrally. There is no fluctuance, no skin necrosis, no bullae, and no palpable crepitus. Pain on palpation is consistent with the area of visible erythema and not out of proportion. There is no lymphangitic streaking proximally, though a tender right inguinal node is palpable. Distal pulses are intact, capillary refill is brisk, and sensation is grossly intact. The contralateral leg is normal. There is no calf cord or asymmetric edema suggesting DVT.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Deep vein thrombosis
Cellulitis
Necrotizing fasciitis
Stasis dermatitis
stages.explanation: Unilateral, warm, tender, spreading erythema with indistinct borders, fever, and a portal of entry (the abrasion) in a diabetic patient is the classic picture of cellulitis. Deep vein thrombosis can cause a swollen, tender leg but typically lacks the warmth, advancing erythema, fever, and portal of entry seen here, and erythema in DVT is usually less prominent. Necrotizing fasciitis is excluded by the absence of pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis, and rapid systemic toxicity. Stasis dermatitis is usually bilateral, chronic, and associated with venous insufficiency and hyperpigmentation rather than acute unilateral spreading erythema with fever.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which finding, if present, would most strongly raise concern for necrotizing fasciitis rather than simple cellulitis?
options: Erythema with sharply demarcated raised borders
A palpable tender regional lymph node
Low-grade fever of 38.0 °C
Pain out of proportion to physical exam findings with rapid progression
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion to the visible findings, along with rapid progression, is the hallmark early red flag of necrotizing fasciitis, often preceding the late findings of crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis, and systemic toxicity. Sharply demarcated raised borders describe erysipelas, a superficial form of cellulitis, and are not specific for necrotizing infection. A tender regional lymph node reflects local infection draining to nodes and is common in ordinary cellulitis. A low-grade fever is nonspecific and occurs in simple cellulitis; high fevers with hemodynamic instability would be more concerning, but pain out of proportion is the single most useful early discriminator prompting surgical evaluation.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 12.8 ×10³/µL with neutrophil predominance, Hgb 14.2 g/dL, platelets 260
BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 1.0, glucose 184 mg/dL
CRP: 38 mg/L (elevated)
Lactate: 1.4 mmol/L (normal)
Blood cultures: pending
Venous duplex ultrasound: no deep vein thrombosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no purulence, no abscess, and stable vitals. What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy for this non-purulent cellulitis?
options: An agent targeting beta-hemolytic streptococci and MSSA (e.g., cephalexin)
Broad anaerobic coverage with metronidazole alone
Topical mupirocin only
Immediate surgical debridement
stages.explanation: Non-purulent cellulitis is most often caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus, so empiric therapy with a beta-lactam such as cephalexin (or, if severe, IV cefazolin) is appropriate; routine MRSA coverage is added only with purulence, abscess, penetrating trauma, MRSA risk factors, or systemic toxicity. Metronidazole alone covers anaerobes and would miss the predominant streptococcal and staphylococcal pathogens. Topical mupirocin is inadequate for a spreading deep skin infection with systemic signs and is used for impetigo or decolonization. Immediate surgical debridement is the treatment for necrotizing fasciitis, which has been excluded here; it is not indicated for uncomplicated cellulitis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor would most appropriately favor admission for IV antibiotics rather than discharge on oral therapy?
options: A clearly demarcated 6 cm area of erythema with normal vital signs
Systemic signs of infection such as hemodynamic instability or rapidly advancing erythema despite oral therapy
A single tender inguinal lymph node
A history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes
stages.explanation: Systemic toxicity (hypotension, tachycardia, high fever, or rapidly spreading erythema, especially failing outpatient oral therapy) indicates more severe infection and favors admission for IV antibiotics and closer monitoring. A small, well-demarcated area of erythema with normal vitals is mild cellulitis suitable for outpatient oral therapy. A solitary tender lymph node simply reflects regional drainage and is common in uncomplicated cellulitis, not an admission criterion by itself. Diabetes is a risk factor that warrants careful follow-up and lowers the threshold for closer monitoring, but well-controlled diabetes alone, without systemic signs or treatment failure, does not mandate admission. Immunocompromise, inability to tolerate oral intake, or concern for a deeper infection would also tip toward admission.

cases.diagnosis: Cellulitis
pearls: Cellulitis is unilateral spreading warm tender erythema with indistinct borders, often with a portal of entry; DVT and stasis dermatitis are common mimics.
Pain out of proportion to exam, rapid progression, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis are red flags for necrotizing fasciitis requiring urgent surgical evaluation.
Non-purulent cellulitis: cover strep and MSSA (cephalexin/cefazolin). Add MRSA coverage for purulent infection, abscess, or systemic toxicity.
Mark the leading edge of erythema to track response, and address predisposing factors like edema, tinea, and hyperglycemia.

cases.title: Weakness and palpitations on a dialysis day missed
cases.chiefComplaint: I feel weak and my heart feels funny and I missed my dialysis.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presents with generalized weakness, muscle cramps, and a sensation of palpitations. He missed his last two scheduled dialysis sessions because of a family emergency and a transportation problem. Over the past day he has felt increasingly fatigued and weak, with mild nausea, and he reports his legs feel 'heavy.' He denies chest pain, syncope, or shortness of breath. Past medical history includes ESRD secondary to diabetic and hypertensive nephropathy, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Medications include insulin glargine, lisinopril 10 mg daily, carvedilol, sevelamer, and a recently added spironolactone. He has no known drug allergies. He admits to dietary indiscretion, including orange juice and a banana, over the past two days. He does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. He lives with his daughter. Family history includes diabetes and kidney disease. His last potassium at dialysis was 5.2 mEq/L. He denies recent blood transfusion or new medications other than spironolactone.
cases.exam: Fatigued-appearing man in no acute distress. He is alert and oriented. Cardiac exam reveals a regular but bradycardic rhythm without murmur or rub; heart sounds are normal. JVP is mildly elevated. Lungs are clear bilaterally without crackles. The abdomen is soft and nontender; a left forearm arteriovenous fistula has a palpable thrill and audible bruit. There is trace bilateral lower extremity edema. Neurologic exam shows symmetric but mildly reduced (4/5) proximal muscle strength in the lower extremities, diminished deep tendon reflexes, and intact sensation. There is no focal weakness or facial asymmetry. Skin is warm and dry without rash. Capillary refill is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his missed dialysis and dietary history, what is the most appropriate immediate test?
options: 12-lead ECG and a stat serum potassium
CT head without contrast
Echocardiogram
Chest X-ray and D-dimer
stages.explanation: A dialysis patient who missed sessions and ingested high-potassium foods now presenting with weakness, cramps, and bradycardia is at high risk for life-threatening hyperkalemia, so an immediate 12-lead ECG and a stat serum potassium are essential: the ECG detects cardiotoxic changes that demand emergent treatment before the lab value returns. CT head evaluates intracranial pathology and does not address his metabolic emergency or his nonfocal exam. An echocardiogram assesses cardiac structure and function but does not diagnose or guide acute hyperkalemia management. Chest X-ray and D-dimer target pulmonary or volume processes; while volume overload is possible from missed dialysis, the bradycardia and weakness point first to hyperkalemia.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
ECG: peaked, symmetric, narrow-based T waves; PR prolongation; QRS widening to 130 ms; bradycardia at 56 bpm
Serum potassium: 7.4 mEq/L
BMP: Na 137, HCO3 18, BUN 88, Cr 8.9 mg/dL, glucose 142
Ionized calcium: 1.05 mmol/L (low-normal)
VBG: pH 7.30
Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His potassium is 7.4 with QRS widening and peaked T waves. What is the single most urgent first intervention?
options: IV regular insulin with dextrose
Nebulized albuterol
IV calcium gluconate
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (oral)
stages.explanation: With ECG changes of hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, QRS widening), the first and most urgent step is IV calcium (calcium gluconate or chloride), which stabilizes the cardiac membrane and antagonizes the cardiotoxic effects of potassium within minutes; it does not lower the potassium level but prevents lethal arrhythmia while other measures work. Insulin with dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is a key next step but takes 15-30 minutes and does not provide immediate cardioprotection. Nebulized albuterol also shifts potassium intracellularly as an adjunct but is slower and less reliable. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (or newer binders) removes potassium from the body but acts slowly over hours and is not the emergent intervention for a cardiotoxic level.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: After membrane stabilization and shifting agents, what is the definitive treatment to remove potassium in this anuric dialysis patient?
options: Aggressive IV normal saline diuresis
Emergent hemodialysis
High-dose IV furosemide
Repeating insulin and albuterol every 30 minutes indefinitely
stages.explanation: In an anuric ESRD patient, the definitive way to remove the excess potassium load is emergent hemodialysis; shifting agents (insulin, albuterol) and membrane stabilization (calcium) are only temporizing because the potassium remains in the body. Aggressive IV saline is ineffective and dangerous in an anuric patient who cannot excrete the fluid and is already at risk of volume overload. High-dose furosemide relies on functioning kidneys to produce a kaliuresis and will not work in an anuric patient. Repeatedly redosing insulin and albuterol only transiently shifts potassium and risks rebound hyperkalemia and hypoglycemia; it cannot substitute for definitive removal by dialysis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To reduce the risk of recurrent hyperkalemia, which of his home medications should be reviewed and most likely held?
options: Carvedilol
Sevelamer
Insulin glargine
Lisinopril and spironolactone
stages.explanation: The ACE inhibitor lisinopril and the potassium-sparing diuretic spironolactone both raise serum potassium and are common contributors to hyperkalemia in chronic kidney disease; in an ESRD patient with severe hyperkalemia, both should be reviewed and typically held, especially the recently added spironolactone. Carvedilol is a beta-blocker; nonselective beta-blockade can mildly impair potassium handling but is far less important and is not the primary culprit here. Sevelamer is a phosphate binder that does not raise potassium and is part of standard ESRD care. Insulin glargine actually lowers potassium by driving it intracellularly and should not be held; dietary counseling to avoid high-potassium foods and adherence to scheduled dialysis are also essential.

cases.diagnosis: Hyperkalemia
pearls: Peaked T waves, PR prolongation, and QRS widening signal cardiotoxic hyperkalemia; treat based on the ECG, not just the lab value.
IV calcium is the first step with ECG changes: it stabilizes the myocardium within minutes but does not lower potassium.
Insulin/dextrose and albuterol shift potassium intracellularly; binders and dialysis remove it from the body.
Hemodialysis is the definitive therapy for severe hyperkalemia in anuric ESRD patients; review culprit drugs like ACE inhibitors and spironolactone.

cases.title: Sudden breathlessness waking from sleep
cases.chiefComplaint: I woke up gasping for air and I can't breathe lying down.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman is brought to the ED by EMS with acute severe dyspnea that woke her from sleep about an hour ago. She reports she could not breathe lying flat and had to sit upright at the edge of the bed gasping. She describes a few days of increasing leg swelling and exertional breathlessness, and she ran out of her water pill a week ago. She denies chest pain, fever, or cough productive of purulent sputum, though she has frothy sputum now. Past medical history includes hypertension, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%), atrial fibrillation, and chronic kidney disease. Medications are furosemide 40 mg daily (recently lapsed), carvedilol, lisinopril, and apixaban. She has no known drug allergies. She lives alone, does not smoke, and does not drink alcohol. She admits to eating canned soup and salty foods recently. Family history includes coronary artery disease. She denies recent missed cardiology appointments other than her medication lapse and reports no recent illness or new medications.
cases.exam: Elderly woman in obvious respiratory distress, sitting bolt upright, anxious, diaphoretic, and using accessory muscles, speaking in short two-to-three word phrases. She is tachypneic with audible gurgling. Lung exam reveals diffuse bilateral crackles extending more than halfway up both lung fields with scattered wheezes. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular tachycardic rhythm with an audible S3 gallop; no rub. JVP is elevated to the angle of the jaw. There is pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Extremities are cool and mildly mottled. Capillary refill is mildly delayed. Abdomen is soft with mild right upper quadrant fullness. Neurologic exam is grossly nonfocal though she is too dyspneic to fully cooperate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She is in severe distress with SpO2 84% and rising work of breathing. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Immediate endotracheal intubation
Non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP) with supplemental oxygen
Low-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/min and observation
Nebulized ipratropium-albuterol alone
stages.explanation: For acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema with severe respiratory distress and hypoxemia in an awake, cooperative patient, non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) is the immediate intervention of choice; positive intrathoracic pressure reduces preload and afterload, recruits alveoli, and improves oxygenation, often averting intubation. Immediate endotracheal intubation is reserved for patients who fail NIPPV, cannot protect their airway, or are obtunded; she is alert and a candidate for a trial of BiPAP first. Low-flow nasal cannula is grossly inadequate for an SpO2 of 84% with this work of breathing. Nebulized bronchodilators treat bronchospasm and do not address the underlying cardiogenic edema; her wheezing is 'cardiac asthma' from interstitial edema.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' alveolar infiltrates, cephalization of pulmonary vessels, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly
ECG: atrial fibrillation, rate 112, LVH, no acute ST elevations
BNP: 1840 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
Troponin T: 0.03 ng/mL (mildly elevated, no dynamic change)
BMP: Na 136, K 4.4, Cr 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.4)
Bedside lung ultrasound: diffuse bilateral B-lines

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her BP is 198/104 and she remains in flash pulmonary edema on BiPAP. Besides diuresis, what pharmacologic therapy most directly relieves her symptoms?
options: IV nitroglycerin for preload and afterload reduction
IV normal saline bolus
IV metoprolol to slow the heart rate aggressively
Oral amlodipine
stages.explanation: In hypertensive flash pulmonary edema, IV nitroglycerin is highly effective: at low doses it reduces preload and at higher doses also lowers afterload, rapidly relieving pulmonary congestion and improving symptoms, complementing BiPAP and an IV loop diuretic. An IV saline bolus would worsen volume overload and is contraindicated in pulmonary edema. Aggressive IV beta-blockade is hazardous in acute decompensated heart failure with reduced EF and pulmonary edema, as it can precipitate cardiogenic shock; rate control of her AF should be gentle and is secondary to relieving congestion. Oral amlodipine is slow in onset and not appropriate for the acute crisis; rapid-acting IV vasodilation is preferred.

stages.label: DIURESIS
stages.question: Which additional medication should be given to reduce her volume overload?
options: IV furosemide (loop diuretic)
Oral hydrochlorothiazide as monotherapy
IV vasopressin
Spironolactone for immediate diuresis
stages.explanation: An IV loop diuretic such as furosemide is the standard agent to reduce volume overload in acute decompensated heart failure; given intravenously it produces a prompt diuresis and natriuresis, and patients already on oral loop diuretics typically require an IV dose at or above their home dose. Oral hydrochlorothiazide as monotherapy is too weak and slow for acute pulmonary edema, though a thiazide may be added to a loop diuretic for sequential nephron blockade in refractory cases. IV vasopressin raises blood pressure and is used in shock, not for diuresis, and would worsen congestion. Spironolactone provides long-term mortality benefit in HFrEF but is a weak, slow diuretic unsuitable for acute decongestion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She improves on BiPAP, nitroglycerin, and IV furosemide. Which factor most likely precipitated this decompensation and should be addressed before discharge?
options: Diuretic nonadherence and dietary sodium indiscretion
Initiation of a new beta-blocker that morning
Acute pulmonary embolism
Overdiuresis from excessive furosemide use
stages.explanation: The history points clearly to medication nonadherence (she ran out of her furosemide a week ago) combined with high dietary sodium intake (canned soup, salty foods) as the precipitant of this acute decompensation; these are among the most common and most preventable triggers of heart failure exacerbations, so patient education on adherence and sodium restriction is essential before discharge. There is no mention of a newly started beta-blocker, and her carvedilol is chronic. Pulmonary embolism can precipitate decompensation but is not supported by her presentation of clear volume overload and a hypertensive picture. Overdiuresis would cause hypovolemia and prerenal azotemia, the opposite of the volume overload she demonstrates, so it is not the precipitant here.

cases.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure (flash pulmonary edema)
pearls: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema presents with sudden dyspnea, orthopnea/PND, bilateral crackles, an S3, elevated JVP, and a 'bat-wing' chest X-ray.
BiPAP/CPAP reduces preload and afterload, improves oxygenation, and can avert intubation in awake patients with flash pulmonary edema.
In hypertensive flash pulmonary edema, IV nitroglycerin (preload/afterload reduction) plus an IV loop diuretic rapidly relieves congestion.
Avoid IV fluids and aggressive beta-blockade during acute decompensation; look for precipitants like dietary indiscretion or diuretic nonadherence.

cases.title: Black tarry stools and feeling faint
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been passing black tarry stools and I almost passed out standing up.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents with 2 days of black, tarry, foul-smelling stools and worsening fatigue, lightheadedness, and a near-syncopal episode when he stood up this morning. He denies hematemesis but reports nausea and one episode of 'coffee-ground' vomiting last night. He has had no abdominal pain. Past medical history is significant for alcohol-related cirrhosis with known esophageal varices on prior endoscopy, hepatitis C, and peptic ulcer disease. Medications include propranolol (which he admits he stopped taking weeks ago), furosemide, spironolactone, and occasional ibuprofen for knee pain. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks a pint of liquor most days and has a 40-pack-year smoking history. He lives alone and is poorly engaged with care. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior GI bleeds requiring transfusion but recalls being told his liver is 'scarred.' He denies recent NSAID binge beyond his usual ibuprofen and reports no anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: Pale, ill-appearing man who is mildly diaphoretic and lightheaded. He is alert and oriented. Conjunctivae are pale and there is mild scleral icterus. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is mildly distended with shifting dullness suggesting ascites, a palpable splenomegaly, and visible periumbilical collateral veins; it is soft and nontender without rebound. There are several spider angiomata on the chest and palmar erythema. Rectal exam reveals melena in the vault without bright red blood or a palpable mass. Extremities show mild peripheral edema and asterixis is absent. Skin shows no petechiae. Capillary refill is delayed and extremities are cool.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He is tachycardic and hypotensive with active GI bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Send for urgent CT abdomen before any resuscitation
Establish two large-bore IVs, begin volume resuscitation, and type and crossmatch blood
Give oral proton pump inhibitor and discharge with outpatient endoscopy
Start IV vasopressin and observe without IV access
stages.explanation: A hemodynamically unstable patient with an active upper GI bleed requires immediate resuscitation: two large-bore IV catheters, crystalloid while blood is prepared, and an urgent type and crossmatch, alongside continuous monitoring. Stabilizing the airway, breathing, and circulation always precedes diagnostic imaging. Sending an unstable patient to CT first dangerously delays resuscitation and is not the test of choice for upper GI bleeding. Discharging a hypotensive, actively bleeding patient on oral PPI is unsafe and inappropriate. Starting a vasoactive drug without first obtaining IV access and volume resuscitation neglects the fundamental priority of restoring circulating volume and a route for transfusion.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hgb 7.6 g/dL (baseline ~12), MCV 88, platelets 92 ×10³/µL
INR: 1.8
BMP: BUN 48 mg/dL, Cr 1.2, Na 133
LFTs: AST 96, ALT 44, total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, albumin 2.6 g/dL
Lactate: 2.6 mmol/L
Type and screen: O+ ; crossmatch in progress

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his cirrhosis and known varices, which pharmacologic therapies should be started promptly, in addition to an IV proton pump inhibitor?
options: IV octreotide and prophylactic IV ceftriaxone
IV vitamin K alone
Oral sucralfate and an antiemetic
IV epinephrine infusion
stages.explanation: In a cirrhotic patient with suspected variceal hemorrhage, two evidence-based therapies should start promptly: a splanchnic vasoconstrictor (octreotide) to reduce portal pressure and bleeding, and prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone), which reduce infection, rebleeding, and mortality in cirrhotic GI bleeds. An IV PPI is also given empirically until endoscopy clarifies the source. IV vitamin K may modestly help coagulopathy from cirrhosis but does not address portal pressure and is not sufficient alone. Sucralfate and an antiemetic do not control variceal bleeding. An epinephrine infusion is not standard therapy for variceal hemorrhage; endoscopic injection of epinephrine is a procedural technique, not a systemic infusion.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After resuscitation and medical therapy, what is the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic intervention?
options: Colonoscopy
Mesenteric angiography first-line
Exploratory laparotomy
Upper endoscopy (EGD) with band ligation
stages.explanation: Upper endoscopy (EGD) is both diagnostic and therapeutic and should be performed within 12 hours for variceal bleeding once the patient is resuscitated; endoscopic band ligation is the treatment of choice for bleeding esophageal varices and also allows identification and treatment of other upper sources such as peptic ulcers. Colonoscopy evaluates lower GI bleeding and would not address melena from an upper source. Mesenteric angiography (with embolization) is a second-line option when endoscopy fails or is unavailable, not the first-line intervention. Exploratory laparotomy is reserved for uncontrollable hemorrhage refractory to endoscopic and angiographic measures and carries high risk in a decompensated cirrhotic patient.

stages.label: TRANSFUSION
stages.question: His hemoglobin is 7.6 g/dL and bleeding has slowed after initial resuscitation. Which transfusion strategy is most appropriate?
options: Transfuse to a target hemoglobin above 12 g/dL
Withhold all transfusion regardless of hemodynamics
Adopt a restrictive strategy, transfusing to a target of about 7 g/dL
Transfuse only platelets, never packed red cells
stages.explanation: A restrictive transfusion strategy targeting a hemoglobin of roughly 7 g/dL (transfusing when Hgb falls below 7) improves survival in acute upper GI bleeding compared with a liberal strategy, and is especially important in cirrhotic variceal bleeding, where overtransfusion raises portal pressure and can worsen rebleeding. Targeting above 12 g/dL reflects an outdated liberal approach associated with worse outcomes. Withholding transfusion entirely is unsafe in an actively bleeding, recently unstable patient who may need blood if hemoglobin drops further or hemodynamics deteriorate. Transfusing platelets alone ignores the need for red cells to maintain oxygen-carrying capacity; platelets and FFP are given selectively for significant thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy with active bleeding, not as a substitute for red-cell transfusion.

cases.diagnosis: Upper gastrointestinal bleed (suspected variceal hemorrhage)
pearls: Melena and coffee-ground emesis indicate an upper GI source; an elevated BUN:Cr ratio supports an upper GI bleed.
Resuscitate first: two large-bore IVs, volume, and type/crossmatch before any imaging in an unstable bleeder.
In cirrhotics with suspected variceal bleeding, start octreotide and prophylactic ceftriaxone plus an IV PPI; antibiotics reduce mortality.
Perform EGD within ~12 hours; band ligation is first-line for bleeding esophageal varices.

cases.title: Boring epigastric pain after a weekend of drinking
cases.chiefComplaint: I have severe stomach pain that bores straight through to my back.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man presents with 12 hours of severe, constant epigastric pain that radiates straight through to his back and is partially relieved by leaning forward. The pain began the morning after a weekend of heavy alcohol consumption and is associated with persistent nausea and several episodes of vomiting. He denies hematemesis, melena, chest pain, or fever at home. He has had milder similar episodes after past drinking binges. Past medical history includes hypertension and prior alcohol-related gastritis. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily; he takes no NSAIDs and reports no recent new drugs. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks heavily, consuming 8-10 beers daily and more on weekends, and has done so for over 15 years. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and denies illicit drug use. He works in warehouse logistics. Family history includes type 2 diabetes. He denies gallstones, recent ERCP, hypertriglyceridemia history, or recent abdominal trauma, and reports no prior pancreatitis diagnosis.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man lying still, leaning slightly forward, in moderate distress from pain. He is mildly tachycardic and afebrile to low-grade febrile. The abdomen is soft with marked epigastric tenderness and voluntary guarding but no rigidity or rebound; bowel sounds are hypoactive. There is no palpable mass or organomegaly, and Murphy's sign is negative. There is no flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign) or periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen sign). Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable with clear lungs. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There is no scleral icterus. Skin shows no jaundice or spider angiomata. Extremities are well-perfused without edema, and the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory test is most useful to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Serum lipase
Serum troponin
Serum ammonia
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate
stages.explanation: Serum lipase is the most useful and specific test to confirm acute pancreatitis; it rises within hours, remains elevated longer than amylase, and a level at least three times the upper limit of normal in the right clinical context supports the diagnosis. Serum troponin evaluates myocardial injury and is not relevant to epigastric pain radiating to the back from a pancreatic source, although ACS can occasionally present atypically. Serum ammonia is used to assess hepatic encephalopathy, not pancreatitis. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate is a nonspecific inflammatory marker that does not confirm or exclude pancreatitis and does not change acute management.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Lipase: 1240 U/L (normal <60), more than 3x upper limit of normal
CBC: WBC 13.2 ×10³/µL, Hgb 15.0 g/dL, platelets 240
BMP: Na 138, K 3.9, Cr 1.0, glucose 158 mg/dL, Ca 8.9 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 70, ALT 42, alkaline phosphatase 96, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (near normal)
Triglycerides: 180 mg/dL
Lactate: 1.6 mmol/L

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Given his history and the near-normal liver function tests and triglycerides, what is the most likely etiology of his pancreatitis?
options: Gallstone (biliary) pancreatitis
Hypertriglyceridemia
Alcohol use
Medication-induced pancreatitis
stages.explanation: His chronic heavy alcohol use with onset of pain following a binge, combined with near-normal liver enzymes and bilirubin and a non-elevated triglyceride level, points to alcohol as the most likely cause; alcohol and gallstones together account for the majority of acute pancreatitis. Gallstone pancreatitis is suggested by markedly elevated ALT and bilirubin reflecting biliary obstruction, which are absent here. Hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis typically requires triglycerides well above 1000 mg/dL, far higher than his 180. Medication-induced pancreatitis is a diagnosis of exclusion and is unlikely given his only medication is amlodipine and he has a clear alcohol precipitant.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of initial management for his acute pancreatitis?
options: Early aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and antiemetics
Immediate broad-spectrum prophylactic antibiotics
Emergent ERCP for all patients
Strict prolonged bowel rest with delayed feeding for at least one week
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of early acute pancreatitis management is supportive care: early goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (e.g., lactated Ringer's), adequate analgesia, antiemetics, and electrolyte correction, with early oral or enteral feeding once tolerated. Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended in acute pancreatitis without evidence of infection (such as infected necrosis), as they do not improve outcomes and promote resistance. Emergent ERCP is reserved for gallstone pancreatitis complicated by cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction, not for alcoholic pancreatitis. Prolonged bowel rest is outdated; current guidelines favor early enteral feeding within 24-72 hours as tolerated, which improves outcomes compared with prolonged fasting.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next 48 hours, which finding would most strongly indicate progression to severe acute pancreatitis with a worse prognosis?
options: Persistent organ failure beyond 48 hours (e.g., hypotension, hypoxemia, or rising creatinine)
Lipase rising further to 1800 U/L
Mild nausea that resolves with antiemetics
A single low-grade temperature of 37.8 °C on day one
stages.explanation: Severity in acute pancreatitis is defined by the presence and persistence of organ failure: persistent organ failure lasting more than 48 hours (cardiovascular, respiratory, or renal) defines severe acute pancreatitis and carries substantially higher mortality, which is why ongoing monitoring of hemodynamics, oxygenation, and renal function matters more than enzyme trends. The absolute lipase level does not correlate with severity and is not used for prognostication once the diagnosis is made. Mild nausea that resolves is expected and reassuring. A transient low-grade fever early in the course reflects the systemic inflammatory response and, in isolation without organ dysfunction or evidence of infected necrosis, does not signify severe disease. Scoring tools and a rising hematocrit or BUN also help flag patients at higher risk.

cases.diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis (alcohol-induced)
pearls: Diagnose acute pancreatitis with 2 of 3: characteristic epigastric pain radiating to the back, lipase >3x upper limit, or imaging findings.
Lipase is more specific and stays elevated longer than amylase; routine CT is not needed early in a clear-cut presentation.
Alcohol and gallstones cause most cases; normal LFTs/bilirubin argue against a biliary cause, while triglycerides must exceed ~1000 to implicate hypertriglyceridemia.
Management is supportive: early IV fluids, analgesia, antiemetics, and early enteral feeding; avoid prophylactic antibiotics without infected necrosis.

cases.title: Fever and Worsening Headache in a College Freshman
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache, fever, and neck stiffness for the past 12 hours.
cases.history: A 20-year-old college sophomore is brought to the ED by his roommate for a severe, diffuse headache that began this morning along with fever, chills, and progressive neck stiffness. Over the past several hours he has become more drowsy and intolerant of the overhead lights in the room. He vomited twice. He denies recent trauma, focal weakness, or seizure. He lives in a crowded dormitory; his roommate notes that two other students on his floor were hospitalized this week with similar symptoms. He has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. He is up to date on childhood vaccines but is unsure whether he received the meningococcal booster. He drinks alcohol on weekends, denies illicit drug use, and is sexually active with one partner using condoms. Allergies: no known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory. He reports no rash but feels generally weak and achy. He has not traveled recently and has no sick contacts outside the dorm.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing young man who is drowsy but arousable and oriented. Notable nuchal rigidity with pain on passive neck flexion; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs. Photophobia present. Pupils equal and reactive; fundoscopy shows no papilledema. Cranial nerves intact, no focal motor or sensory deficit, normal reflexes, no pronator drift. No petechial or purpuric rash on skin exam. Mucous membranes dry. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals tachycardia with clear lungs. Abdomen soft and nontender. No joint swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate FIRST step in management of this patient?
options: Obtain a noncontrast CT head before any other intervention
Draw blood cultures, then immediately give dexamethasone and empiric antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture at bedside before giving any medications
Start IV acyclovir alone pending viral studies
stages.explanation: In suspected bacterial meningitis, antibiotics must not be delayed. The correct sequence is blood cultures, then dexamethasone plus empiric antibiotics, with LP performed afterward—ideally CSF is obtained before antibiotics, but treatment should never wait. Option A (CT first) delays therapy and is only indicated before LP when specific risk features (immunocompromise, focal deficit, papilledema, seizure, altered consciousness severe enough to herniate) are present—and even then antibiotics precede CT. Option C delays empiric antibiotics, which increases mortality. Option D treats only HSV and omits bacterial coverage, which would be fatal in bacterial meningitis. Empiric coverage and adjunctive dexamethasone are the priority while diagnostics proceed.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for this 20-year-old?
options: Vancomycin plus ceftriaxone
Ceftriaxone alone
Ampicillin plus gentamicin
Vancomycin plus ceftriaxone plus ampicillin
stages.explanation: For immunocompetent adults aged 2–50, empiric therapy is vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae (including resistant strains) and Neisseria meningitidis. Option B (ceftriaxone alone) omits vancomycin, leaving cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus uncovered. Option C is a regimen for neonatal coverage and does not adequately treat the common adult pathogens. Option D adds ampicillin for Listeria, which is indicated only in patients >50, neonates, pregnant women, or the immunocompromised—not in a healthy 20-year-old. Dexamethasone is given with or just before the first antibiotic dose because it reduces neurologic complications, particularly in pneumococcal disease.

stages.label: LABS & CSF
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Na 137 mEq/L
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
CT head: no mass, midline shift, or obstructive hydrocephalus
CSF opening pressure: 32 cm H2O
CSF WBC: 2,400 /µL (92% neutrophils)
CSF glucose: 28 mg/dL (serum glucose 96 mg/dL)
CSF protein: 210 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: gram-negative diplococci

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the CSF findings, which organism is most likely responsible?
options: Streptococcus pneumoniae
Listeria monocytogenes
Neisseria meningitidis
Haemophilus influenzae type b
stages.explanation: Gram-negative diplococci in the CSF of a young adult living in a crowded dormitory with a cluster of cases is classic for Neisseria meningitidis. The neutrophilic pleocytosis, low CSF glucose, and high protein confirm bacterial meningitis. Option A (S. pneumoniae) appears as gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci, not gram-negative. Option B (Listeria) is a gram-positive rod seen in neonates, elderly, and immunocompromised hosts, not typically in healthy young adults. Option D (H. influenzae) is a gram-negative coccobacillus, now rare due to Hib vaccination and not diplococcal in morphology. The epidemiologic setting and Gram stain morphology together point firmly to meningococcus.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: Which intervention is most appropriate for the patient's close dormitory contacts?
options: No prophylaxis is needed unless contacts develop symptoms
Chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone
A two-week course of oral amoxicillin for all floor residents
Immediate lumbar puncture for every exposed roommate
stages.explanation: Close contacts of a meningococcal case (household members, roommates, anyone with exposure to oral secretions) should receive chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or a single dose of ceftriaxone to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases. This should be given as soon as possible, ideally within 24 hours. Option A is unsafe because secondary cases can occur rapidly and prophylaxis is most effective when prompt. Option C uses the wrong agent (amoxicillin does not eradicate carriage) and overly broad targeting. Option D (LP for asymptomatic contacts) is not indicated and does not prevent disease. The case must also be reported to public health authorities.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient?
options: Discharge home with oral antibiotics and 24-hour follow-up
Admit to a general medical floor with standard precautions
Admit to an ICU or monitored bed with droplet precautions
Observe in the ED for 6 hours, then reassess for discharge
stages.explanation: Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency requiring inpatient IV antibiotics and close monitoring for complications such as cerebral edema, seizures, septic shock, and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome. Given his elevated opening pressure, drowsiness, and tachycardia, he warrants an ICU or closely monitored bed. Droplet precautions are required for the first 24 hours of effective therapy for meningococcal disease. Option A is dangerous—oral therapy is inadequate and outpatient management risks rapid deterioration. Option B underestimates the need for close monitoring and omits droplet precautions. Option D delays definitive admission and provides insufficient monitoring for a patient at high risk of deterioration.

cases.diagnosis: Bacterial (meningococcal) meningitis
pearls: Never delay antibiotics for CT or LP in suspected bacterial meningitis—blood cultures, then dexamethasone plus antibiotics, then LP.
Empiric therapy for adults 2–50 is vancomycin plus ceftriaxone; add ampicillin for Listeria coverage if >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant.
CSF showing neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, and high protein indicates bacterial meningitis; gram-negative diplococci suggest N. meningitidis.
Give chemoprophylaxis (rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) to close contacts and report meningococcal cases to public health.

cases.title: Sudden Severe Scrotal Pain in a Teenage Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep about 5 hours ago.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents with acute, severe left-sided scrotal pain that woke him from sleep roughly 5 hours ago. The pain was abrupt in onset, is constant, and radiates to the left lower abdomen. He has vomited once and feels nauseated. He denies dysuria, urinary frequency, penile discharge, or fever. There was no trauma. He has had brief similar episodes of self-resolving scrotal pain over the past few months, each lasting minutes. He is not sexually active. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He is a high-school athlete and denies recent illness. Immunizations are up to date including mumps. Family history is noncontributory. His mother reports he initially delayed telling anyone because he was embarrassed, which accounts for the delay in presentation. He rates the pain 9/10 and is unable to find a comfortable position.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable adolescent in obvious distress, frequently shifting position. The left testis is exquisitely tender, swollen, and lies higher in the scrotum than the right with a horizontal (transverse) orientation. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left and present on the right. Elevation of the scrotum does not relieve the pain (negative Prehn sign). There is no overlying erythema or warmth early on. No inguinal hernia is palpable. Abdomen is soft with mild left lower quadrant tenderness, no rebound or guarding. No penile discharge.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which physical exam finding most strongly supports the leading diagnosis?
options: Positive Prehn sign with relief upon scrotal elevation
Presence of a brisk cremasteric reflex bilaterally
Absent cremasteric reflex with a high-riding, horizontally oriented testis
Tenderness localized to the superior pole with a palpable 'blue dot'
stages.explanation: Testicular torsion classically presents with an absent cremasteric reflex, a high-riding testis, and a horizontal (transverse) lie due to twisting of the spermatic cord. These findings, with abrupt severe pain and nausea, strongly favor torsion. Option A (positive Prehn sign—relief with elevation) suggests epididymitis, not torsion. Option B (brisk bilateral cremasteric reflex) argues against torsion, which abolishes the reflex on the affected side. Option D (the 'blue dot' sign with superior-pole tenderness) indicates torsion of the testicular appendage, a benign and self-limited condition, not torsion of the testis itself. The combination here is highly specific for true testicular torsion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order a urinalysis and treat empirically for epididymitis
Obtain an immediate urology consult for likely surgical exploration
Wait for scrotal Doppler ultrasound results before any consultation
Administer antibiotics and arrange outpatient urology follow-up
stages.explanation: When the clinical findings strongly suggest testicular torsion, the priority is immediate urologic consultation for surgical exploration—imaging must not delay the operating room because the testis salvage rate falls sharply after about 6 hours of ischemia. Doppler ultrasound can confirm the diagnosis but should be obtained concurrently and never allowed to delay surgery in a high-probability case. Option A misdiagnoses the problem and risks losing the testis. Option C wrongly prioritizes imaging over consultation in a time-critical scenario. Option D treats the wrong condition and dangerously delays definitive care. Torsion is a surgical emergency; involve urology immediately while diagnostics proceed in parallel.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no pyuria
WBC: 9.8 ×10³/µL
Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound: enlarged, heterogeneous left testis with absent intratesticular arterial flow
Whirlpool sign of the spermatic cord present on the left
Right testis: normal size with preserved arterial flow

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While the operating room is being prepared, which bedside maneuver may be attempted?
options: Manual detorsion by rotating the testis medially ('closing a book')
Manual detorsion by rotating the testis laterally ('opening a book')
Firm continuous compression of the affected testis to reduce swelling
Ice pack application with no attempt at detorsion
stages.explanation: Manual detorsion is a temporizing measure when surgery will be delayed. Most testes torse medially (inward), so detorsion is classically performed by rotating the testis laterally—'opening a book'—often described as turning outward toward the thigh. Successful detorsion brings pain relief and improved positioning, but surgical exploration with orchiopexy is still required because detorsion may be incomplete or residual twisting may persist. Option A rotates the wrong direction and can worsen torsion. Option C (compression) is harmful and does not relieve cord twisting. Option D abandons a potentially testis-saving maneuver; ice alone does not restore perfusion. Detorsion is a bridge to, not a substitute for, the operating room.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: At surgical exploration the left testis is found to be viable after detorsion. What is the appropriate operative management?
options: Orchiopexy of the affected testis only
Left orchiectomy regardless of viability
Detorsion alone without fixation
Bilateral orchiopexy—fixation of both the affected and contralateral testes
stages.explanation: A viable testis should be detorsed and fixed (orchiopexy), and the contralateral testis must also be fixed in the same operation because the anatomic predisposition (bell-clapper deformity) is typically bilateral, putting the other side at risk of future torsion. Option A leaves the unaffected side vulnerable. Option B (orchiectomy) is reserved for a clearly nonviable, necrotic testis—not a viable one. Option C (detorsion alone) risks recurrent torsion without fixation. Bilateral orchiopexy is standard of care whether the affected testis is salvaged or removed, to protect the remaining testicle. This patient's prior intermittent episodes were warning signs of recurrent partial torsion.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor is the most important determinant of testicular salvage in this patient?
options: The patient's age at presentation
Time from symptom onset to detorsion
Whether antibiotics were given preoperatively
The presence or absence of nausea and vomiting
stages.explanation: Salvage of the torsed testis depends overwhelmingly on the duration of ischemia. Salvage rates are highest (often >90%) when detorsion occurs within about 6 hours of onset, fall substantially between 6 and 12 hours, and are poor beyond 24 hours. This patient presented around 5 hours after onset, placing him within the optimal salvage window—hence the urgency. Option A (age) does not determine salvage once torsion has occurred. Option C (antibiotics) plays no role in salvage, as torsion is ischemic, not infectious. Option D (nausea/vomiting) reflects severity but is not the determinant of outcome. The single most actionable variable is time to restoring perfusion.

cases.diagnosis: Testicular torsion (left)
pearls: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis: abrupt severe pain, high-riding horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex, and negative Prehn sign.
Do not let ultrasound delay urologic consultation—salvage rates drop sharply after 6 hours of ischemia.
Attempt manual detorsion ('opening a book,' rotating laterally/outward) as a bridge if surgery is delayed.
Always perform bilateral orchiopexy because the bell-clapper deformity is usually bilateral.

cases.title: Collapse and Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe lower abdominal pain followed by light-headedness and a near-fainting episode.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman is brought to the ED after sudden, severe left lower abdominal pain that began an hour ago while at work, followed by light-headedness and a near-syncopal episode. She describes the pain as sharp and now diffuse across the lower abdomen, with new right shoulder discomfort. Her last menstrual period was approximately 7 weeks ago, and her cycles are usually regular; she had attributed a missed period to stress. She has had light vaginal spotting for two days. She is sexually active, uses condoms inconsistently, and has a history of chlamydial pelvic inflammatory disease two years ago. She had one prior ectopic pregnancy managed medically. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is noncontributory. She feels increasingly weak and cold. On arrival she is pale and diaphoretic. She has no prior abdominal surgeries and no current contraception with an IUD.
cases.exam: Pale, diaphoretic, anxious young woman appearing acutely ill. Skin cool and clammy. Abdomen diffusely tender, most marked in the left lower quadrant, with guarding and rebound tenderness; mild distension noted. Pelvic exam reveals scant dark blood at the cervical os, marked cervical motion tenderness, and left adnexal fullness with severe tenderness. Capillary refill is delayed at 3 seconds. Heart sounds rapid but regular; lungs clear. Extremities show no edema. The patient is mentating but somewhat slow to respond.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate action in this hypotensive patient?
options: Send her to CT of the abdomen and pelvis to localize the bleeding
Establish large-bore IV access and begin aggressive fluid resuscitation while obtaining a bedside pregnancy test
Administer IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease
Give IM methotrexate for a presumed ectopic pregnancy
stages.explanation: This patient is in hemorrhagic shock. The immediate priority is resuscitation: two large-bore IVs, fluids, type and crossmatch, and a bedside qualitative beta-hCG to rapidly confirm pregnancy. Stabilization and identifying pregnancy guide everything that follows. Option A (CT) is inappropriate for an unstable patient—she cannot safely leave the resuscitation area, and a bedside FAST is faster. Option C treats PID, which does not explain shock from hemoperitoneum and delays correct management. Option D (methotrexate) is absolutely contraindicated in a hemodynamically unstable patient or ruptured ectopic—surgery is required, not medical therapy. Resuscitate first, confirm pregnancy, then proceed to definitive surgical care.

stages.label: BEDSIDE STUDIES
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: positive
Serum beta-hCG: 4,800 mIU/mL
Hemoglobin: 8.1 g/dL (baseline 13.0)
FAST exam: free fluid in Morrison's pouch and the pelvis
Transvaginal ultrasound: empty uterus, no intrauterine gestational sac; complex left adnexal mass; moderate free fluid in the cul-de-sac
Blood type: O negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do the ultrasound and beta-hCG findings best explain her presentation?
options: An intrauterine pregnancy with a corpus luteum cyst
A ruptured ectopic pregnancy with hemoperitoneum
A threatened miscarriage of an intrauterine pregnancy
Hemorrhagic ovarian cyst unrelated to pregnancy
stages.explanation: A positive beta-hCG of 4,800 mIU/mL (above the discriminatory zone) with an empty uterus, a complex adnexal mass, and free fluid on FAST in a shocked patient is diagnostic of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy with hemoperitoneum. The shoulder pain reflects diaphragmatic irritation from intraperitoneal blood. Option A is excluded—above the discriminatory zone an intrauterine sac should be visible if the pregnancy were intrauterine. Option C requires an identifiable intrauterine pregnancy, which is absent. Option D ignores the positive pregnancy test, which mandates assuming ectopic until proven otherwise. Her prior ectopic and PID are major risk factors. This is a surgical emergency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management for this patient?
options: IM methotrexate with serial beta-hCG monitoring
Expectant management with repeat ultrasound in 48 hours
Emergent surgical intervention (laparoscopy or laparotomy)
Dilation and curettage to evacuate the uterus
stages.explanation: A ruptured ectopic pregnancy with hemodynamic instability and hemoperitoneum requires emergent surgery—laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingostomy, or laparotomy if the patient is too unstable. Surgery controls hemorrhage and removes the ectopic tissue. Option A (methotrexate) is contraindicated in rupture and instability; it is reserved for small, unruptured ectopics in stable patients with low beta-hCG and no contraindications. Option B (expectant management) is dangerous and only ever considered for stable patients with declining low beta-hCG. Option D (D&C) treats the uterus, but the pregnancy is extrauterine, so curettage would not address the bleeding source. This patient needs the operating room immediately alongside ongoing resuscitation and blood products.

stages.label: RESUSCITATION
stages.question: She remains hypotensive despite 2 L of crystalloid. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue crystalloid boluses until 4 L total is infused
Transfuse O-negative packed red blood cells and activate massive transfusion as needed
Start a norepinephrine infusion to raise the blood pressure
Administer tranexamic acid alone and reassess in 30 minutes
stages.explanation: In ongoing hemorrhagic shock that does not respond to initial crystalloid, the next step is blood—transfuse packed red cells (O negative until crossmatched blood is available, appropriate here since she is O negative) and activate massive transfusion protocol if bleeding continues. Definitive hemorrhage control in surgery proceeds in parallel. Option A (more crystalloid) worsens dilutional coagulopathy and does not replace lost oxygen-carrying capacity; large-volume crystalloid is harmful in hemorrhage. Option C (vasopressors) does not address the underlying volume loss and can mask continued bleeding; pressors are not first-line in hemorrhagic shock.
