It should be performed within about 1–2 weeks to confirm the diagnosis; steroids should not be delayed for it
It must be done before any steroids are given or it will be falsely negative
It replaces the need for steroid treatment
It should only be done if the ESR is normal
stages.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy remains the diagnostic gold standard and should be obtained, ideally within 1–2 weeks of starting steroids, to confirm the diagnosis and justify the prolonged steroid course; importantly, treatment is started first and biopsy is not delayed (B). The biopsy stays positive for at least 2 weeks after steroids begin because the histologic changes (granulomatous inflammation, giant cells) persist, so the claim that steroids cause an immediate false negative (C) is wrong. The diagnosis is supported clinically but biopsy confirmation matters given the long, toxic steroid course (A). Biopsy does not replace treatment (D). A normal ESR (E) does not gate biopsy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which additional medication is most appropriate to add to corticosteroids to reduce ischemic complications?
options: Low-dose aspirin
Therapeutic warfarin
Methotrexate as monotherapy
Sumatriptan
Calcium channel blocker uptitration
stages.explanation: Low-dose aspirin is commonly added in GCA to reduce the risk of cranial ischemic events such as vision loss and stroke (A). Therapeutic warfarin (B) is not indicated and adds bleeding risk without proven benefit. Methotrexate or tocilizumab may be used as steroid-sparing agents to reduce cumulative steroid toxicity, but methotrexate is an adjunct, not monotherapy replacing steroids (C). Triptans (D) are vasoconstrictors, contraindicated, and irrelevant. Uptitrating a calcium channel blocker (E) addresses blood pressure, not the arteritis. Patients on prolonged high-dose steroids also need bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate) and glucose monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis with impending anterior ischemic optic neuropathy
pearls: Suspect giant cell arteritis in patients over 50 with new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual change — and start high-dose steroids immediately, before biopsy.
ESR and CRP are markedly elevated; expect a normocytic anemia and thrombocytosis. A normal ESR does not fully exclude GCA if clinical suspicion is high.
Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard and remains positive for up to ~2 weeks after steroids start, so never delay treatment for it.
Add low-dose aspirin to reduce ischemic events, and protect against steroid complications (bone, glucose); GCA frequently overlaps with polymyalgia rheumatica.

cases.title: Daily Headache and Transient Vision Blackouts
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening daily headaches with brief episodes of going blind in both eyes and ringing in the ears.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents with a six-week history of worsening generalized headaches that are worse in the morning and when she lies down, bends over, or strains. She describes brief episodes lasting seconds in which her vision "blacks out" in both eyes, often when standing up (transient visual obscurations). She also notes a whooshing, pulsatile ringing in her ears and intermittent horizontal double vision. She denies fever, neck stiffness, weakness, numbness, or seizures. She has gained about 25 pounds over the past year. PMH: obesity (BMI 38), polycystic ovary syndrome. Medications: started isotretinoin two months ago for acne; oral contraceptive pill; occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, no alcohol or drugs. Family history noncontributory. She is frustrated that over-the-counter analgesics no longer help and the headaches wake her at night.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing woman with elevated BMI. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally; visual fields show enlarged blind spots bilaterally on confrontation. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema with blurred, elevated disc margins and obscured vessels. There is a partial right sixth nerve (abducens) palsy with limited abduction of the right eye and horizontal diplopia on right gaze; this is a non-localizing false-localizing sign. Pupils equal and reactive, no afferent pupillary defect. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Full strength and symmetric reflexes, normal sensation, normal coordination and gait. Neck supple. No focal motor deficit. Cardiopulmonary exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given bilateral papilledema, what is the most appropriate first diagnostic step before lumbar puncture?
options: Proceed directly to lumbar puncture
Obtain neuroimaging (MRI brain, ideally with MR venography) to exclude a mass and venous sinus thrombosis
Start acetazolamide empirically and discharge
Order a temporal artery biopsy
Begin broad-spectrum antibiotics for meningitis
stages.explanation: With papilledema, you must first exclude a space-occupying lesion and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis before LP, so neuroimaging — MRI brain with MR venography — is the appropriate first step (B). Performing LP before imaging in the presence of papilledema risks herniation if a mass is present and could miss venous thrombosis, a key IIH mimic (A). Empiric acetazolamide with discharge (C) skips the required exclusion of secondary causes and confirmatory LP. Temporal artery biopsy (D) is for GCA, not this young patient. Antibiotics for meningitis (E) are unwarranted without fever, meningismus, or supportive findings; this presentation fits raised ICP, not infection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: no mass lesion; partially empty sella; flattening of the posterior globes; distended optic nerve sheaths
MR venography: patent dural venous sinuses (no thrombosis)
Lumbar puncture (lateral decubitus): opening pressure 32 cm H2O
CSF analysis: WBC 1 /µL, protein 22 mg/dL, glucose 58 mg/dL, normal cell count and chemistries
CBC and basic metabolic panel: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best confirms idiopathic intracranial hypertension in this patient?
options: Papilledema with a mass lesion on MRI
Elevated CSF opening pressure with normal CSF composition and no mass or venous thrombosis on imaging
Low CSF opening pressure with high CSF protein
Normal opening pressure with abnormal CSF white count
Bilateral papilledema alone
stages.explanation: IIH is diagnosed by the modified Dandy criteria: signs of raised ICP (papilledema), an elevated CSF opening pressure (>25 cm H2O in adults) with normal CSF composition, and no structural or vascular cause on neuroimaging — exactly this patient's picture (B). A mass lesion (A) would make the intracranial hypertension secondary, not idiopathic. Low opening pressure with high protein (C) suggests other pathology (e.g., spinal block) and is the opposite of IIH. An abnormal CSF white count (D) points to infection or inflammation. Papilledema alone (E) is insufficient without documenting elevated opening pressure and excluding secondary causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment, in addition to weight management?
options: Acetazolamide to reduce CSF production
Long-term oral corticosteroids
Therapeutic anticoagulation
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics
Immediate optic nerve sheath fenestration for all patients
stages.explanation: First-line medical therapy for IIH is acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production, combined with weight loss, which is disease-modifying (A). Identifying and stopping contributing agents (her isotretinoin and possibly the estrogen-containing contraceptive) is also important. Long-term steroids (B) are avoided — they cause weight gain and rebound on withdrawal. Anticoagulation (C) would be indicated only if venous sinus thrombosis were present, which imaging excluded. Antibiotics (D) have no role without infection. Surgical options such as optic nerve sheath fenestration or venous sinus stenting/shunting (E) are reserved for medication failure or rapidly progressive vision loss, not first-line for all patients.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most warrant urgent specialist referral and consideration of surgical intervention?
options: Persistent pulsatile tinnitus
Progressive visual field loss or worsening visual acuity despite medical therapy
Mild residual morning headache
A single transient visual obscuration
Weight loss of five pounds
stages.explanation: The feared, preventable complication of IIH is permanent vision loss. Progressive visual field loss or declining acuity despite maximal medical therapy mandates urgent ophthalmology/neurosurgery referral for surgical intervention (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF diversion/venous sinus stenting) (B). Pulsatile tinnitus (A) is a common, benign-feeling symptom of IIH and not by itself an indication for surgery. A residual mild headache (C) and a single transient obscuration (D) are expected and managed medically. Weight loss (E) is therapeutic and reassuring, not a trigger for escalation. Serial visual fields and acuity monitoring are essential to detect deterioration early.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri)
pearls: IIH classically affects women of childbearing age with obesity; suspect it with headache, papilledema, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, and a non-localizing CN VI palsy.
Always image (MRI plus MR venography) before LP when papilledema is present to exclude a mass and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.
Diagnosis requires an elevated CSF opening pressure (>25 cm H2O) with normal CSF composition and no structural or vascular cause (modified Dandy criteria).
First-line treatment is acetazolamide plus weight loss and removing offending agents; escalate to surgery for progressive vision loss despite medical therapy.

cases.title: Ascending Weakness and Tingling Feet
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive weakness and numbness rising from the feet into the legs over four days.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man presents with four days of symmetric tingling and weakness that began in his feet and has ascended to his thighs; today he noticed his hands feel clumsy and he climbed stairs with difficulty. He fell once because his legs "gave out." He denies bowel or bladder incontinence, back pain, or a sensory level on his trunk. He had a self-limited diarrheal illness about two and a half weeks ago after a barbecue, attributed to undercooked chicken. He now reports mild shortness of breath when lying flat and a weaker cough. He denies fever, neck stiffness, or recent travel. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works construction, social alcohol, nonsmoker, no drug use. Family history noncontributory. On further questioning he notes some difficulty with double vision today and a sense that his face feels weak. He is anxious about the rapid progression and his breathing.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly tachypneic, speaking in slightly shortened sentences. Cranial nerves: mild bilateral facial weakness; extraocular movements largely intact with subtle limitation. Motor: symmetric weakness, 3/5 in the lower extremities and 4/5 in the upper extremities, proximal and distal. Deep tendon reflexes are absent throughout (areflexia) with downgoing toes. Sensation: mildly decreased vibration and light touch distally, no truncal sensory level. Coordination limited by weakness; gait unsafe. Autonomic: labile blood pressure and tachycardia. Lungs clear but with shallow respirations and weak cough; he cannot count past 15 in a single breath. Abdomen benign. No spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best characterizes this presentation?
options: Asymmetric weakness with a sensory level and bowel/bladder dysfunction
Symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia following a recent gastrointestinal infection
Fluctuating weakness worse at the end of the day with ptosis and normal reflexes
Acute unilateral facial droop with forehead sparing
Proximal weakness with elevated creatine kinase and a heliotrope rash
stages.explanation: This is Guillain-Barre syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy): symmetric, ascending weakness with areflexia, often following a Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis 1–3 weeks earlier (B). A sensory level with bowel/bladder involvement and asymmetric weakness (A) points to spinal cord compression/transverse myelitis, not GBS. Fatigable weakness with ptosis and preserved reflexes (C) describes myasthenia gravis. Unilateral facial droop with forehead sparing (D) is a central facial palsy. Proximal weakness with high CK and heliotrope rash (E) is dermatomyositis. The hallmarks of GBS are the ascending pattern, areflexia, antecedent infection, and the relative sparing of bladder function.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which bedside assessment is most important to perform repeatedly to detect impending respiratory failure?
options: Pulse oximetry alone
Serial forced vital capacity and negative inspiratory force measurements
Peak expiratory flow rate compared with asthma norms
Arterial blood gas every six hours regardless of status
Chest X-ray every shift
stages.explanation: In GBS, neuromuscular respiratory failure is the leading cause of death, and it develops before oxygen desaturation occurs. Serial bedside spirometry — forced vital capacity (FVC) and negative inspiratory force (NIF/maximal inspiratory pressure) — is the most sensitive way to track diaphragmatic and respiratory muscle decline (B). The "20/30/40 rule" (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30 cm H2O, or maximal expiratory pressure <40) signals the need for intubation. Pulse oximetry (A) is a late, falsely reassuring indicator. Peak flow (C) is for obstructive disease. Routine ABGs (D) and chest X-rays (E) lag behind and do not capture the trend in respiratory muscle strength.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bedside forced vital capacity: 14 mL/kg (declining from 22 mL/kg on arrival)
Negative inspiratory force: −22 cm H2O
Lumbar puncture CSF: protein 95 mg/dL (elevated), WBC 3 /µL (albuminocytologic dissociation)
Nerve conduction studies: prolonged distal latencies and conduction block consistent with demyelination
MRI spine: normal cord, no compressive lesion
Electrolytes and CK: within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the declining vital capacity and negative inspiratory force, what is the most urgent intervention?
options: High-flow nasal cannula and continued observation on the floor
Intubation and mechanical ventilation with ICU admission
Immediate high-dose IV corticosteroids
Bilevel positive airway pressure for home use
Bedside chest physiotherapy and incentive spirometry
stages.explanation: An FVC that has fallen to 14 mL/kg with a NIF of only −22 cm H2O meets criteria for impending neuromuscular respiratory failure (the 20/30/40 rule), so the patient needs intubation and mechanical ventilation with ICU-level monitoring (B). Waiting on high-flow nasal cannula on the floor (A) is unsafe because oxygenation can look normal until abrupt collapse. Corticosteroids (C) are ineffective in GBS and not indicated. BiPAP (D) is inappropriate as a substitute for definitive airway control in rapidly progressive, bulbar-involved GBS at risk of aspiration. Chest physiotherapy (E) does not address the failing respiratory muscles. ICU admission also allows monitoring for autonomic instability.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which disease-modifying therapy is appropriate to initiate for this patient's underlying condition?
options: High-dose IV corticosteroids
IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange
Pyridostigmine
Broad-spectrum antibiotics
Long-term oral azathioprine alone
stages.explanation: The disease-modifying treatments for GBS are IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasma exchange (plasmapheresis); they are equally effective and are not combined (B). Starting either early shortens recovery, especially in patients who are non-ambulatory or have rapid progression, as here. Corticosteroids (A) have repeatedly failed to show benefit in GBS and may be harmful. Pyridostigmine (C) treats myasthenia gravis, a different disorder. Antibiotics (D) do not treat the post-infectious autoimmune neuropathy and the antecedent Campylobacter illness has already resolved. Chronic immunosuppressants like azathioprine (E) are used in CIDP, not acute GBS. Supportive ICU care plus IVIG or plasma exchange is the standard of care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While in the ICU, the patient suddenly develops wide swings in blood pressure and an episode of bradycardia and asystole. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Pulmonary embolism
Autonomic dysfunction (dysautonomia), a recognized and dangerous feature of Guillain-Barre syndrome
Acute myocardial infarction
Medication overdose from IVIG
Hypoglycemia
stages.explanation: GBS commonly involves the autonomic nervous system, causing labile blood pressure, tachy- and bradyarrhythmias, and even asystole — dysautonomia is a leading cause of death and mandates continuous cardiac monitoring in the ICU (B). Pulmonary embolism (A) is a real risk in immobilized patients and warrants DVT prophylaxis, but it does not classically cause this swinging autonomic instability with bradyasystole. Myocardial infarction (C) is possible but less likely than dysautonomia given the pattern and his presentation. IVIG (D) does not cause this picture, though it has its own risks (thrombosis, aseptic meningitis). Hypoglycemia (E) would be detected on point-of-care glucose and does not explain blood-pressure lability.

cases.diagnosis: Guillain-Barre syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy) with impending respiratory failure
pearls: GBS presents as symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia, often 1–3 weeks after a Campylobacter or viral infection; CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal cells).
Respiratory failure is the leading cause of death — monitor serial FVC and NIF, and intubate using the 20/30/40 rule rather than waiting for desaturation.
Treat with IVIG or plasma exchange (not both); corticosteroids are ineffective and should not be used.
Watch for autonomic instability (labile blood pressure, arrhythmias) and bulbar weakness raising aspiration risk; manage in an ICU setting.

cases.title: Worsening Droopy Eyes and Choking on Food
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of worsening droopy eyelids, slurred speech, choking on liquids, and now shortness of breath.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis is admitted to the ICU with two days of worsening generalized weakness. She reports drooping eyelids and double vision worse in the evening, increasingly slurred and nasal speech, difficulty chewing, and choking on liquids with nasal regurgitation. Today she developed progressive shortness of breath and a weak cough. Symptoms began after she started a course of azithromycin for a presumed sinus infection and admits she also missed several doses of her usual medication while feeling unwell with a recent cold. PMH: myasthenia gravis diagnosed three years ago (acetylcholine receptor antibody positive), hypothyroidism. Medications: pyridostigmine 60 mg five times daily, prednisone 10 mg daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; recently started azithromycin. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, no alcohol. Family history: autoimmune thyroid disease. She is frightened and increasingly fatigued with the effort of breathing.
cases.exam: Anxious, fatigued woman sitting upright using accessory muscles. Bilateral ptosis worsening on sustained upgaze; fatigable extraocular movements with diplopia. Speech is nasal and becomes less intelligible as she talks. Weak palatal elevation with poor gag and pooling of secretions; she cannot fully clear her throat. Neck flexor weakness (cannot lift head off the bed against gravity). Symmetric proximal limb weakness that fatigues with repetition; deep tendon reflexes present and normal; sensation intact. Shallow, rapid breathing with a weak, ineffective cough; she can count only to 12 on one breath. Paradoxical abdominal movement noted with inspiration. Lungs clear. No fever.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate monitoring to guide the need for airway intervention in this patient?
options: Continuous pulse oximetry as the primary indicator
Serial negative inspiratory force and forced vital capacity measurements
End-tidal CO2 trending alone
Hourly chest auscultation
Serial troponin measurements
stages.explanation: Myasthenic crisis is neuromuscular respiratory failure, and the most reliable bedside guide is serial negative inspiratory force (NIF) and forced vital capacity (FVC) (B). These detect declining respiratory muscle strength before hypoxia appears. As in GBS, the 20/30/40 thresholds (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30 cm H2O) signal impending failure and the need to intubate. Pulse oximetry (A) and end-tidal CO2 (C) are late indicators — oxygenation is preserved until abrupt decompensation. Auscultation (D) does not quantify muscle strength. Troponin (E) is irrelevant to the respiratory mechanics driving this crisis. Bulbar weakness with pooling secretions further raises aspiration risk.

stages.label: BEDSIDE STUDIES
stages.content:
Forced vital capacity: 13 mL/kg (was 18 mL/kg two hours ago)
Negative inspiratory force: −18 cm H2O
Arterial blood gas: pH 7.31, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 78 mmHg on room air (rising CO2)
Chest X-ray: low lung volumes, no infiltrate
Bedside ice-pack test earlier: improved ptosis
TSH normal; electrolytes normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the FVC of 13 mL/kg, NIF of −18 cm H2O, and rising PaCO2, what is the immediate next step?
options: Increase the pyridostigmine dose and reassess in two hours
Intubate and provide mechanical ventilation
Administer IV edrophonium to confirm the diagnosis
Start noninvasive ventilation and discharge planning
Give a beta-agonist nebulizer treatment
stages.explanation: The patient is in frank neuromuscular respiratory failure — FVC 13 mL/kg, NIF −18 cm H2O, rising PaCO2 with acidosis, weak cough, and pooling secretions — so the immediate step is intubation and mechanical ventilation (B). Increasing pyridostigmine (A) is dangerous now: excess cholinergic stimulation worsens secretions and does not reverse impending failure, and it complicates the picture. Edrophonium testing (C) is largely historical, risks bradycardia/secretions, and is inappropriate in a crashing patient with an established diagnosis. Noninvasive ventilation (D) may be used very early in select cooperative patients without bulbar failure, but this patient has bulbar weakness, secretions, and hypercapnia — she needs a definitive airway. Beta-agonists (E) treat bronchospasm, not muscle weakness.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor most likely precipitated this myasthenic crisis?
options: Starting azithromycin and missing pyridostigmine doses during an intercurrent infection
Her levothyroxine dose
Eating a high-protein meal
Mild dehydration
Daytime napping
stages.explanation: Myasthenic crises are commonly triggered by infection, surgery, medication noncompliance, and drugs that impair neuromuscular transmission. Here, both an offending antibiotic and missed doses contributed: macrolides (azithromycin), aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, and magnesium can all worsen myasthenia, and a concurrent respiratory infection plus missed pyridostigmine compounded the risk (A). Levothyroxine (B) at a stable dose did not precipitate this. Meals (C), mild dehydration (D), and napping (E) are not recognized crisis triggers. Recognizing and avoiding aggravating medications is a high-yield point: always check whether a newly added drug can unmask or worsen myasthenia.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After securing the airway, which disease-directed therapy is most appropriate for myasthenic crisis?
options: IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange
High-dose pyridostigmine infusion
IV magnesium sulfate
Empiric aminoglycoside antibiotics
Immediate thymectomy
stages.explanation: The rapid disease-directed treatments for myasthenic crisis are plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) or IVIG, which act within days to remove or neutralize pathogenic antibodies (A). Corticosteroids are often continued/added but can transiently worsen weakness initially, so they are managed carefully alongside IVIG/PLEX. A high-dose pyridostigmine infusion (B) is avoided in crisis because it increases secretions and can cause cholinergic complications. IV magnesium (C) impairs neuromuscular transmission and is contraindicated. Aminoglycosides (D) worsen myasthenia and should be avoided. Thymectomy (E) is a long-term management consideration (especially with thymoma) but is not the acute treatment during crisis. Treat the precipitant (stop azithromycin, treat infection) as well.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The team must distinguish a myasthenic crisis from a cholinergic crisis. Which findings would instead point toward a CHOLINERGIC crisis from excess anticholinesterase medication?
options: Dry mouth, mydriasis, and tachycardia
Excessive salivation and bronchial secretions, miosis, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, and fasciculations
Ptosis that improves with edrophonium
Isolated diplopia with no autonomic symptoms
Fever and neck stiffness
stages.explanation: A cholinergic crisis results from excess acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (too much pyridostigmine), producing muscarinic overactivity: SLUDGE-type features — excessive salivation and bronchial secretions, lacrimation, miosis, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, bradycardia — plus nicotinic fasciculations and weakness (B). Distinguishing it matters because the treatment is the opposite (hold the anticholinesterase). Dry mouth, mydriasis, and tachycardia (A) are anticholinergic, not cholinergic. Improvement with edrophonium (C) suggests undertreated myasthenia, not cholinergic excess. Isolated diplopia without autonomic features (D) is ocular myasthenia. Fever and neck stiffness (E) suggest meningitis, unrelated to either crisis. In practice, in true crisis both are managed with airway control and PLEX/IVIG.

cases.diagnosis: Myasthenic crisis (acute neuromuscular respiratory failure in myasthenia gravis)
pearls: Myasthenic crisis is neuromuscular respiratory failure — track NIF and FVC serially and intubate using the 20/30/40 rule, not pulse oximetry.
Common precipitants include infection, medication noncompliance, and offending drugs (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, magnesium).
Treat with plasma exchange or IVIG; avoid high-dose pyridostigmine in crisis (worsens secretions) and avoid magnesium and aminoglycosides.
Bulbar weakness with pooling secretions raises aspiration risk and favors definitive intubation over noninvasive ventilation.

cases.title: Back Pain and Weak Legs in a Cancer Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening mid-back pain for two weeks, now with leg weakness and trouble urinating.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer presents with two weeks of progressively severe mid-thoracic back pain that is worse at night and when lying down, and not relieved by his usual analgesics. Over the past two days he has developed bilateral leg heaviness and weakness, difficulty walking, and today noticed numbness over his lower abdomen and legs along with difficulty starting urination and a sense of incomplete emptying. He reports new constipation. He denies fever or recent trauma. PMH: prostate adenocarcinoma with known bony metastases, hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: leuprolide, oxycodone for pain, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, lives with his wife. Family history noncontributory. He is in obvious distress from pain and frightened by the leg weakness. On review, he notes the pain is band-like across his back and radiates around his torso.
cases.exam: Alert man in pain, lying still. Focal tenderness to percussion over the mid-thoracic spine. Motor: bilateral lower extremity weakness, 3/5 proximally and distally, with increased tone; upper extremities normal. Reflexes: brisk patellar and Achilles reflexes with bilateral upgoing plantar responses (Babinski). Sensory: a sensory level at approximately T10 with decreased pinprick below the umbilicus. Decreased rectal tone and a palpable distended bladder; post-void residual on bladder scan markedly elevated. Saddle sensation diminished. Gait not tested due to weakness and risk of falling. Upper extremity exam normal. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a cancer patient with back pain, progressive bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level, and urinary retention, what is the most urgent immediate action?
options: Schedule outpatient MRI within one week
Administer IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) immediately and arrange emergent MRI of the whole spine
Order a plain radiograph of the thoracic spine and discharge with analgesics
Start physical therapy and reassess in 48 hours
Obtain a bone scan before any treatment
stages.explanation: This is malignant epidural spinal cord compression, a neuro-oncologic emergency. The two immediate priorities are starting IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) to reduce vasogenic edema and protect neurologic function, and obtaining emergent MRI of the entire spine to localize the compression and plan definitive therapy (B). Outpatient or delayed imaging (A) risks irreversible paralysis. Plain films (C) are insensitive and discharge is unsafe. Physical therapy with delay (D) ignores the emergency. A bone scan (E) does not visualize cord compression and would delay care. Neurologic status at the time of treatment is the strongest predictor of outcome, so minutes matter.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding indicates the lesion is causing cord compression (an upper motor neuron pattern) rather than a peripheral nerve problem?
options: Distal sensory tingling alone
Hyperreflexia with upgoing plantar responses and a sensory level
Absent reflexes throughout
Asymmetric facial weakness
Normal rectal tone
stages.explanation: Compression of the spinal cord produces an upper motor neuron syndrome below the lesion: weakness with increased tone, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantar responses (Babinski), and a discrete sensory level — all present here (B). A purely distal sensory disturbance (A) suggests peripheral neuropathy. Diffuse areflexia (C) is seen in disorders like GBS or in acute spinal shock, not in this established compressive picture with brisk reflexes. Facial weakness (D) is unrelated to a thoracic cord lesion. Preserved rectal tone (E) would argue against significant cord/cauda involvement; this patient has reduced rectal tone and retention, consistent with cord compression and autonomic involvement.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI total spine with contrast: epidural soft-tissue mass at T9–T10 from a metastatic vertebral body lesion, causing >50% spinal canal compromise and cord compression with cord edema
Additional metastatic deposits at L2 and T4 without cord compression
WBC 8.2 ×10³/µL
Calcium 11.6 mg/dL (elevated)
PSA markedly elevated
Bladder scan: 620 mL retained urine

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After steroids and confirmatory MRI, what is the most appropriate definitive management for this single-level compression with neurologic deficit?
options: Analgesia alone with hospice referral
Urgent neurosurgical and radiation oncology consultation for decompressive surgery and/or radiotherapy
Chemotherapy as the sole modality
Repeat MRI in one week to assess progression
Bisphosphonate therapy alone
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of malignant cord compression requires urgent multidisciplinary involvement of neurosurgery and radiation oncology (B). For a good-candidate patient with a single area of compression and neurologic deficit, direct decompressive surgery followed by radiotherapy improves ambulation outcomes compared with radiotherapy alone; radiotherapy alone is used for radiosensitive tumors or poor surgical candidates. Analgesia/hospice alone (A) abandons salvageable neurologic function. Chemotherapy alone (C) acts too slowly for an acute compression. Delaying with repeat imaging (D) wastes the critical window. Bisphosphonates (E) and treating his hypercalcemia are important adjuncts but do not relieve the mechanical compression. Also place a urinary catheter for retention.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor is the strongest predictor of neurologic recovery in malignant cord compression?
options: The patient's age
Neurologic/ambulatory status at the time treatment is initiated
The serum PSA level
The number of vertebral metastases
The presence of hypercalcemia
stages.explanation: Pretreatment neurologic function — especially whether the patient is still ambulatory when treatment begins — is the single strongest predictor of post-treatment ambulation and recovery (B). This is precisely why cord compression is a true emergency: every hour of delay risks converting a walking patient into a permanently paraplegic one. Age (A) influences surgical candidacy but is not the dominant outcome predictor. PSA (C) reflects tumor burden, not neurologic prognosis. The number of metastases (D) affects overall prognosis and treatment choice but not the immediate likelihood of recovering function. Hypercalcemia (E) is a metabolic complication to treat but does not predict neurologic recovery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His serum calcium is 11.6 mg/dL with associated symptoms. What is the most appropriate initial management of the hypercalcemia of malignancy?
options: Fluid restriction and a thiazide diuretic
Aggressive isotonic IV fluids, followed by an IV bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid)
Oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Immediate hemodialysis for all patients
Loop diuretics alone without fluid resuscitation
stages.explanation: Hypercalcemia of malignancy is treated first with aggressive isotonic saline to restore volume and promote calciuresis, followed by an IV bisphosphonate such as zoledronic acid (or denosumab) to inhibit osteoclastic bone resorption; calcitonin can bridge for rapid short-term lowering (B). Fluid restriction with a thiazide (A) is exactly wrong — thiazides raise calcium and these patients are volume-depleted. Calcium/vitamin D supplementation (C) would worsen it. Routine hemodialysis (D) is reserved for severe, refractory, or renal-failure cases, not first-line for all. Loop diuretics alone without rehydration (E) cause dangerous further volume depletion; loops are only used after volume repletion, if at all.

cases.diagnosis: Malignant epidural spinal cord compression (metastatic prostate cancer at T9–T10)
pearls: New or worsening back pain in a cancer patient — especially nocturnal pain with leg weakness, a sensory level, or urinary retention — is cord compression until proven otherwise.
Give IV dexamethasone immediately and obtain emergent MRI of the whole spine; do not wait for outpatient imaging.
Cord compression produces an upper motor neuron pattern: hyperreflexia, upgoing toes, and a sensory level below the lesion.
Ambulatory/neurologic status at treatment onset is the strongest predictor of recovery — decompressive surgery plus radiotherapy beats radiotherapy alone in good surgical candidates.

cases.title: Brief Episode of One-Sided Weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: An hour of right arm and face weakness with slurred speech that has now completely resolved.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents after an episode of sudden right-sided face and arm weakness with slurred speech that began two hours ago and lasted about 60 minutes before fully resolving. His wife noticed his face drooping and that he could not hold his coffee cup; his speech was garbled. By the time they reached the ED, his symptoms had completely resolved and he feels back to normal. He denies headache, visual loss, vertigo, chest pain, or palpitations. He has never had a similar episode. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily; he is not on any antiplatelet agent. Allergies: none. Social: 40-pack-year smoking history, drinks alcohol occasionally, retired. Family history: father had a stroke. He is anxious but feels well now and wants to go home.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, well-appearing man with a normal neurologic exam at presentation: speech fluent and clear, no facial asymmetry, full and symmetric strength (5/5) in all extremities with no pronator drift, normal sensation, normal coordination and gait, intact visual fields, and normal cranial nerves. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs; no carotid bruits auscultated. Lungs clear. No signs of trauma. Fundoscopy normal. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable. His resolved deficit and current normal exam are consistent with a transient event.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most accurate way to characterize this event now that symptoms have fully resolved?
options: A completed ischemic stroke
A transient ischemic attack — a transient neurologic deficit of vascular origin without acute infarction
A complex migraine
A focal seizure with Todd paralysis
Conversion disorder
stages.explanation: A transient ischemic attack is a transient episode of focal neurologic dysfunction caused by focal brain ischemia without acute infarction; the modern tissue-based definition requires no infarct on imaging (B). His abrupt focal deficit (face/arm weakness, dysarthria) in a vasculopath that resolved within an hour fits TIA. A completed stroke (A) would leave a persistent deficit or show infarction. Migraine (C) typically has positive, spreading symptoms (e.g., scintillating scotoma) and headache, not abrupt isolated weakness in a 70-year-old. Focal seizure with Todd paralysis (D) usually follows convulsive activity. Conversion disorder (E) is a diagnosis of exclusion and inappropriate given his vascular risk profile and classic presentation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the ABCD2 score to risk-stratify, which combination of this patient's features contributes the most points?
options: Young age and brief duration
Age ≥60, blood pressure ≥140/90, unilateral weakness, duration ≥60 minutes, and diabetes
Absence of diabetes and normal blood pressure
Female sex and migraine history
Normal exam at presentation
stages.explanation: The ABCD2 score predicts short-term stroke risk after TIA: Age ≥60 (1), Blood pressure ≥140/90 (1), Clinical features — unilateral weakness (2) or speech disturbance without weakness (1), Duration ≥60 min (2) or 10–59 min (1), and Diabetes (1). This patient scores high — age ≥60, BP 168/94, unilateral weakness with speech involvement, duration ~60 minutes, and diabetes — placing him at high risk (B). Young age and short duration (A) would lower the score. Lacking diabetes/hypertension (C) is not his profile. Sex and migraine (D) are not ABCD2 components. A normal current exam (E) is expected in TIA and does not reduce his calculated risk.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage, no acute infarct
MRI brain (DWI): no acute infarction
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no atrial fibrillation
Carotid duplex ultrasound: 70% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery
Glucose 142 mg/dL, LDL 138 mg/dL
CBC, coagulation panel, electrolytes within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a high-risk TIA, what is the most appropriate antithrombotic management to start?
options: No antiplatelet therapy; observe only
Short-term dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel) started early, with high-intensity statin and risk-factor control
Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin
Thrombolysis with alteplase
Aspirin to be started in two weeks
stages.explanation: For high-risk TIA (e.g., ABCD2 ≥4) or minor stroke, early short-term dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus clopidogrel (typically for about 21–90 days, then single agent) reduces recurrent stroke, combined with a high-intensity statin and aggressive risk-factor control (B). Doing nothing (A) leaves him at high early stroke risk. Anticoagulation (C) is reserved for cardioembolic sources such as atrial fibrillation, which his ECG did not show. Thrombolysis (D) treats acute ischemic stroke with active deficits, not a resolved TIA without infarct. Delaying aspirin for two weeks (E) misses the highest-risk early window, when recurrence is most likely.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the carotid ultrasound finding, what additional intervention should be arranged?
options: No further intervention; medical therapy alone suffices
Referral for carotid revascularization (carotid endarterectomy) for symptomatic high-grade stenosis
Immediate carotid stenting regardless of symptoms
Repeat carotid ultrasound in one year only
Anticoagulation instead of any procedure
stages.explanation: He has a symptomatic, high-grade (70%) internal carotid stenosis ipsilateral to his TIA symptoms; carotid endarterectomy (preferred over stenting in most older patients) within about two weeks substantially reduces recurrent stroke risk and is indicated for symptomatic stenosis of 70–99% (B). Medical therapy alone (A) is insufficient for symptomatic high-grade stenosis. Reflexive immediate stenting regardless of symptoms (C) is not the default — endarterectomy is generally preferred and indication depends on symptomatic status. Deferring to a one-year ultrasound (D) ignores the urgent intervention window. Anticoagulation instead of revascularization (E) does not address the stenotic plaque and is not the evidence-based approach for large-artery atherosclerotic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Transient ischemic attack (high-risk, with symptomatic high-grade carotid stenosis)
pearls: TIA is a transient focal neurologic deficit of vascular origin without infarction on imaging — it is a warning of high short-term stroke risk, not a benign event.
Risk-stratify with ABCD2 (Age, Blood pressure, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes); high scores warrant expedited evaluation and admission/observation.
Start early short-term dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for high-risk TIA, plus high-intensity statin and risk-factor control; anticoagulate only for cardioembolic sources.
Symptomatic carotid stenosis of 70–99% warrants carotid endarterectomy, ideally within two weeks of the event.

cases.title: Sudden Spinning and Unsteadiness
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, constant room-spinning dizziness with nausea and difficulty walking for the past three hours.
cases.history: A 65-year-old man presents with three hours of sudden, persistent, severe room-spinning vertigo, nausea, and vomiting that came on while he was sitting still. He feels markedly unsteady and could not walk to the car without his son holding him up. He denies hearing loss or tinnitus. He reports a brief, vague headache at the back of his head at onset and mild difficulty articulating a few words that his son noticed. He has no ear pain or recent upper respiratory illness. PMH: hypertension, atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated due to a prior fall), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes. Medications: metoprolol, atorvastatin, metformin; he stopped his anticoagulant months ago. Allergies: none. Social: 30-pack-year smoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: stroke. The vertigo is continuous rather than positional, though head movement makes the nausea worse. He is anxious and pale, with persistent imbalance even when lying still.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, pale, diaphoretic man, reluctant to move his head. Nystagmus is present and notably direction-changing (beats to the right on right gaze and to the left on left gaze) and does not suppress with fixation; there is a vertical/torsional component. HINTS exam: head impulse test is NORMAL (no corrective saccade), there is direction-changing gaze-evoked nystagmus, and test of skew shows vertical ocular misalignment (positive skew deviation). He has truncal ataxia and cannot sit unsupported or stand without falling. Mild dysarthria. Finger-to-nose shows past-pointing on the left. Hearing intact bilaterally. No facial droop, limbs with full strength, normal pinprick. Cardiac rhythm irregularly irregular.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination tool is most useful at the bedside to distinguish a central from a peripheral cause of acute continuous vertigo?
options: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver
The HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew)
The Romberg test alone
Pure-tone audiometry
The Epley repositioning maneuver
stages.explanation: In a patient with the acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo with nystagmus, nausea, and gait instability), the HINTS exam — Head Impulse, Nystagmus, and Test of Skew — is the key bedside tool and, when properly performed, is more sensitive than early MRI for posterior circulation stroke (B). The Dix-Hallpike (A) and Epley maneuvers (E) are for diagnosing/treating BPPV, a positional condition, not continuous vertigo. Romberg alone (C) is nonspecific and does not separate central from peripheral causes. Audiometry (D) assesses hearing and is not the discriminating test here. HINTS applies specifically to continuous vertigo, not brief positional spells.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of HINTS findings indicates a CENTRAL (dangerous) cause such as a posterior circulation stroke?
options: Abnormal head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, no skew
Normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, and a positive test of skew
Abnormal head impulse with horizontal nystagmus that suppresses with fixation
Normal head impulse with no nystagmus
Positive Dix-Hallpike with fatigable nystagmus
stages.explanation: The central (dangerous) HINTS pattern is the opposite of what reassures: a NORMAL/negative head impulse test, DIRECTION-CHANGING (gaze-evoked) nystagmus, and a PRESENT skew deviation — remembered by INFARCT (Impulse Normal, Fast-phase Alternating, Refixation on Cover Test) (B). Any one of these three suggests a central lesion. In contrast, a peripheral vestibular neuritis gives an ABNORMAL head impulse (corrective saccade), unidirectional horizontal nystagmus that suppresses with fixation, and no skew (A, C). No nystagmus at all (D) does not fit the acute vestibular syndrome and warrants other workup. A positive Dix-Hallpike with fatigable nystagmus (E) indicates BPPV, a peripheral positional disorder.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular rate
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage; no early ischemic changes (CT is insensitive for posterior fossa ischemia)
MRI brain with DWI: acute infarct in the right cerebellum (PICA territory)
CT/MR angiography: no large-vessel occlusion amenable to thrombectomy; atherosclerotic vertebrobasilar disease
Glucose 156 mg/dL; INR 1.0 (not anticoagulated)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The non-contrast CT head is normal. What is the most appropriate next step given the central HINTS findings and clinical picture?
options: Diagnose peripheral vertigo and discharge with meclizine
Obtain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging because CT is insensitive for posterior fossa stroke
Perform the Epley maneuver and reassess
Reassure and observe without further imaging
Start IV antibiotics for labyrinthitis
stages.explanation: A normal non-contrast CT does NOT exclude posterior circulation (cerebellar/brainstem) infarction — CT misses the great majority of early posterior fossa strokes. With a central HINTS pattern, dysarthria, truncal ataxia, and atrial fibrillation, MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging is required to confirm the stroke (B). Discharging as peripheral vertigo (A) would miss a life-threatening cerebellar stroke. The Epley maneuver (C) treats BPPV and is inappropriate. Reassurance without imaging (D) is unsafe given the central findings. Antibiotics for labyrinthitis (E) are wrong — there is no infection, and labyrinthitis includes hearing loss, which he lacks. Posterior fossa strokes can rapidly cause edema and herniation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important reason to admit this patient with a cerebellar (PICA) infarct for close monitoring?
options: To titrate meclizine dosing
Risk of cerebellar swelling causing brainstem compression and obstructive hydrocephalus requiring neurosurgery
To complete outpatient vestibular rehabilitation
Because vertigo always requires a three-day admission
To repeat the HINTS exam hourly
stages.explanation: Cerebellar infarcts can develop malignant edema over the first few days, compressing the brainstem and obstructing the fourth ventricle to cause hydrocephalus — a life-threatening situation that may require suboccipital decompressive craniectomy or ventricular drainage (B). Close neuro monitoring detects this early. Titrating meclizine (A) is trivial and not the reason for admission; symptomatic vestibular suppressants are minimized in central vertigo. Outpatient rehab (C) is a later consideration. There is no rule that vertigo mandates a fixed admission length (D). Repeating HINTS hourly (E) is not the monitoring goal — serial neurologic checks for declining consciousness and new brainstem signs are. Secondary prevention (anticoagulation for AF) is also addressed during admission.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond his current deficits, which finding during monitoring would most strongly suggest evolving brainstem compression from cerebellar edema?
options: Improving nausea
Decreasing level of consciousness, new gaze palsy, or worsening respiratory drive
Resolution of the nystagmus
Mild residual unsteadiness
A transient rise in blood pressure that normalizes
stages.explanation: Evolving cerebellar edema compressing the brainstem and obstructing CSF outflow manifests as a declining level of consciousness, new cranial nerve/gaze abnormalities, and depressed respiratory drive — ominous signs requiring emergent neurosurgical decompression or ventricular drainage (B). Improving nausea (A) and resolving nystagmus (C) are reassuring, not concerning. Mild residual unsteadiness (D) is expected with a cerebellar infarct and does not by itself signal herniation. A transient, self-resolving BP rise (E) is nonspecific; it is the sustained Cushing response (hypertension with bradycardia and irregular respirations) plus declining consciousness that signals dangerous mass effect. Serial neuro checks in the first 72 hours are essential.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior circulation (cerebellar, PICA territory) ischemic stroke presenting as acute vertigo
pearls: Use HINTS only for the acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo); the dangerous central pattern is INFARCT — Impulse Normal, Fast-phase Alternating (direction-changing), Refixation on cover test (skew).
A peripheral lesion gives an abnormal head impulse with unidirectional nystagmus that suppresses with fixation and no skew — the reverse of the central pattern.
A normal non-contrast CT does not rule out posterior fossa stroke; obtain MRI with DWI when central features are present.
Cerebellar infarcts can swell and cause brainstem compression and hydrocephalus — admit for monitoring and neurosurgical readiness, and address stroke risk factors like atrial fibrillation.

cases.title: Brief Spinning Spells When Rolling Over in Bed
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent brief episodes of the room spinning whenever she turns her head or rolls over in bed.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with five days of recurrent dizziness. She describes a true spinning sensation (the room moving) that begins abruptly when she rolls over in bed, looks up to a high shelf, or tips her head back at the hair salon. Each episode lasts about 20-30 seconds and then resolves completely if she holds still. There is no spinning at rest. She reports mild nausea during the spells but no vomiting, no hearing loss, no tinnitus, no ear fullness, and no headache. She denies weakness, numbness, dysarthria, diplopia, or difficulty walking between episodes. There were no recent upper respiratory infections. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine occasionally. Family history is notable for migraine in her mother. She is anxious because a coworker recently had a stroke and she fears the same. She has not fallen.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing woman in no distress at rest. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams normal. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; extraocular movements full with no spontaneous nystagmus in primary gaze. No facial droop, dysarthria, or tongue deviation. Strength 5/5 and sensation intact in all extremities. Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal bilaterally. Romberg negative. Gait steady with normal tandem walk. Otoscopy shows normal tympanic membranes bilaterally; Weber and Rinne normal. Resting orthostatic vitals show no significant drop. No carotid bruits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and resting exam, which feature most strongly suggests a peripheral (benign) rather than central cause of vertigo?
options: Presence of nausea during the episodes
Brief, position-triggered episodes lasting under one minute with a normal neurologic exam
History of hypertension and hyperlipidemia
Anxiety about having a stroke
stages.explanation: Brief (seconds to <1 minute), positionally triggered vertigo with a completely normal interictal neurologic exam is the classic peripheral pattern of BPPV. Nausea is nonspecific and occurs with both peripheral and central vertigo, so it does not localize. Vascular risk factors raise concern for central causes but do not themselves establish or exclude one. Anxiety is understandable but is not a diagnostic feature. The combination of positional triggering, short duration, fatigability, and a normal exam is what points strongly toward a benign peripheral process and away from posterior circulation stroke, which typically produces persistent symptoms or accompanying neurologic deficits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which bedside maneuver is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: HINTS exam (head impulse, nystagmus, test of skew)
Romberg test with eyes closed
Dix-Hallpike maneuver
Valsalva maneuver
stages.explanation: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver is the diagnostic test of choice for posterior canal BPPV: the patient is rapidly moved from sitting to supine with the head turned 45 degrees and extended ~20 degrees below horizontal, provoking transient torsional-upbeat nystagmus and vertigo. The HINTS exam is validated for continuous vertigo (acute vestibular syndrome), not episodic positional vertigo, and can be misleading here. Romberg assesses proprioception/balance broadly but does not localize BPPV. Valsalva is used to evaluate perilymph fistula or superior canal dehiscence, not BPPV. Dix-Hallpike directly reproduces the symptom and identifies the affected canal.

stages.label: BEDSIDE MANEUVER FINDINGS
stages.content:
Dix-Hallpike with head turned to the RIGHT: after a 3-second latency, the patient develops vertigo and upbeating-torsional nystagmus (top poles of eyes beating toward the down/right ear).
Nystagmus fatigues within ~25 seconds and resolves.
Dix-Hallpike to the LEFT: no nystagmus, no vertigo.
No vertical skew, no gaze-evoked nystagmus, normal smooth pursuit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given right-sided posterior canal BPPV confirmed on Dix-Hallpike, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Start meclizine 25 mg three times daily for two weeks
Order MRI of the brain with MRA of the head and neck
Begin a 5-day course of oral prednisone
Perform the Epley (canalith repositioning) maneuver
stages.explanation: The Epley canalith repositioning maneuver is first-line, definitive treatment for posterior canal BPPV; it guides displaced otoconia out of the affected canal and resolves symptoms in most patients in one or two sessions. Vestibular suppressants like meclizine only blunt symptoms, are discouraged for more than a few days because they impair central compensation, and do not cure BPPV. MRI/MRA is unnecessary when the history and Dix-Hallpike are classic and the exam is otherwise normal; reserve imaging for atypical features or central red flags. Prednisone is used for vestibular neuritis, not BPPV. Repositioning addresses the underlying mechanical cause.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After a successful Epley maneuver in clinic, what is the most appropriate counseling and follow-up plan?
options: Admit for observation given the vascular risk factors
Reassure her, advise that symptoms may recur, teach home repositioning/precautions, and arrange routine follow-up; return for any new neurologic symptoms
Prescribe lifelong daily meclizine to prevent recurrence
Refer urgently to neurosurgery for canal occlusion surgery
stages.explanation: BPPV is benign and often self-limited; after successful repositioning the appropriate plan is reassurance, education that recurrence is common (and can often be re-treated), instruction in home maneuvers/positional precautions, and routine follow-up, with clear return precautions for any new persistent vertigo, focal weakness, diplopia, dysarthria, or gait instability suggesting a central process. Admission is unwarranted with a normal exam and a benign mechanical diagnosis. Chronic meclizine impairs vestibular compensation and is not preventive. Surgical canal occlusion is reserved for rare, intractable cases refractory to repeated repositioning, not first presentation.

cases.diagnosis: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (right posterior canal)
pearls: BPPV produces brief (<1 min), position-triggered vertigo with a normal interictal neurologic exam; persistent vertigo or focal deficits should prompt evaluation for a central cause.
Dix-Hallpike reproducing transient upbeating-torsional, fatigable nystagmus confirms posterior canal BPPV and identifies the affected side.
The Epley canalith repositioning maneuver is definitive first-line therapy; vestibular suppressants only mask symptoms and impair central compensation.
Use the HINTS exam for continuous (acute vestibular syndrome) vertigo, not for episodic positional vertigo.

cases.title: Unresponsive Young Woman With a Wide-Complex Rhythm
cases.chiefComplaint: Found drowsy with an empty pill bottle, then became unresponsive with seizures in the emergency department.
cases.history: A 25-year-old woman is brought in by EMS after her roommate found her lethargic next to an empty bottle of amitriptyline prescribed for chronic pain and depression. The roommate estimates the bottle held roughly thirty 75 mg tablets and was filled two days ago. The ingestion is thought to have occurred 1-2 hours before arrival. En route she was drowsy but arousable; on ED arrival she becomes increasingly obtunded. Past medical history includes major depressive disorder and migraine. Home medications are amitriptyline and an oral contraceptive. She has a prior suicide attempt by acetaminophen overdose one year ago. No known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially; the roommate is unsure whether alcohol or other pills were involved. Family history is noncontributory. Shortly after triage she has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about 60 seconds, after which she is postictal with shallow respirations. The team prepares for airway protection while obtaining IV access and a cardiac monitor.
cases.exam: Obtunded, postictal, not following commands. Pupils 6 mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally. Skin warm, dry, and flushed; mucous membranes dry. Lungs with shallow respirations and pooled secretions; no focal crackles. Tachycardic with regular rhythm, no murmur. Abdomen soft with hypoactive bowel sounds; bladder distended. No focal motor deficit appreciated. Diffusely diminished reflexes after the seizure. No signs of trauma. Foley placed drains a large volume of urine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After securing the airway and IV access, which single test will most rapidly guide management of her cardiotoxicity?
options: Serum tricyclic antidepressant drug level
12-lead ECG
CT head without contrast
Serum acetaminophen and salicylate levels
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is the fastest and most actionable test in suspected TCA toxicity: QRS widening (especially >100 ms) predicts seizures and ventricular arrhythmias, and a terminal R wave in aVR (>3 mm) supports the diagnosis—findings that immediately trigger sodium bicarbonate therapy. Quantitative TCA levels are slow to return and correlate poorly with acute toxicity, so they do not guide bedside decisions. CT head is reasonable later if the picture is unexplained, but here the anticholinergic toxidrome, seizure, and ingestion history point to TCA. Acetaminophen and salicylate co-ingestion screening is appropriate but does not address the immediate cardiotoxic threat, which the ECG identifies.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
ECG: sinus tachycardia at 128 bpm, QRS 138 ms, prominent R wave in aVR (~5 mm) with deep S in aVL, rightward terminal axis, QTc 470 ms.
pH 7.28, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 20 mEq/L (mixed acidosis)
Na 139 | K 3.6 | glucose 104 mg/dL
Acetaminophen <10 µg/mL, salicylate <5 mg/dL, ethanol negative
CK 180 U/L

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given QRS 138 ms with a prominent terminal R in aVR and hypotension, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: IV sodium bicarbonate bolus
IV amiodarone for the wide-complex rhythm
IV physostigmine to reverse anticholinergic effects
Oral activated charcoal via the now-protected airway, then observe
stages.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is the cornerstone of TCA cardiotoxicity management. TCAs block fast sodium channels (the mechanism behind QRS widening and ventricular arrhythmias); bicarbonate both raises serum pH (increasing protein binding of drug) and provides a sodium load that overcomes channel blockade, narrowing the QRS and supporting blood pressure. Amiodarone and other class I/III antiarrhythmics can worsen sodium-channel blockade and are contraindicated. Physostigmine can precipitate asystole and seizures in TCA poisoning and is contraindicated. Charcoal may be considered for a recent ingestion once the airway is secured, but it does not treat existing cardiotoxicity and is secondary to bicarbonate when QRS is already wide.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After two bicarbonate boluses the QRS narrows to 110 ms. What is the best strategy to sustain the benefit and monitor her?
options: Switch to a continuous sodium bicarbonate infusion targeting serum pH ~7.50-7.55 with frequent ECG and potassium monitoring
Begin a phenytoin infusion to prevent further seizures
Start a norepinephrine infusion and stop all bicarbonate
Administer IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium
stages.explanation: After bolus-driven improvement, a continuous bicarbonate infusion maintains serum alkalinization (target pH ~7.50-7.55) to keep sodium channels open while the drug redistributes and is metabolized; serial ECGs track QRS, and potassium must be followed because alkalinization drives hypokalemia. Phenytoin is ineffective and potentially harmful for TCA seizures—benzodiazepines are the agents of choice. Vasopressors (norepinephrine) are added for refractory hypotension but should supplement, not replace, bicarbonate, which directly counters the sodium-channel blockade. Calcium stabilizes the myocardium in hyperkalemia or calcium-channel-blocker toxicity, not TCA-induced sodium-channel blockade, and is not indicated here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite a bicarbonate infusion she develops recurrent ventricular tachycardia with hypotension. Besides ongoing alkalinization, which adjunct is most appropriate?
options: IV procainamide
Additional sodium bicarbonate boluses, consider hypertonic saline, and lidocaine if arrhythmia persists
Flumazenil to reverse possible benzodiazepine effect
Immediate hemodialysis to remove the drug
stages.explanation: For refractory ventricular arrhythmias in TCA toxicity, escalate sodium loading with additional bicarbonate boluses (and hypertonic saline if needed), and use lidocaine—a class Ib agent with rapid off-kinetics that competes less detrimentally at the sodium channel—as the preferred antiarrhythmic. Procainamide is a class Ia sodium-channel blocker and is contraindicated because it compounds the toxic mechanism. Flumazenil is dangerous in mixed/unknown overdose and can precipitate seizures, especially with TCAs. Hemodialysis does not effectively remove TCAs because they are large, highly protein-bound, and have a large volume of distribution; it is not a rescue for cardiotoxicity. Refractory cases may warrant lipid emulsion or ECMO.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After her QRS normalizes and arrhythmias resolve on a bicarbonate infusion, what is the most appropriate disposition and observation plan?
options: Discharge home once awake since the level was never very high
Transfer directly to an unmonitored psychiatric unit as soon as she is alert
Admit to the ICU on continuous cardiac monitoring until she is asymptomatic with a normal QRS off bicarbonate for an adequate period, with psychiatric evaluation before discharge
Observe 4 hours in the ED then discharge with cardiology follow-up
stages.explanation: TCA overdose warrants ICU admission with continuous cardiac monitoring because cardiotoxicity and seizures can recur as drug redistributes; patients should be observed until they are asymptomatic with a normal mental status and a persistently normal QRS off bicarbonate for an adequate period, and—because this was an intentional ingestion—formal psychiatric evaluation must occur before discharge. Discharging an awake patient ignores the risk of delayed deterioration and the suicide attempt. Transfer to an unmonitored psychiatric unit while cardiotoxicity risk persists is unsafe; medical clearance and a monitoring period come first. A brief 4-hour ED observation is inadequate for a serious TCA ingestion with documented QRS widening and arrhythmia.

cases.diagnosis: Tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline) overdose with cardiotoxicity and seizures
pearls: QRS >100 ms and a terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm predict seizures and ventricular arrhythmias in TCA overdose and should trigger sodium bicarbonate.
Sodium bicarbonate treats TCA cardiotoxicity by alkalinization plus a sodium load that overcomes fast-sodium-channel blockade; maintain pH ~7.50-7.55 and watch potassium.
Avoid class Ia/Ic and III antiarrhythmics, physostigmine, and flumazenil; benzodiazepines (not phenytoin) treat TCA seizures.
Hemodialysis does not remove TCAs (large Vd, highly protein-bound); refractory toxicity may need lipid emulsion or ECMO.

cases.title: Ringing Ears, Fast Breathing, and Confusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive nausea, ringing in the ears, rapid breathing, and confusion over several hours.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man is brought to the ED by his wife for several hours of worsening nausea, vomiting, ringing in both ears, and rapid breathing. Earlier in the day he complained of a severe tension headache and, according to his wife, took 'a lot' of aspirin over the afternoon to control the pain; she found a nearly empty 325 mg aspirin bottle and a bottle of an oil of wintergreen rub in the bathroom. Over the last hour he has become agitated, sweaty, and intermittently confused. He denies chest pain or hematemesis. Past medical history includes chronic low back pain and anxiety. Medications are as-needed aspirin and occasional ibuprofen. No known allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no illicit drugs per his wife. Family history is noncontributory. There is no fever history, no neck stiffness, and no recent travel or sick contacts. The picture of tinnitus, hyperpnea, vomiting, and altered mental status after a large salicylate exposure prompts urgent laboratory and blood gas evaluation.
cases.exam: Diaphoretic, restless man who is alert but disoriented to date and is mildly agitated. Breathing is deep and rapid (Kussmaul-like) without respiratory distress or accessory muscle use. Lungs clear bilaterally. Tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft with mild epigastric tenderness; no rebound. Neurologic exam without focal deficit; he reports bilateral tinnitus and difficulty hearing. Skin warm and sweaty. No rash, no neck rigidity, no papilledema. Mucous membranes dry.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of initial tests will best characterize the suspected toxicity?
options: Arterial blood gas with serum electrolytes and a serum salicylate level
CT head and lumbar puncture
Serum lactate alone
Chest X-ray and D-dimer
stages.explanation: Salicylate toxicity classically produces a mixed acid-base disturbance, so an arterial blood gas plus electrolytes (to compute the anion gap) and a quantitative salicylate level are the key initial studies; serial salicylate levels also track whether the burden is rising. CT head and LP would be appropriate if meningitis or intracranial hemorrhage were the leading concern, but the tinnitus, hyperpnea, and vomiting after a large aspirin exposure point to salicylates. Lactate alone is nonspecific and incomplete. Chest X-ray/D-dimer target pulmonary embolism, which does not explain the tinnitus or toxidrome. The ABG-electrolyte-level triad directly establishes severity.

stages.label: LABS & BLOOD GAS
stages.content:
ABG: pH 7.46 | pCO2 22 mmHg | HCO3 15 mEq/L
Na 140 | K 3.4 | Cl 101 | HCO3 15 → anion gap 24
Glucose 70 mg/dL | BUN 18 | Cr 1.1 mg/dL
Lactate 3.2 mmol/L
Salicylate level 62 mg/dL (drawn ~4 h post-ingestion, rising on repeat)
Acetaminophen <10 µg/mL | INR 1.1 | urine pH 5.5

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this patient's acid-base disturbance best characterized?
options: Pure respiratory alkalosis
Pure high anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Mixed primary respiratory alkalosis and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation
stages.explanation: The hallmark of salicylate toxicity in adults is a mixed disorder: salicylate directly stimulates the medullary respiratory center causing a primary respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2), while uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation and accumulation of organic acids produce a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Here pH is near-normal but pCO2 is markedly low and the anion gap is elevated at 24—each pointing to a separate primary process rather than simple compensation. A pure respiratory alkalosis would not have an elevated anion gap; a pure metabolic acidosis would not show such profound hypocapnia. Metabolic alkalosis is excluded by the low bicarbonate. Recognizing the mixed pattern is diagnostic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In addition to IV fluids and glucose, what is the most appropriate next therapeutic step?
options: Administer oral activated charcoal and start a sodium bicarbonate infusion to alkalinize the urine, supplementing potassium
Intubate and hyperventilate to lower the pCO2 further
Give acetazolamide to enhance bicarbonate excretion
Begin IV normal saline only and recheck the level in 6 hours
stages.explanation: Treatment combines GI decontamination (activated charcoal if airway is protected and ingestion is recent/ongoing) with serum and urine alkalinization using a sodium bicarbonate infusion, which 'ion-traps' salicylate in the blood and urine to enhance elimination; potassium must be repleted because hypokalemia prevents effective urinary alkalinization. Intubating a spontaneously hyperventilating salicylate patient is hazardous—if minute ventilation falls, pH drops and central nervous system salicylate uptake rises, worsening toxicity; it should be avoided unless truly necessary and then with aggressive ventilation. Acetazolamide worsens acidemia and is contraindicated. Saline alone without alkalinization or monitoring is inadequate for a rising level of 62 mg/dL.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: While alkalinization continues, his bedside glucose is 68 mg/dL but he remains confused. Beyond following serum glucose, what intervention is most appropriate?
options: Restrict all dextrose to avoid worsening cerebral edema
Provide supplemental dextrose to maintain neuroglycopenic CNS glucose even when serum glucose is normal-low, and continue close neurologic monitoring
Give a bolus of insulin to drive salicylate into cells
Withhold fluids and recheck glucose in 6 hours
stages.explanation: In salicylate toxicity, CNS glucose can be low despite a normal or borderline serum glucose because salicylate increases cerebral glucose utilization (neuroglycopenia); supplemental dextrose is therefore recommended for altered mental status even when the serum value is not frankly hypoglycemic, alongside continued neurologic monitoring. Restricting dextrose would worsen CNS energy deprivation. Insulin has no role in salicylate elimination and would dangerously lower glucose. Withholding fluids and delaying reassessment ignores ongoing CNS toxicity and the risk of cerebral edema in severe poisoning. Maintaining CNS glucose supply while alkalinization and elimination strategies proceed protects the brain.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His salicylate level rises to 95 mg/dL and he becomes more confused despite alkalinization. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue bicarbonate and reassess in the morning
Arrange urgent hemodialysis
Add a second dose of activated charcoal and observe
Start IV lipid emulsion therapy
stages.explanation: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning include altered mental status (CNS toxicity), a level above roughly 90-100 mg/dL acutely (or >40 in chronic), worsening clinical status despite alkalinization, severe acidemia, renal failure, pulmonary or cerebral edema, and an inability to tolerate fluids/bicarbonate. This patient has a rising level near 100 mg/dL plus progressive neurologic deterioration—clear dialysis criteria—so urgent hemodialysis is indicated to rapidly remove salicylate and correct the acid-base derangement. Continuing bicarbonate alone ignores deterioration. Repeat charcoal does not remove drug already absorbed and will not reverse CNS toxicity. Lipid emulsion has no established role in salicylate poisoning.

cases.diagnosis: Acute salicylate toxicity with mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis
pearls: Suspect salicylate toxicity with the triad of tinnitus, hyperpnea/respiratory alkalosis, and a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis; the mixed acid-base picture is classic in adults.
Treat with serum and urine alkalinization (sodium bicarbonate) plus potassium repletion—hypokalemia blocks effective urinary alkalinization.
Avoid intubation when possible; iatrogenic hypoventilation drops pH and drives salicylate into the CNS, worsening toxicity.
Hemodialysis is indicated for altered mental status, levels ~90-100 mg/dL (acute), refractory acidosis, renal failure, pulmonary/cerebral edema, or clinical deterioration.

cases.title: Crushing Fatigue and a Dangerously Slow Pulse
cases.chiefComplaint: Profound weakness, dizziness, and a very slow heart rate after an intentional overdose.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man is transferred to the ICU after presenting to an outside ED with profound weakness and near-syncope. His wife reports he has been despondent over financial stress and admitted to taking 'a handful' of his blood pressure pills about three hours ago. His prescriptions include metoprolol tartrate 100 mg twice daily and amlodipine, and the pharmacy confirms a recent refill of diltiazem extended-release that was also in the medicine cabinet. The exact quantities are unknown but several bottles appear depleted. Past medical history includes hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and type 2 diabetes. Medications also include metformin. No known allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not use illicit drugs. Family history includes coronary artery disease. In the outside ED he received atropine 1 mg twice and a 1 L saline bolus with minimal response; he remains bradycardic and hypotensive on arrival to your unit, prompting central access and a structured toxicologic resuscitation. He is drowsy but answers simple questions.
cases.exam: Drowsy but rousable, oriented to person and place. Skin cool with delayed capillary refill (~4 seconds) and weak peripheral pulses. Bradycardic, regular rhythm, no murmur; jugular venous pressure not elevated. Lungs clear without crackles. Abdomen soft, nontender, normoactive bowel sounds. No focal neurologic deficit; pupils midsize and reactive. Extremities cool without edema. Mentation waxes with positioning, suggesting hypoperfusion. No signs of trauma. Point-of-care glucose 168 mg/dL.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After atropine and fluids have failed, which feature most helps distinguish calcium channel blocker from beta-blocker toxicity at the bedside?
options: The presence of bradycardia
Relative hyperglycemia, which is characteristic of calcium channel blocker toxicity
Hypotension
Cool, clammy skin
stages.explanation: Both beta-blocker and calcium channel blocker overdoses cause bradycardia, hypotension, and hypoperfusion, so those features do not discriminate. A useful clue is glucose: non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil) impair pancreatic insulin release and cause insulin resistance, producing relative hyperglycemia, whereas beta-blocker toxicity tends toward normal or low glucose. This patient's glucose of 168 mg/dL despite stress supports a significant CCB contribution. That said, he has co-ingested both classes, and the distinction matters less than recognizing combined cardiotoxicity—but hyperglycemia is the classic discriminating laboratory finding and also foreshadows the rationale for high-dose insulin therapy.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
ECG: junctional bradycardia ~38 bpm, PR prolonged, narrow QRS, no ischemic ST changes
Na 138 | K 4.2 | HCO3 18 | Cr 1.3 mg/dL
Glucose 178 mg/dL
Lactate 4.6 mmol/L
iCal 1.18 mmol/L
Troponin mildly elevated; bedside echo: globally reduced contractility, no pericardial effusion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which initial pharmacologic therapy is most appropriate for this refractory bradycardia and shock while preparing further treatment?
options: IV calcium (calcium gluconate or chloride) and start glucagon
IV adenosine
Synchronized cardioversion
IV digoxin to improve contractility
