Lungs clear. Neuro: oriented x3, cranial nerves intact, mild patchy paresthesias in the right leg. Cervical spine nontender. Tympanic membranes intact. No obvious large skin burn despite the mechanism.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the small entry/exit burns but a high-voltage 'arm-to-leg' current path, what principle should guide your concern?
options: Skin burn size accurately reflects the extent of internal injury
High-voltage injury can cause extensive deep tissue and muscle damage despite minimal cutaneous findings
If the skin looks fine, no further workup is needed
Electrical injury rarely affects muscle or kidneys
stages.explanation: In high-voltage electrical injury, current travels through deep tissues and bone, generating heat that destroys muscle, nerve, and vessels while sparing much of the skin—so cutaneous burns dramatically underestimate internal injury. The visible wounds here are small, but the arm-to-leg path crosses the thorax and a large muscle mass, raising concern for deep necrosis, rhabdomyolysis, compartment syndrome, and cardiac injury. Believing skin reflects internal damage (A) or that normal skin means no workup (C) is dangerous. The claim that electrical injury rarely affects muscle/kidney (D) is false—rhabdomyolysis and myoglobinuric AKI are hallmark complications.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What cardiac evaluation is most appropriate immediately for this high-voltage injury with transient palpitations and LOC?
options: No cardiac evaluation is needed since he is asymptomatic now
12-lead ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring; check for arrhythmia/ischemia
Immediate coronary angiography
Echocardiogram only, no ECG
stages.explanation: Electrical injury can cause dysrhythmias (including VF at the time of shock), conduction abnormalities, and myocardial injury. An immediate 12-lead ECG plus continuous cardiac monitoring is the appropriate evaluation, particularly with transient palpitations, LOC, and a transthoracic current path. Doing nothing (A) is unsafe given his symptoms and mechanism. Coronary angiography (C) is not first-line; electrical cardiac injury is usually not coronary thrombosis. An echocardiogram alone (D) misses arrhythmias and conduction changes that the ECG/monitor detect; echo may be added if hemodynamic compromise or suspected myocardial dysfunction. Patients with significant injury, abnormal ECG, or LOC warrant monitoring.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CK 18,500 U/L
K 5.6 mEq/L
Cr 1.9 mg/dL
Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L
Urinalysis: dark, tea-colored; large blood on dipstick, 1 RBC/hpf
ECG: sinus tachycardia, nonspecific ST changes, normal QTc
Left wrist X-ray: nondisplaced distal radius fracture
Right lower leg: compartments firm clinically

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With CK 18,500 U/L, tea-colored urine, and rising creatinine, what is the cornerstone of management to protect the kidneys?
options: Fluid restriction to avoid pulmonary edema
Aggressive IV crystalloid to maintain brisk urine output (target ~1–2 mL/kg/hr or higher)
Immediate hemodialysis for all patients with elevated CK
Loop diuretics as first-line to force diuresis
stages.explanation: Myoglobinuric AKI from rhabdomyolysis is managed primarily with aggressive isotonic IV fluids to restore volume and maintain brisk urine output (often targeted to ~1–2 mL/kg/hr or higher), diluting and clearing nephrotoxic myoglobin. Fluid restriction (A) is dangerous and accelerates renal failure. Immediate dialysis for all elevated CK (C) is not indicated; dialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or established failure with volume overload. Loop diuretics as first-line (D) are not recommended—volume repletion comes first, and diuretics can worsen volume depletion. Hyperkalemia here also needs urgent treatment given the ECG and renal impairment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The right calf is increasingly tense with worsening pain on passive stretch and new paresthesias despite analgesia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Apply ice and elevate, then recheck in 6 hours
Urgent surgical evaluation for fasciotomy for suspected compartment syndrome
Obtain an MRI of the leg before any intervention
Reassure the patient; deep muscle damage from electricity does not cause compartment syndrome
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch, a tense compartment, and paresthesias are classic for compartment syndrome, which is common after high-voltage injury because of deep muscle necrosis and edema. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent evaluation and fasciotomy; delay risks irreversible muscle and nerve loss. Ice/elevation with delayed recheck (A) wastes critical time and elevation can reduce perfusion pressure. Waiting for MRI (C) is inappropriate when the clinical diagnosis is evident—measure compartment pressures if needed but do not delay surgery for advanced imaging. Denying that electrical injury causes compartment syndrome (D) is false; it is a well-known complication.

cases.diagnosis: High-voltage electrical injury with deep tissue damage, rhabdomyolysis, and lower-extremity compartment syndrome
pearls: In high-voltage injury, skin burns grossly underestimate deep muscle, nerve, and vessel damage along the current path.
Obtain an ECG and cardiac monitoring—electrical injury can cause arrhythmias, including VF at the moment of shock.
Rhabdomyolysis is common; treat with aggressive crystalloid to keep urine output brisk and watch potassium.
Suspect compartment syndrome with deep electrical burns—pain on passive stretch and a tense compartment warrant urgent fasciotomy.

cases.title: Dark Urine After Leg Day
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe thigh pain and cola-colored urine two days after an intense first workout.
cases.history: A 21-year-old college student presents with severe bilateral thigh and buttock pain and dark, cola-colored urine. Two days ago he attended his first high-intensity 'leg day' bootcamp class, doing hundreds of squats and lunges to failure. The next morning his thighs were extremely sore and progressively stiff and swollen; today he noticed his urine turned brown. He has had decreased oral intake because of nausea and has urinated less than usual. He is otherwise healthy and not a regular exerciser. Medications: occasional creatine and a pre-workout supplement; no other drugs. He denies anabolic steroids, statins, alcohol binge, or recreational drugs. No recent illness, trauma, or seizures. No known drug allergies. Family history: no muscle disorders or known metabolic myopathy. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. He drinks energy drinks. He reports no fevers, rash, or recent prolonged immobilization, and there is no history of statin use or hereditary muscle disease in the family.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable young man holding his thighs still. Quadriceps and gluteal muscles are markedly tender, firm, and mildly swollen bilaterally with painful active and passive movement; he walks with a stiff, antalgic gait. Distal pulses intact; no tense compartments and no pain wildly out of proportion to exam at rest, no paresthesias. Skin without rash or trauma. Cardiac regular, lungs clear. Abdomen soft, mild diffuse discomfort. Neuro grossly intact with full strength limited by pain. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No flank tenderness to suggest pyelonephritis; urine grossly brown.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which single laboratory test best confirms the suspected diagnosis?
options: Serum myoglobin level
Serum creatine kinase (CK)
Urine culture
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)
stages.explanation: Serum creatine kinase (CK) is the most sensitive and reliable test to confirm rhabdomyolysis; levels are markedly elevated (often >5× the upper limit, frequently in the tens of thousands) and CK is more stable and standardized than serum myoglobin. Serum myoglobin (A) rises and clears rapidly, making it less reliable for diagnosis and monitoring. Urine culture (C) evaluates infection, which is not the issue—the brown urine here reflects myoglobin, not pyuria. ESR (D) is a nonspecific inflammatory marker and does not diagnose muscle breakdown. The clinical triad of myalgia, weakness, and dark urine plus a high CK confirms rhabdomyolysis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CK 42,000 U/L
K 5.4 mEq/L
Ca 7.8 mg/dL
Phosphate 5.9 mg/dL
Cr 1.6 mg/dL (no known baseline)
Bicarbonate 19 mEq/L
Urinalysis: brown urine, large blood on dipstick, 0–2 RBC/hpf
BUN 24 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With CK 42,000 U/L and early AKI, what is the most important initial treatment?
options: Oral hydration and discharge with rest
Aggressive IV isotonic crystalloid to establish and maintain brisk urine output
Immediate hemodialysis
IV calcium gluconate to correct the mild hypocalcemia
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management is early, aggressive IV isotonic crystalloid (e.g., normal saline) to restore intravascular volume and maintain brisk urine output, which dilutes and flushes nephrotoxic myoglobin and prevents/limits AKI. Fluid rates are titrated to urine output (commonly targeting ~200–300 mL/hr in adults) with close monitoring. Oral hydration and discharge (A) are inadequate for this severity with AKI. Immediate dialysis (C) is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or established failure with volume overload. Treating mild hypocalcemia (D) is generally avoided unless symptomatic or with severe hyperkalemia—calcium can precipitate during the later hypercalcemic phase and worsen tissue calcification.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which electrolyte abnormality is the most immediately life-threatening in rhabdomyolysis and must be monitored closely?
options: Hypocalcemia
Hyperphosphatemia
Hyperkalemia
Hyponatremia
stages.explanation: Hyperkalemia is the most immediately life-threatening derangement in rhabdomyolysis because injured muscle releases large amounts of potassium, and concurrent AKI impairs excretion—this can precipitate fatal arrhythmias. It requires frequent monitoring, ECG surveillance, and prompt treatment (calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin/glucose, etc.). Hypocalcemia (A) is common early (calcium deposits in damaged muscle) but is usually not treated unless symptomatic or with severe hyperkalemia. Hyperphosphatemia (B) occurs but is rarely immediately lethal. Hyponatremia (D) is not a defining electrolyte issue here. Continuous attention to potassium and the ECG is essential during resuscitation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient with CK 42,000 U/L, K 5.4 mEq/L, and Cr 1.6 mg/dL?
options: Discharge home with oral fluids and follow-up labs in a week
Admit for IV fluids, serial CK and electrolyte monitoring, and renal function surveillance
Refer to outpatient physical therapy
Discharge after a single liter of saline if he feels better
stages.explanation: Markedly elevated CK with early AKI and hyperkalemia mandates admission for continuous IV fluids titrated to urine output, serial CK and electrolyte monitoring (especially potassium), and renal surveillance until trends improve. Outpatient management (A) or discharge after a single bolus (D) is unsafe given the risk of progressive AKI and dangerous hyperkalemia. Physical therapy referral (C) ignores the acute metabolic emergency. Patients can require several days of inpatient therapy; severe or refractory cases may need nephrology and dialysis. Watch for compartment syndrome as the muscles swell, particularly with very high CK.

cases.diagnosis: Exertional rhabdomyolysis with early acute kidney injury and hyperkalemia
pearls: CK is the confirmatory test for rhabdomyolysis (more reliable than serum myoglobin) and tracks the course.
Aggressive IV isotonic fluids titrated to brisk urine output are the cornerstone to prevent myoglobinuric AKI.
Hyperkalemia is the most immediately life-threatening complication—monitor potassium and the ECG closely.
Avoid early calcium for asymptomatic hypocalcemia; calcium can deposit and rebound hypercalcemia occurs in recovery.

cases.title: Bitten on the Trail
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive painful swelling of the hand after a snake bite while hiking.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man was bitten on the right hand by a snake he disturbed while moving rocks on a desert hiking trail about 90 minutes ago. He describes the snake as having a triangular head and a rattle. Two puncture marks appeared at the base of the thumb, with immediate burning pain and rapidly progressive swelling now extending toward the wrist and forearm. He feels lightheaded and nauseated and reports a metallic taste and tingling around the mouth. Past medical history: hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily. No known drug allergies; he has never received antivenom before. He is a nonsmoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. He is up to date on tetanus. Family history non-contributory. He has had no previous snake bites. He denies blood thinners. Bystanders applied a tight 'tourniquet' above the elbow and tried to cut and suck the wound before EMS arrived and loosened the constriction. No prior reactions to medications or antivenoms are known.
cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic man. Right hand with two fang puncture wounds at the thenar region, oozing serosanguineous fluid; tense edema and ecchymosis extending to the mid-forearm with early blistering. Marked tenderness; distal capillary refill 2–3 seconds, radial pulse present, fingers move but painfully. Mild oral/perioral paresthesias and fasciculations noted. No stridor, no diffuse urticaria. Cardiac tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft. No active gross bleeding elsewhere, though gums ooze slightly when probed. A leading edge of swelling is marked with a pen and the time noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial first-aid measure was harmful and should be avoided in pit-viper (crotalid) envenomation?
options: Removing rings and constrictive items from the limb
Tight arterial tourniquet plus incision and oral suction of the wound
Immobilizing the limb and keeping it at roughly heart level
Marking the leading edge of swelling and timing its progression
stages.explanation: Tight arterial tourniquets and the 'cut and suck' technique are harmful in crotalid envenomation: tourniquets concentrate venom and worsen local tissue necrosis and ischemia, while incision/suction increases tissue damage and infection without removing meaningful venom. Recommended measures include removing constrictive items (A) before swelling worsens, gently immobilizing the limb near heart level (C), and marking/timing the advancing edge of swelling (D) to track progression. Definitive treatment is antivenom for progressive or systemic envenomation. The bystander tourniquet here should be removed cautiously, anticipating a possible surge of venom effect as circulation is restored.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Platelets 78 ×10³/µL (and falling on repeat)
Fibrinogen 90 mg/dL (low)
PT 18 s / INR 1.7
aPTT 42 s
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
CK 240 U/L
Cr 1.0 mg/dL
Limb: swelling now advanced ~5 cm in 1 hour per skin marking

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given progressive swelling, systemic symptoms, thrombocytopenia, low fibrinogen, and prolonged coagulation times, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Prophylactic antibiotics and observation alone
Crotalidae polyvalent immune Fab (CroFab) antivenom, titrated to control of envenomation
Immediate fasciotomy of the hand and forearm
Platelet and fresh frozen plasma transfusion as the primary therapy
stages.explanation: Crotalid antivenom (e.g., CroFab or F(ab')2 antivenom) is the definitive treatment for envenomation with progressive local swelling, systemic symptoms, or hematologic toxicity (thrombocytopenia, hypofibrinogenemia, coagulopathy). It is given as an initial loading dose and repeated until 'initial control' (arrest of progression), then maintenance dosing per protocol. Antibiotics/observation alone (A) ignore active venom toxicity. Fasciotomy (C) is rarely needed; true compartment syndrome is uncommon and antivenom usually addresses the swelling—operating prematurely worsens outcomes. Blood products (D) treat the consequences but do not neutralize venom; the coagulopathy is best corrected by antivenom, with products reserved for active bleeding or severe deficits refractory to antivenom.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the appropriate response if signs of envenomation continue to progress after the initial antivenom dose?
options: Withhold further antivenom because the first dose failed
Administer additional antivenom doses until control of envenomation ('initial control') is achieved, then maintenance dosing
Switch to high-dose corticosteroids as primary therapy
Proceed directly to amputation
stages.explanation: Crotalid antivenom is titrated to effect: if swelling, systemic toxicity, or coagulopathy continue to progress after the initial loading dose, additional doses are given until 'initial control' (cessation of progression) is reached, followed by scheduled maintenance dosing to prevent recurrence. Withholding antivenom (A) allows ongoing tissue and hematologic injury. Corticosteroids (C) are not primary therapy for envenomation (they may treat allergic reactions to antivenom or serum sickness). Amputation (D) is not indicated for venom-mediated swelling. Monitor for recurrent coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia after discharge, as late recurrence can occur and may require repeat dosing or outpatient lab follow-up.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After achieving control with antivenom, what monitoring is most important before and after discharge?
options: No follow-up labs are needed once swelling stops
Serial CBC and coagulation studies (platelets, fibrinogen, PT/INR) to detect recurrent or late coagulopathy
Daily wound cultures for two weeks
Routine serial echocardiograms
stages.explanation: Crotalid envenomation can cause recurrent or delayed coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia days after apparent control, so serial CBC and coagulation studies (platelets, fibrinogen, PT/INR) before and after discharge are essential, with outpatient follow-up labs to catch late recurrence and decide on re-dosing. Stopping all monitoring (A) risks missing dangerous late bleeding. Daily wound cultures (C) are unnecessary absent infection. Serial echocardiograms (D) are not indicated for envenomation. Patients should also be counseled on bleeding precautions (avoid contact sports, NSAIDs) until labs normalize, and watch for serum sickness 1–2 weeks after antivenom.

cases.diagnosis: Crotalid (pit-viper/rattlesnake) envenomation with progressive swelling and venom-induced coagulopathy
pearls: Avoid tourniquets and 'cut and suck'—they worsen local injury; remove constrictive items and mark/time the swelling edge.
Antivenom (CroFab/F(ab')2) is definitive; titrate doses until 'initial control,' then give scheduled maintenance.
Venom-induced coagulopathy (low platelets, low fibrinogen, high INR) is treated by antivenom—blood products only for active bleeding.
Recurrent/late coagulopathy is common; arrange serial CBC and coagulation studies after discharge.

cases.title: Cramping Belly After a Garage Sting
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe generalized abdominal and muscle cramping after a spider bite in the garage.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents with severe, escalating muscle cramping that began about an hour after she felt a sharp pinprick on her left hand while reaching into a cluttered, dimly lit garage. She recalls a small dark spider but did not see clear markings. Over the next hour she developed intense cramping spreading from her hand to her back, chest, and abdomen, with profuse sweating, nausea, and a headache. The abdominal pain is now severe with a rigid abdomen. She has no surgical history and her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. Past medical history: migraines. Medications: oral contraceptive pill. No known drug allergies. She is a nonsmoker, drinks alcohol rarely, no drug use. Family history non-contributory. She has never been pregnant. She reports no fever, no diarrhea, no urinary symptoms, and no vaginal bleeding. The bite site itself is only mildly uncomfortable, which surprised her given how sick she feels overall right now.
cases.exam: Diaphoretic, restless woman in obvious pain. Left hand with a small erythematous papule and faint surrounding erythema; mild local tenderness only. Abdomen is diffusely rigid and board-like with generalized tenderness but NO rebound and NO localized peritoneal point; bowel sounds present. She has periodic involuntary muscle spasms of the trunk and limbs and a grimacing, sweaty face. Cardiac tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. No urticaria, no stridor, no wheeze. Neuro nonfocal. Pelvic exam (given LMP) unremarkable; no cervical motion tenderness. The systemic findings are markedly out of proportion to the minor bite.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse muscle cramps but a minor bite and no true peritoneal signs most likely represents which envenomation?
options: Brown recluse (loxoscelism) envenomation
Black widow (latrodectism) envenomation
Scorpion sting with cranial nerve dysfunction
Bee sting anaphylaxis
stages.explanation: Black widow (Latrodectus) envenomation classically causes latrodectism: severe, generalized muscle cramping and rigidity—often producing a board-like abdomen that can mimic an acute surgical abdomen—plus diaphoresis, hypertension, tachycardia, and pain out of proportion to a trivial-appearing bite. Alpha-latrotoxin triggers massive neurotransmitter release. Brown recluse (A) causes local necrotic skin lesions (and rarely systemic hemolysis), not diffuse cramping rigidity. Scorpion envenomation (C) (in the U.S., Centruroides) causes neuromuscular hyperactivity and cranial nerve/eye findings, more common in children, not this abdominal-rigidity picture. Bee-sting anaphylaxis (D) presents with urticaria, bronchospasm, and hypotension—absent here. The dissociation between a minor bite and severe systemic symptoms is the key clue.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before attributing the rigid abdomen to envenomation, what is essential in this woman of reproductive age?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy
Exclude other causes of an acute abdomen (e.g., pregnancy/ectopic, surgical pathology) with a pregnancy test and clinical assessment
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed peritonitis
Give oral contrast and obtain an upper GI series
stages.explanation: Latrodectism can convincingly mimic an acute surgical abdomen, so it is essential to exclude dangerous alternatives—most importantly pregnancy/ectopic in a woman of reproductive age—with a urine/serum hCG and a careful clinical assessment before attributing rigidity to envenomation. Rushing to laparotomy (A) on a soft-but-rigid abdomen without true peritoneal signs risks an unnecessary operation. Empiric antibiotics for peritonitis (C) treat a diagnosis not established here. An upper GI series (D) is not the appropriate workup for an acute abdomen and delays care. Recognizing latrodectism (board-like abdomen without true rebound, plus diaphoresis and a minor bite) prevents a needless surgical exploration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate first-line management for moderate-to-severe latrodectism symptoms?
options: Antivenom as the sole therapy for all patients regardless of severity
Aggressive analgesia (opioids) and benzodiazepines for muscle spasms, with antivenom reserved for severe/refractory cases
Calcium gluconate infusion, which reliably relieves the cramps
Surgical excision of the bite site
stages.explanation: Most latrodectism is managed supportively with aggressive pain control (opioids) and benzodiazepines for muscle spasms; these relieve the dominant symptoms of cramping and pain. Latrodectus antivenom is effective and reserved for severe or refractory cases (uncontrolled pain, severe systemic symptoms, pregnancy with complications) because it carries a risk of hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis and serum sickness—so it is used selectively, not for everyone (A). IV calcium (C) was historically used but is unreliable and not recommended as standard therapy. Surgical excision (D) is not indicated—black widow bites are not locally necrotic. Antihypertensives may be needed if blood pressure remains markedly elevated despite analgesia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her pregnancy test is negative and her pain improves substantially with opioids and a benzodiazepine. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Immediate discharge once any pain relief is noted
Observe for symptom control and recurrence, treat persistent symptoms, and discharge when comfortable with return precautions
Admit everyone with a black widow bite to the ICU for 72 hours
Discharge with a prescription for prophylactic antivenom to self-administer at home
stages.explanation: After excluding other causes and controlling symptoms, latrodectism patients are observed for adequate symptom control and possible recurrence; symptoms can wax and wane over hours to a few days. When the patient is comfortable on oral analgesics with stable vitals, discharge with return precautions and analgesia is appropriate; severe or refractory cases warrant admission and consideration of antivenom. Immediate discharge at the first sign of relief (A) risks rebound symptoms. Routine 72-hour ICU admission for all (C) is excessive. Home self-administered antivenom (D) is unsafe and not a thing—antivenom is hospital-administered due to anaphylaxis risk. Counsel that residual aches can persist for days.

cases.diagnosis: Black widow spider (Latrodectus) envenomation — latrodectism mimicking an acute abdomen
pearls: Latrodectism causes severe diffuse muscle cramps and a rigid, board-like abdomen out of proportion to a trivial bite.
Always exclude true surgical/obstetric causes (check hCG) before attributing a rigid abdomen to envenomation.
First-line therapy is analgesia (opioids) plus benzodiazepines; antivenom is reserved for severe/refractory cases.
IV calcium is unreliable and not recommended; black widow bites are not locally necrotic (unlike brown recluse).

cases.title: Aching Joints After Surfacing
cases.chiefComplaint: Joint pain, tingling, and weakness developing within an hour after a scuba dive.
cases.history: A 37-year-old recreational diver presents about 45 minutes after surfacing from his second dive of the day. He reports the dives were deeper and longer than planned and that he ascended faster than usual after a buddy signaled low air; he skipped his safety stop. Within 30 minutes he developed a deep, boring ache in both shoulders and knees, then tingling and weakness in his legs and a band-like numbness around his trunk. He feels fatigued and has mild difficulty urinating. Past medical history: none significant. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. He is a nonsmoker, drinks socially, no recreational drugs. He has a patent diving certification and dives a few times a year. Family history non-contributory. He flew in two days ago but has no flights planned imminently. He denies chest pain but notes mild cough and shortness of breath. No recent illness. He used standard air (not nitrox) and dove in cold open water.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man rubbing his shoulders. Joints (shoulders, knees) are painful but without swelling, warmth, or erythema; pain not reproduced by palpation. Skin shows a mottled, marbled rash (cutis marmorata) over the trunk. Neuro: decreased strength (4/5) in both lower extremities, a sensory level around the lower thorax with diminished pinprick below it, and mildly brisk reflexes; gait unsteady. Bladder mildly distended. Lungs with scattered crackles; mild tachypnea. Cardiac regular. No focal cranial nerve deficit. The combination of joint pain plus a spinal-cord pattern of deficits is notable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Joint pain, cutis marmorata, and a spinal-cord sensory/motor level after a rapid ascent point to which diagnosis?
options: Septic arthritis with sepsis
Decompression sickness (DCS) with neurologic involvement
Acute ischemic stroke
Guillain-Barré syndrome
stages.explanation: The temporal link to a dive with rapid ascent and a skipped safety stop, plus the classic features—'the bends' (deep joint pain), cutis marmorata (skin marbling), and a spinal-cord syndrome (sensory level, leg weakness, bladder dysfunction)—is decompression sickness (Type II, neurologic). Inert gas (nitrogen) comes out of solution as bubbles in tissues and blood. Septic arthritis (A) shows hot, swollen joints and systemic infection, absent here. Stroke (B) would not produce a discrete spinal sensory level with bilateral leg involvement in this dive context. Guillain-Barré (C/D) is areflexic, ascending, and subacute over days—not minutes after diving. Recognizing DCS triggers time-critical treatment.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management while arranging definitive therapy?
options: Place the patient in steep head-down (Trendelenburg) position and give nitrous oxide for pain
Administer high-flow 100% oxygen, lay the patient supine, give IV isotonic fluids, and arrange transfer for hyperbaric therapy
Give oral acetazolamide and discharge with rest
Encourage immediate air travel to reach a specialist faster
stages.explanation: Initial management of DCS is high-flow 100% oxygen (which washes out nitrogen and shrinks bubbles), supine positioning, IV isotonic fluids for hydration, and urgent arrangement of recompression (hyperbaric oxygen) therapy. Steep Trendelenburg is no longer recommended and can raise intracranial pressure; nitrous oxide (A) is contraindicated because it diffuses into and enlarges bubbles. Acetazolamide with discharge (C) treats altitude illness, not DCS, and discharging is dangerous. Air travel/altitude (D) lowers ambient pressure and expands bubbles, worsening DCS—patients must avoid flying and ascent. Contact a diving medicine resource (e.g., DAN) to coordinate the nearest hyperbaric chamber.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Neuro exam: persistent lower-extremity weakness 4/5, sensory level at ~T10, urinary retention
SpO2 95% on room air, improves to 99% on 100% O2
Chest X-ray: no pneumothorax; mild interstitial markings
ECG: sinus rhythm
Bedside bladder scan: 450 mL retained urine
Diving Alert Network (DAN) consulted; nearest hyperbaric chamber identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for neurologic decompression sickness?
options: Oral corticosteroids and physical therapy
Recompression in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber (e.g., U.S. Navy Treatment Table 6)
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin
Surgical decompression of the spine
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for DCS, especially neurologic (Type II) disease, is recompression therapy in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber (commonly a U.S. Navy Treatment Table 6). Hyperbaric pressure shrinks and redissolves nitrogen bubbles while high-FiO2 accelerates inert-gas washout and improves tissue oxygenation; even delayed treatment can benefit, so referral should proceed regardless of time elapsed. Steroids and PT (A) are not definitive. Anticoagulation (C) is not standard therapy for DCS. Surgical spinal decompression (D) addresses mechanical compression, not gas bubbles, and is not indicated. While awaiting transfer, continue 100% oxygen and hydration, and manage urinary retention with catheterization.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which instruction is critical during transfer of this patient to a distant hyperbaric facility?
options: Transport by high-altitude aircraft to save time, regardless of cabin pressure
If air transport is required, maintain cabin altitude as low as possible (ideally near sea-level pressure) and continue 100% oxygen
Withhold oxygen during flight to conserve supply
Allow the patient to drink alcohol to relax during transfer
stages.explanation: Ascent to altitude lowers ambient pressure and expands nitrogen bubbles, worsening DCS, so if air transport is needed the cabin should be pressurized to as low an altitude as possible—ideally near sea-level pressure—or a low-altitude/ground route used, with continuous 100% oxygen throughout. High-altitude flight without cabin-pressure control (A) is dangerous. Withholding oxygen (C) removes the key bridging therapy that washes out nitrogen. Alcohol (D) promotes dehydration and impairs assessment. Continuing fluids, oxygen, and avoiding pressure changes preserves the patient until recompression. Coordinate with diving medicine and the receiving chamber on the safest transport profile.

cases.diagnosis: Neurologic (Type II) decompression sickness after rapid ascent
pearls: DCS combines 'the bends' (joint pain), cutis marmorata, and possible spinal-cord/neuro deficits soon after diving.
Immediate care: 100% oxygen, supine positioning, IV fluids—avoid nitrous oxide and Trendelenburg.
Definitive treatment is hyperbaric recompression (e.g., Navy Table 6); benefit can occur even with delay—always refer.
Avoid altitude/flying; if air transport is unavoidable, keep cabin pressure near sea level and continue oxygen.

cases.title: Short of Breath on the Mountain
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening shortness of breath and cough two days after rapidly ascending to high altitude.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to a mountain-town clinic at 3,500 m (11,500 ft) with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and a dry cough that is becoming productive of frothy, pink-tinged sputum. He flew in and drove up rapidly two days ago for a ski trip without acclimatization. Initially he had a mild headache and poor sleep; today he is breathless even at rest and cannot keep up with his group, with chest tightness and a sense of suffocation when lying flat. He is normally fit and runs marathons. No cardiac or pulmonary history. Medications: none; he took no acetazolamide prophylaxis. No known drug allergies. Nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no drugs. Family history non-contributory. He has been to altitude once before with similar but milder symptoms that resolved on descent. He denies fever, chills, or sick contacts. He notes his exercise tolerance has collapsed over 24 hours and that resting makes him only slightly better. He has not had chest pain radiating to the arm.
cases.exam: Anxious, tachypneic young man using accessory muscles. Lungs: bilateral crackles, more prominent at the right base, with scattered wheeze; no focal consolidation by percussion. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, a loud/accentuated P2 (pulmonic component) suggesting elevated pulmonary pressures; no murmur of valvular disease. No jugular venous distension at rest, no peripheral edema. Mild perioral cyanosis. Neuro: alert, oriented, no ataxia, normal mentation (no signs of cerebral edema). No fever; chest wall nontender. Capillary refill slightly delayed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Two days after rapid ascent, with severe hypoxemia, bilateral crackles, frothy pink sputum, and a loud P2, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Community-acquired pneumonia
High-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE)
Acute coronary syndrome
High-altitude cerebral edema (HACE)
stages.explanation: High-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE) typically appears 2–4 days after rapid ascent in unacclimatized individuals and presents with dyspnea (initially on exertion, then at rest), cough with frothy/pink sputum, marked hypoxemia, bilateral crackles, tachycardia, and signs of pulmonary hypertension (loud P2). It is a noncardiogenic edema from hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction. Pneumonia (A) usually has fever and focal findings, not this rapid altitude-linked bilateral picture. ACS (C) does not explain the diffuse crackles and frothy sputum in a fit 28-year-old without ischemic chest pain. HACE (D) is cerebral (ataxia, altered mentation), which he lacks. His prior milder episode reflects HAPE susceptibility.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important and definitive treatment for HAPE?
options: Immediate intubation
Descent to a lower altitude (plus supplemental oxygen)
IV furosemide to remove the edema fluid
Broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: Descent is the single most important and definitive treatment for HAPE; even a few hundred to ~1,000 meters often produces rapid improvement, ideally combined with supplemental oxygen. If descent is impossible, oxygen, a portable hyperbaric (Gamow) bag, and pharmacologic therapy bridge the patient. Intubation (A) is reserved for respiratory failure refractory to oxygen and is not first-line. Furosemide (C) is not recommended—HAPE is noncardiogenic and patients are often intravascularly dry; diuresis risks hypotension. Antibiotics (B/D) treat pneumonia, not HAPE, though the two can be hard to distinguish and antibiotics may be added if infection is suspected. Descend and oxygenate first.

stages.label: CLINIC DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
SpO2: 78% on room air → 92% on 4 L/min oxygen by nasal cannula
Lung ultrasound: diffuse bilateral B-lines (interstitial edema)
ECG: sinus tachycardia, right-axis findings; no ischemic ST changes
Temp 37.3 °C, no leukocytosis on point-of-care testing
After oxygen, work of breathing improves and he feels less suffocated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If descent must be delayed and oxygen is available, which medication is the preferred pharmacologic adjunct for HAPE?
options: Dexamethasone
Nifedipine (a pulmonary vasodilator that lowers pulmonary artery pressure)
Acetazolamide as primary treatment
Albuterol nebulizer alone
stages.explanation: Nifedipine is the preferred pharmacologic adjunct for HAPE: by lowering hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and pulmonary artery pressure, it reduces edema, useful when descent is delayed or as an adjunct to oxygen (phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors like tadalafil/sildenafil are alternatives). Dexamethasone (A) is the key drug for HACE and for AMS, not the primary HAPE treatment (though it may be added when HACE coexists). Acetazolamide (C) is used for AMS prevention/treatment and aids acclimatization, not as primary HAPE therapy. Albuterol alone (D) does not address the pulmonary hypertension driving HAPE. The mainstays remain descent and oxygen, with nifedipine as the adjunct.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After oxygen and nifedipine he improves. What counseling regarding future high-altitude travel is most appropriate?
options: He can reascend immediately the next day without precautions
Ascend gradually with acclimatization on future trips and consider prophylaxis (e.g., nifedipine) given his HAPE susceptibility
He should never travel above sea level again
Prophylactic furosemide before each ascent
stages.explanation: Patients with a HAPE history are susceptible to recurrence, so counseling focuses on gradual, staged ascent with proper acclimatization, avoiding rapid altitude gain and overexertion, and considering pharmacologic prophylaxis—nifedipine is used to prevent HAPE in susceptible individuals (acetazolamide helps with AMS/acclimatization). Immediate reascent (A) risks recurrence before recovery. A lifetime ban on altitude (C) is excessive and unnecessary with proper precautions. Prophylactic furosemide (D) is not indicated and risks dehydration. He should also know to descend promptly at the earliest recurrence of symptoms on future trips and to allow staged ascent above ~2,500–3,000 m.

cases.diagnosis: High-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE)
pearls: HAPE appears 2–4 days after rapid ascent: dyspnea, cough with frothy/pink sputum, severe hypoxemia, loud P2.
Descent (plus oxygen) is the definitive treatment; a Gamow bag bridges when descent is impossible.
Nifedipine lowers pulmonary artery pressure and is the preferred drug adjunct; furosemide is NOT recommended.
Dexamethasone treats HACE/AMS and acetazolamide aids acclimatization—neither is primary HAPE therapy.

cases.title: Third-Trimester Bleeding and Severe Abdominal Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding at 34 weeks of pregnancy.
cases.history: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with the abrupt onset of constant, severe lower abdominal pain that began 90 minutes ago, accompanied by dark vaginal bleeding. The pain is unrelenting rather than intermittent. She reports decreased fetal movement over the last hour. She denies trauma or recent intercourse, though she notes her blood pressure has been 'borderline high' at recent visits. Pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated; she has had no anatomy-scan abnormalities and the placenta was documented fundal and not low-lying. PMH: chronic hypertension, for which she takes labetalol 200 mg twice daily. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and has continued during pregnancy; she also reports occasional cocaine use, most recently two days ago. No alcohol. Allergies: none. Prior deliveries were vaginal and term. Family history is noncontributory. She is Rh-positive. She has had no prenatal care for the past three weeks because of insurance lapse.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic and in obvious distress. Abdomen reveals a gravid uterus that is firm, tense ('woody'), and exquisitely tender with no relaxation between contractions; fundal height is consistent with dates. There is dark blood at the introitus on inspection; a sterile speculum exam shows blood pooling from a closed cervical os. The fetal heart rate by bedside Doppler is 100 bpm with recurrent decelerations. No clots are expressed cervically. Lower extremities show no edema or calf tenderness. No external signs of trauma. Skin is cool and pale peripherally with delayed capillary refill of 3 seconds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate first step in evaluating this patient?
options: Perform a digital cervical exam to assess dilation and effacement
Place two large-bore IVs, begin resuscitation, and obtain continuous fetal and maternal monitoring
Send the patient for an immediate MRI of the pelvis
Administer terbutaline to stop the contractions before further workup
Discharge with outpatient follow-up after a single reassuring fetal heart tone
stages.explanation: Painful third-trimester bleeding with a rigid, tender uterus and a non-reassuring fetal heart rate signals placental abruption with possible hemodynamic compromise. The priority is simultaneous maternal resuscitation (two large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type-and-cross) and continuous electronic fetal and maternal monitoring while OB is mobilized. A digital cervical exam is contraindicated until placenta previa is excluded by ultrasound, because it can provoke catastrophic hemorrhage. MRI is too slow and inappropriate for an unstable obstetric emergency. Terbutaline (tocolysis) is contraindicated in significant abruption with fetal distress—it worsens maternal hemodynamics and delays delivery. Discharge is unsafe given the unstable fetus and mother.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL
Platelets 96 ×10³/µL
Fibrinogen 140 mg/dL
PT 16 s / INR 1.5
aPTT 42 s
Type & screen: O-positive
Bedside ultrasound: live fetus, FHR 98 bpm with decelerations; retroplacental hypoechoic collection consistent with abruption; placenta not over the os
Urine toxicology: positive for cocaine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most specifically supports the diagnosis of placental abruption over placenta previa?
options: Painless bright-red vaginal bleeding
Placenta covering the internal cervical os on ultrasound
Painful bleeding with a tense, tender uterus and a retroplacental collection
A reassuring fetal heart tracing with normal uterine tone
Bleeding provoked only by intercourse
stages.explanation: Abruption classically presents with painful, often dark bleeding, a tense and tender ('woody') uterus, and frequently a non-reassuring fetal tracing; a retroplacental hematoma on ultrasound is specific when seen. Painless bright-red bleeding describes placenta previa, not abruption. A placenta covering the os defines previa, the opposite diagnosis. A reassuring tracing with normal tone argues against significant abruption. Postcoital bleeding suggests previa or cervical pathology. Importantly, ultrasound is insensitive for abruption (many are clinically diagnosed), so a normal scan does not exclude it—but a visualized retroplacental clot strongly confirms it.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The labs return as above. What complication is developing and what is the best initial corrective measure?
options: Isolated thrombocytopenia; transfuse platelets only
Disseminated intravascular coagulation; replace fibrinogen with cryoprecipitate and give blood products guided by labs
Hemolytic anemia; start corticosteroids
Dilutional coagulopathy; restrict all fluids
Vitamin K deficiency; give oral vitamin K and observe
stages.explanation: The low fibrinogen (140), falling platelets, prolonged PT/aPTT, and clinical abruption indicate consumptive coagulopathy—DIC, a recognized complication of abruption from thromboplastin release. The cornerstone is treating the cause (delivery) plus product-guided resuscitation; fibrinogen <200 mg/dL in obstetric hemorrhage warrants cryoprecipitate (or fibrinogen concentrate), with FFP, platelets, and PRBCs as needed. Transfusing platelets alone ignores the critical fibrinogen deficit. Hemolytic anemia/steroids and vitamin K deficiency do not fit this consumptive picture. Fluid restriction is wrong in a hemorrhaging, hypovolemic patient. Definitive control requires delivery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given fetal heart rate 98 bpm with recurrent decelerations, maternal tachycardia, and DIC at 34 weeks, what is the definitive management?
options: Admit for expectant management with serial ultrasounds
Administer betamethasone and delay delivery 48 hours for fetal lung maturity
Proceed to emergent cesarean delivery
Begin magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection and continue monitoring only
Trial of oxytocin induction over the next 24 hours
stages.explanation: With a non-reassuring fetal tracing, maternal hemodynamic instability, and evolving DIC, this is a severe abruption requiring emergent delivery—typically cesarean given fetal compromise. Expectant management is reserved for stable mother, reassuring fetus, and preterm gestation, not this scenario. Delaying 48 hours for betamethasone is unsafe when mother and fetus are deteriorating now; antenatal steroids are not a reason to delay emergent delivery. Magnesium neuroprotection may be given concurrently but is not definitive and cannot replace delivery. A prolonged oxytocin induction is far too slow for acute fetal distress. Stabilize, transfuse, and deliver without delay.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After delivery, the uterus is boggy and bleeding continues. Aside from massive transfusion support, which modifiable risk factor most directly contributed to this abruption and should be addressed?
options: Rh incompatibility
Gestational diabetes
Cocaine use and uncontrolled hypertension
Advanced maternal age over 35
Multiparity alone
stages.explanation: Cocaine causes vasoconstriction and acute hypertensive surges that precipitate abruption; combined with her chronic and poorly controlled hypertension and smoking, these are the dominant modifiable contributors and warrant counseling, cessation support, and BP optimization. She is Rh-positive, so Rh incompatibility is not relevant. Gestational diabetes is not a major abruption risk and was not present. Her age (32) is below the typical advanced-age threshold. Multiparity is a weak, non-modifiable association and is not the driving factor here. Addressing substance use and hypertension reduces recurrence risk in future pregnancies.

cases.diagnosis: Placental abruption with disseminated intravascular coagulation and fetal distress
pearls: Painful third-trimester bleeding with a tense, tender uterus is abruption until proven otherwise; avoid digital cervical exam until previa is excluded.
Ultrasound is insensitive for abruption—a normal scan does not rule it out; diagnosis is largely clinical.
Cocaine, smoking, hypertension, prior abruption, and trauma are the key risk factors.
Abruption can trigger DIC; check fibrinogen and replace with cryoprecipitate, and proceed to emergent delivery when mother or fetus is unstable.

cases.title: Severe Headache and High Blood Pressure in Late Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent pounding headache and visual spots at 34 weeks of pregnancy.
cases.history: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with a severe, throbbing frontal headache for the past 12 hours that has not responded to acetaminophen, along with intermittent 'flashing spots' in her vision and right upper-quadrant discomfort. She also reports new swelling of her hands and face over several days. She denies vaginal bleeding, leakage of fluid, or contractions; fetal movement is present. Prenatal course was unremarkable until her last visit two weeks ago, when blood pressure was noted to be mildly elevated and trace protein was found on dipstick; no follow-up was arranged. PMH: none. Medications: prenatal vitamins only. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. Allergies: none. Family history: her mother had 'toxemia' during pregnancy. She works as a teacher. She has had no seizures. She describes the headache as the worst she has ever experienced and unlike any prior headache.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and photophobic. Fundoscopic exam is without papilledema. There is brisk, symmetric 3+ deep tendon reflexes with 2 beats of clonus at the ankles. The abdomen is gravid with mild right-upper-quadrant and epigastric tenderness; fundal height is appropriate; fetal heart tones are 140 bpm and reactive by Doppler. There is notable pitting edema of the hands and periorbital region. No focal neurologic deficits, no neck stiffness, and no rash. Lungs are clear without crackles. A repeat BP 15 minutes later is 168/110 mmHg.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination best establishes the diagnosis of preeclampsia with severe features?
options: Mild hypertension with trace proteinuria and no symptoms
Blood pressure ≥160/110 plus severe persistent headache, visual changes, and RUQ pain after 20 weeks
Isolated lower-extremity edema in the third trimester
New hypertension before 20 weeks of gestation
Proteinuria alone with normal blood pressure
stages.explanation: Preeclampsia is new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction; severe features include systolic ≥160 or diastolic ≥110, plus cerebral/visual symptoms, RUQ/epigastric pain, pulmonary edema, thrombocytopenia, renal or hepatic dysfunction. This patient meets severe criteria by BP and symptoms. Mild hypertension with trace protein and no symptoms is not 'severe.' Isolated edema is common in normal pregnancy and is nonspecific. Hypertension before 20 weeks suggests chronic hypertension, not preeclampsia. Proteinuria alone without hypertension does not establish preeclampsia. Note severe features can be diagnosed even without proteinuria when end-organ involvement is present.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL
AST 142 U/L
ALT 130 U/L
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Uric acid 7.8 mg/dL
LDH 320 U/L
Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.9
Peripheral smear: occasional schistocytes
Fetal monitoring: reactive, no decelerations

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The labs show platelets 88, AST/ALT elevated, and schistocytes. What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
options: Start oral nifedipine and discharge with close follow-up
Administer IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and treat the severe-range blood pressure
Transfuse platelets immediately to correct thrombocytopenia
Give high-dose corticosteroids as definitive treatment for HELLP and observe
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for suspected DIC
stages.explanation: She has preeclampsia with severe features and evolving HELLP (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets). The two urgent priorities are IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (eclampsia prevention) and prompt control of severe-range BP with IV labetalol, hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine. Discharge is unsafe. Platelet transfusion is reserved for active bleeding or very low counts before procedures, not routine thrombocytopenia at 88. Corticosteroids do not definitively treat HELLP (benefit on maternal outcomes is unproven) and are not a substitute for delivery. There is no indication for anticoagulation; fibrinogen-guided support is used only if DIC develops.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her BP remains 168/110. Which is the most appropriate first-line agent and goal for acute severe hypertension in pregnancy?
options: IV nitroprusside to rapidly normalize BP to <120/80
IV labetalol titrated to a target of roughly 140–150/90–100 mmHg
Oral ACE inhibitor to reduce proteinuria
Sublingual nifedipine to drop BP as fast as possible
No treatment unless diastolic exceeds 120 mmHg
stages.explanation: First-line acute agents are IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine, titrated to a goal around 140–150/90–100 to avoid compromising uteroplacental perfusion—not full normalization. Nitroprusside risks cyanide/thiocyanate fetal toxicity and is avoided except in refractory crises. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are teratogenic/fetotoxic and contraindicated in pregnancy. 'Sublingual' rapid-acting nifedipine causes dangerous precipitous drops and is not recommended; immediate-release oral nifedipine is acceptable. Waiting until diastolic >120 is unsafe—treatment is indicated at ≥160 systolic or ≥110 diastolic sustained.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While being stabilized she has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. After protecting the airway and positioning her, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Load with IV phenytoin as the first-line agent
Administer (or rebolus) IV magnesium sulfate and prepare for expedited delivery
Give IV lorazepam and discharge once the seizure stops
Start a continuous propofol infusion and delay delivery
Withhold magnesium and give only additional antihypertensives
stages.explanation: A generalized seizure in this setting is eclampsia, and magnesium sulfate is both first-line prophylaxis and treatment—give a loading dose or an additional 2 g bolus if she is already on an infusion, then continue maintenance, while preparing for expedited delivery (the definitive treatment). Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium for eclamptic seizures and is not first-line. A benzodiazepine may abort an active convulsion but does not replace magnesium, and discharge is dangerous. Propofol infusion with delayed delivery is inappropriate—delivery should not be deferred after eclampsia. Withholding magnesium and giving only antihypertensives fails to address seizure control; BP control and magnesium are complementary, not interchangeable.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: At 34 weeks with preeclampsia with severe features and HELLP, after stabilization, what is the definitive management plan?
options: Discharge home once BP is controlled and recheck labs in one week
Expectant management until 39 weeks regardless of features
Admit, give antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, and proceed to delivery (timing not delayed by maternal/fetal deterioration)
Initiate long-term outpatient magnesium therapy
Perform immediate cesarean under general anesthesia in all cases
stages.explanation: The only cure for preeclampsia is delivery. With severe features/HELLP, delivery is indicated; at 34 weeks a brief course of betamethasone for fetal lung maturity is reasonable if mother and fetus remain stable, but delivery should not be delayed if either deteriorates. Discharge is unsafe with severe features and HELLP. Expectant management to 39 weeks is appropriate only for preeclampsia without severe features, not this case. Outpatient magnesium is not a therapy. Mode of delivery depends on obstetric factors and cervical favorability—vaginal delivery is often attempted; routine general-anesthesia cesarean for all is incorrect and regional anesthesia is generally preferred if platelets permit.

cases.diagnosis: Preeclampsia with severe features progressing to HELLP syndrome
pearls: Severe features = BP ≥160/110, cerebral/visual symptoms, RUQ pain, pulmonary edema, platelets <100k, doubling transaminases, or creatinine >1.1.
Magnesium sulfate is for seizure prophylaxis (and treatment); it is NOT an antihypertensive—control severe BP with labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine.
Target BP ~140–150/90–100; avoid ACE inhibitors/ARBs and nitroprusside in pregnancy.
Delivery is the only cure—preeclampsia with severe features mandates delivery, with steroids for lung maturity if briefly stable before term.

cases.title: Imminent Delivery with a Stuck Newborn
cases.chiefComplaint: Term pregnant woman with the baby's head crowning on arrival.
cases.history: A 24-year-old G3P2 woman at an estimated 40 weeks gestation is brought in by EMS with strong urge to push; she did not make it to her planned hospital. Contractions are 2 minutes apart and she reports the baby is 'coming.' Her two prior deliveries were vaginal; she recalls one baby was 'over 9 pounds' and labor was difficult. She has had limited prenatal care and a recent ultrasound estimated fetal weight at approximately 4,300 g. PMH: gestational diabetes managed with diet this pregnancy, A1c slightly elevated. Medications: prenatal vitamins. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. Allergies: none. Membranes ruptured 30 minutes ago with clear fluid. On the gurney the fetal head is crowning. There is no OB or anesthesia immediately at bedside; they have been paged. The patient is cooperative and able to follow commands. She has no fever and no bleeding beyond bloody show.
cases.exam: The fetal head delivers spontaneously but then retracts tightly back against the perineum (the 'turtle sign'), and external rotation/restitution fails to occur. There is no delivery of the anterior shoulder with the next maternal push and routine gentle downward traction. The maternal perineum is intact without significant laceration yet. The newborn's head is cyanotic at the face. Fundal pressure has not been applied. The umbilical cord is not visible or prolapsed. The mother remains hemodynamically stable. The clock is started to track the head-to-body delivery interval.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The fetal head delivers then retracts (turtle sign) and the shoulder will not deliver. What is the diagnosis?
options: Umbilical cord prolapse
Shoulder dystocia
Uterine rupture
Placental abruption
Breech presentation
stages.explanation: The turtle sign—delivery of the head followed by retraction against the perineum with failure of the anterior shoulder to deliver and failed restitution—is the hallmark of shoulder dystocia, an obstetric emergency from impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the pubic symphysis. Cord prolapse presents with a palpable/visible cord and fetal bradycardia, not the turtle sign. Uterine rupture causes sudden abdominal pain, loss of station, and maternal instability. Abruption causes painful bleeding with a rigid uterus before delivery. Breech presentation would show buttocks or feet presenting, not a delivered head. Recognizing dystocia immediately triggers a stepwise maneuver sequence.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the correct FIRST maneuver to relieve the shoulder dystocia?
options: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby out
McRoberts maneuver (hyperflex maternal hips) with suprapubic pressure
Immediately perform a cesarean section
Aggressively pull the fetal head with strong lateral traction
Cut a fourth-degree episiotomy first
stages.explanation: First-line management is the McRoberts maneuver—sharp hyperflexion of the maternal hips onto the abdomen—combined with suprapubic (not fundal) pressure to dislodge the anterior shoulder; this resolves most dystocias and is low-risk. Fundal pressure is contraindicated: it worsens impaction and risks uterine rupture. Cesarean is not feasible with a delivered head and is reserved for failure of all maneuvers (and is far slower). Excessive lateral traction risks brachial plexus injury (Erb palsy). Episiotomy alone does not relieve a bony impaction and is not the first step, though it may create room for internal maneuvers if needed.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: McRoberts with suprapubic pressure fails after 30–60 seconds. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Repeat McRoberts indefinitely until it works
Apply fundal pressure with two hands
Proceed to internal rotational maneuvers (e.g., Rubin or Woods screw) and/or delivery of the posterior arm
Push the head back in and prepare for cesarean (Zavanelli) as the immediate second step
Administer terbutaline to relax the impacted shoulder
stages.explanation: When McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure fails, escalate to internal maneuvers: rotational maneuvers (Rubin II, Woods corkscrew) to bring the shoulders into the oblique diameter, and delivery of the posterior arm, which is highly effective. Repeating McRoberts indefinitely wastes critical time. Fundal pressure is contraindicated. The Zavanelli maneuver (cephalic replacement then cesarean) is a true last resort, not a routine second step. Terbutaline relaxes the uterus and may help with certain entrapments but does not relieve bony shoulder impaction and is not the appropriate next maneuver here.

stages.label: DELIVERY OUTCOME
stages.content:
Posterior arm delivered; anterior shoulder released at 2 minutes head-to-body interval
Live male infant, birth weight 4,350 g
Apgar 4 at 1 minute, 8 at 5 minutes after stimulation and brief positive-pressure ventilation
Right arm held in extension with absent Moro reflex on the right
No maternal heavy bleeding initially; perineal second-degree laceration

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The newborn holds the right arm extended and adducted with absent Moro on that side. Which complication occurred and what is the typical course?
options: Clavicle fracture that always requires surgical fixation
Erb-Duchenne palsy (C5–C6 brachial plexus injury), most of which resolve with conservative management
Permanent complete arm paralysis requiring immediate amputation evaluation
Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy diagnosed by the arm posture
Klumpke palsy affecting the intrinsic hand muscles
stages.explanation: The 'waiter's tip' posture (arm extended, adducted, internally rotated) with absent ipsilateral Moro reflects Erb-Duchenne palsy from C5–C6 brachial plexus stretch—a known shoulder-dystocia complication; the majority recover with physical therapy over weeks to months. A clavicle fracture is also common but does not by itself cause this neurologic posture, and most clavicle fractures heal without surgery. Permanent total paralysis/amputation is not the expected course. HIE is diagnosed by neuro exam and may follow prolonged hypoxia but is not indicated by arm posture alone. Klumpke palsy (C8–T1) causes a 'claw hand' with intrinsic muscle weakness, not this proximal pattern.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which maternal risk factor most strongly predisposed to this shoulder dystocia and should be documented?
options: Maternal age of 24
Fetal macrosomia in the setting of gestational diabetes and a prior large infant
Group B Streptococcus colonization
Rh-negative blood type
First pregnancy (nulliparity)
stages.explanation: Fetal macrosomia—driven here by gestational diabetes and supported by a prior large infant—is the dominant risk factor for shoulder dystocia; diabetic fetuses also have disproportionate shoulder/trunk growth. Maternal age of 24 is unremarkable. GBS colonization affects neonatal sepsis risk, not dystocia mechanics. Rh status is unrelated to dystocia. She is multiparous (G3P2), not nulliparous, and a prior macrosomic delivery is itself a risk factor. That said, shoulder dystocia is unpredictable and can occur without risk factors, so providers must always be prepared with the maneuver sequence.

cases.diagnosis: Shoulder dystocia during precipitous delivery, complicated by Erb-Duchenne palsy
pearls: Turtle sign = shoulder dystocia; call for help, note the time, and avoid fundal pressure.
First-line: McRoberts (hip hyperflexion) plus suprapubic pressure; escalate to internal rotation and delivery of the posterior arm.
Excessive lateral head traction and fundal pressure cause brachial plexus injury and uterine rupture—avoid both.
Macrosomia (diabetes, prior large infant, post-dates) raises risk, but dystocia is unpredictable—always be ready with the maneuver sequence.

cases.title: Sudden Sharp Pelvic Pain with Nausea
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt severe right-sided pelvic pain with vomiting.
cases.history: A 23-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset, severe, sharp right lower-quadrant and pelvic pain that began abruptly two hours ago while she was at the gym. The pain is intermittent and colicky, radiating to the right flank and groin, associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. She has had similar but milder, self-resolving episodes over the past month. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and was normal; cycles are regular. She is sexually active with one partner and uses combined oral contraceptives. She denies vaginal discharge, dysuria, fever, or vaginal bleeding. PMH: a known 5-cm right ovarian cyst noted incidentally on a prior scan. No prior surgeries. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. No tobacco; social alcohol. Family history noncontributory. A urine pregnancy test in triage is negative. She rates the pain 9/10 and cannot find a comfortable position.
cases.exam: The patient is restless and uncomfortable, frequently shifting on the gurney. Abdomen is soft with marked tenderness in the right lower quadrant and a palpable, tender fullness; there is voluntary guarding but no rigidity or rebound. Bowel sounds are present. Pelvic exam reveals right adnexal tenderness with a palpable tender adnexal mass; the cervix appears normal with no discharge, and there is no cervical motion tenderness on careful exam. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness on the left and minimal on the right. Bimanual exam is limited by pain. No vaginal bleeding is seen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic study in this young woman with acute adnexal pain and a known cyst?
options: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
Pelvic (transvaginal) ultrasound with Doppler flow
MRI of the pelvis
Diagnostic laparoscopy without imaging
Plain abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound with Doppler is the first-line imaging for suspected ovarian torsion: it assesses ovarian size, the cyst, and arterial/venous flow, and can show the 'whirlpool sign' of a twisted vascular pedicle. CT may incidentally show an enlarged ovary but is less specific and exposes a young woman to radiation; it is not first-line. MRI is more time-consuming and reserved for equivocal cases. Going straight to laparoscopy bypasses readily available imaging, though surgery should not be delayed if suspicion is very high. Plain films have no role in evaluating adnexal pathology.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
β-hCG: negative
Urinalysis: unremarkable, no blood, no leukocyte esterase
Transvaginal ultrasound: enlarged right ovary (7 cm) with peripherally displaced follicles and a 5-cm cyst; coiled vascular pedicle ('whirlpool sign'); arterial and venous Doppler flow present but diminished
Left ovary normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Doppler shows preserved but diminished flow and a whirlpool sign. How should this finding be interpreted?
options: Normal Doppler flow reliably excludes ovarian torsion
Torsion is excluded because the ovary is not enlarged enough
Ovarian torsion remains likely; normal or present Doppler flow does NOT rule it out
The whirlpool sign indicates appendicitis, not torsion
Diminished flow indicates a hemorrhagic cyst rather than torsion
stages.explanation: Ovarian torsion can have present or even normal Doppler flow because of dual ovarian blood supply and intermittent or early/partial torsion—so flow on Doppler does NOT exclude torsion. Here the enlarged ovary, peripherally displaced follicles, and especially the whirlpool sign (twisted pedicle) strongly support torsion despite preserved flow. The diagnosis is clinical-plus-imaging, and a normal Doppler should never falsely reassure. The ovary is markedly enlarged at 7 cm. The whirlpool sign is specific for a twisted pedicle (torsion), not appendicitis. A simple hemorrhagic cyst would not produce a coiled pedicle.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the clinical and ultrasound findings, what is the definitive management?
options: Outpatient analgesics and repeat ultrasound in one week
Emergent gynecologic surgery for diagnostic laparoscopy and detorsion
IV antibiotics for presumed tubo-ovarian abscess
Oral contraceptive adjustment and pain control only
Admit for observation without surgical consultation
stages.explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency; the definitive treatment is emergent laparoscopy with detorsion to restore perfusion and salvage the ovary, often with cystectomy. Delays risk ovarian necrosis and loss of fertility. Outpatient analgesia and delayed imaging are unsafe with high suspicion. Antibiotics treat infection (TOA/PID), which this is not—there is no fever, discharge, or cervical motion tenderness. Contraceptive adjustment does not address an acute twist. Observation without surgical consultation risks irreversible ischemia. Even an appearing-necrotic ovary is frequently detorsed first, as viability is often better than it looks.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most important reason to pursue prompt surgical detorsion rather than watchful waiting?
options: To prevent conversion of the cyst into a malignancy
To prevent ovarian ischemia, necrosis, and loss of ovarian function/fertility
To avoid spread of infection to the contralateral ovary
To prevent the cyst from rupturing into the bladder
Because torsion always causes life-threatening hemorrhage
stages.explanation: The twisted pedicle compromises venous then arterial flow, producing progressive ovarian ischemia and necrosis; prompt detorsion preserves ovarian viability and future fertility, which is the central goal. Torsion does not transform a benign cyst into cancer. There is no infection to spread to the other ovary. Rupture into the bladder is not a recognized consequence. While torsion can rarely involve significant bleeding, the dominant and time-sensitive threat is ischemic loss of the ovary, not hemorrhagic shock—making rapid restoration of perfusion the priority.

cases.diagnosis: Ovarian torsion of the right ovary
pearls: Sudden severe unilateral adnexal pain with nausea/vomiting and an enlarged ovary (often with a cyst >5 cm) suggests torsion.
Transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler is first-line; the whirlpool sign indicates a twisted pedicle.
Normal or present Doppler flow does NOT exclude torsion—do not be falsely reassured.
Torsion is a surgical emergency: emergent laparoscopic detorsion preserves the ovary; even ischemic-appearing ovaries are usually detorsed rather than removed.

cases.title: Lower Abdominal Pain and Fever in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of worsening lower abdominal pain with fever and discharge.
cases.history: A 21-year-old woman presents with three days of progressively worsening bilateral lower abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, and a new malodorous vaginal discharge. She also notes deep dyspareunia and abnormal intermenstrual bleeding. Her last menstrual period ended one week ago. She is sexually active with two new partners in the past three months and reports inconsistent condom use; she is not on contraception. She denies prior STIs. She has had nausea but no vomiting and no urinary symptoms. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: penicillin causes hives. No tobacco; occasional alcohol. Family history noncontributory. A urine pregnancy test is negative. She has been able to tolerate oral intake but the pain has intensified today, prompting her visit. She has no IUD. She reports no recent travel and no sick contacts.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable. Abdomen is soft with bilateral lower-quadrant tenderness and mild voluntary guarding, more on the right, without rebound. Pelvic exam reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge, a friable cervix that bleeds with the swab, and marked cervical motion tenderness with bilateral adnexal tenderness. No discrete adnexal mass is clearly palpable, though the right adnexa is fuller and tender. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no rash or joint swelling. Murphy sign is negative. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, which finding is the minimum clinical criterion supporting empiric treatment for pelvic inflammatory disease?
options: A positive pregnancy test
Cervical motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness in an at-risk woman with pelvic pain
Costovertebral angle tenderness
Right-upper-quadrant pain with a positive Murphy sign
Isolated dysuria with pyuria
stages.explanation: PID is treated empirically when a sexually active woman with pelvic/lower-abdominal pain has cervical motion tenderness, uterine tenderness, OR adnexal tenderness, with no other explanation—a deliberately low threshold given the consequences of missed treatment (infertility, ectopic, chronic pain). A positive pregnancy test would actually shift concern toward ectopic and complicate the picture. CVA tenderness suggests pyelonephritis. RUQ pain with positive Murphy sign suggests cholecystitis (or Fitz-Hugh-Curtis perihepatitis as a PID complication, but that is not the minimum criterion). Isolated dysuria with pyuria suggests cystitis. Her CMT and adnexal tenderness meet criteria.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
β-hCG: negative
NAAT: Chlamydia trachomatis positive, Neisseria gonorrhoeae positive
Wet mount: numerous WBCs; HIV test pending
Transvaginal ultrasound: right adnexal complex multiloculated fluid collection 4.5 cm with thick walls, consistent with tubo-ovarian abscess
CRP elevated

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The ultrasound now reveals a 4.5-cm tubo-ovarian abscess. How does this change disposition compared with uncomplicated PID?
options: No change; treat as outpatient with oral antibiotics
It warrants inpatient admission for IV antibiotics and observation
It requires immediate hysterectomy
It can be ignored if the patient feels well
It mandates same-day discharge with topical therapy
stages.explanation: A tubo-ovarian abscess is a complication of PID that meets criteria for inpatient admission and IV antibiotics with close monitoring; image-guided drainage or surgery is considered for large abscesses (often >7 cm), rupture, or failure to improve in 48–72 hours. Uncomplicated PID in a reliable, stable patient may be treated outpatient, but a TOA is different. Hysterectomy is not first-line—fertility-sparing antibiotic therapy ± drainage is preferred. The abscess cannot be ignored; untreated TOA can rupture and cause sepsis. Same-day discharge with topical therapy is inappropriate for an intra-abdominal abscess requiring systemic IV therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is admitted with a TOA and reports a penicillin allergy (hives). Which inpatient regimen is appropriate, and what must be added for TOA coverage?
options: Oral azithromycin alone
IV cefoxitin (or cefotetan) plus doxycycline, with metronidazole added for anaerobic abscess coverage
IV vancomycin alone
Oral metronidazole only
No antibiotics; drainage alone is sufficient
stages.explanation: Inpatient PID is treated with IV cefoxitin or cefotetan plus doxycycline; for a tubo-ovarian abscess, metronidazole (or clindamycin-based regimen) is added for enhanced anaerobic coverage. A hives reaction to penicillin is not a contraindication to cephalosporins in most cases (low cross-reactivity), and cephalosporins remain standard; if true severe allergy, clindamycin plus gentamicin is an alternative. Azithromycin alone or oral metronidazole alone does not provide adequate broad polymicrobial coverage. Vancomycin alone misses gram-negatives and anaerobes. Drainage without antibiotics is inadequate—systemic therapy is essential, with drainage as adjunct for large or refractory abscesses.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before discharge, which additional management step is most important for this patient and her contacts?
options: Recommend lifelong avoidance of all sexual activity
Test and empirically treat sexual partners and screen the patient for other STIs including HIV
Schedule an immediate hysterectomy regardless of response
Prescribe oral contraceptives to prevent reinfection
Reassure her that no follow-up is needed once she feels better
stages.explanation: PID from chlamydia and gonorrhea requires partner notification, testing, and empiric treatment (expedited partner therapy where permitted) to prevent reinfection, plus screening the patient for coexisting STIs including HIV and syphilis. Lifelong abstinence is neither realistic nor the medical recommendation—counsel on condoms and risk reduction. Hysterectomy is not indicated if she responds to therapy. Oral contraceptives prevent pregnancy but do not prevent STIs and are not the priority intervention here. 'No follow-up needed' is wrong—clinical reassessment within 48–72 hours and a test of cure/retesting for reinfection are recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Pelvic inflammatory disease complicated by tubo-ovarian abscess
pearls: Treat PID empirically with a low threshold: cervical motion, uterine, OR adnexal tenderness in an at-risk woman is enough.
Tubo-ovarian abscess warrants admission and IV antibiotics; add anaerobic coverage (metronidazole/clindamycin) and consider drainage for large or refractory abscesses.
A penicillin hives history rarely precludes cephalosporins; clindamycin plus gentamicin is the alternative for severe allergy.
Always treat partners, screen for coexisting STIs (HIV, syphilis), and arrange 48–72 hour follow-up to limit infertility and recurrence.

cases.title: Heavy Vaginal Bleeding After Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: Soaking through pads with heavy bleeding shortly after a precipitous home delivery.
cases.history: A 30-year-old G4P4 woman is brought in by EMS 45 minutes after an unplanned home delivery of a term infant. The placenta delivered spontaneously, but she has had continuous heavy vaginal bleeding since, soaking multiple towels. She feels lightheaded and weak.
